Rawr! Means I Love You in Dinosaur

by AgathaLovesRio

Summary

Rio Vidal (death) adored dinosaurs and was devastated when they were wiped out.
In modern day, Rio gets so annoyed with researchers misrepresenting/falsely describing dinosaurs that she becomes a paleontologist just to refute their theories. The main researcher with whom she comes into conflict with?

Dr. Agatha Harkness.

Notes

Warnings: SMUT.

Chapter 1: Everybody Do The Dinosaur

In modern day, Rio gets so annoyed with researchers misrepresenting/falsely describing dinosaurs that she becomes a paleontologist just to refute their theories. The main researcher with whom she comes into conflict with?

Dr. Agatha Harkness.

Chapter edited on 1/17/25

Death looked around at the lush vegetation on the giant continent and watched the wildlife frolic, bellowing, grunting, and posturing as they communicated. They would be called dinosaurs in the future when humans discovered their bones, but for now, she simply referred to them as animals and, most importantly, her friends. 

Walking among them for a hundred and sixty-five million years, which, for the cosmic entity of Death, felt like nothing. She would change her height and throw a tree branch out for a Tyrannosaurus in the later years, take care of nests of eggs if the mother fell victim to the circle of life, or simply scratch a brontosaurus under the chin. 

It was a peaceful time for Death, not nearly as much work. She still had a job to do, of course, but animals never questioned death the way humans would, once she was introduced to that infuriating species. No, animals simply accepted dying as a fact of life. Following her to the afterlife and looking into her kind eyes with an understanding that most humans would never have. 

It had been her favorite time period, and she mourned her friends when the meteor hit, along with other natural phenomena. Yes, the world had to progress, and some of the creatures survived or evolved, but humans were not nearly as friendly as dinosaurs. Homosapiens were violent, cruel, nasty little things. They bickered, started wars rather than communicate, fought over everything, and rarely thought logically. They often got their historical facts wrong, were very confused about theology, didn’t understand science at all, and would be doing so much better if they would just set their egos aside.

No, Death didn’t care much for humans and grew very frustrated with taking them to the other side. 

The wars absolutely disgusted her, and she detested taking children. Children were innocent and often caught up in the crossfire of adults’ bad decisions. Disease and illness were hard, and while she hated it, it was part of life. Guiding a soul due to another person’s lack of impulse control was high on Rio’s list of ultimate human sins. 

The entity saw many evils over the years, yet found herself walking amongst them from time to time in a human form she had created, with a name she thought was clever, given her true identity.

Rio Vidal.

River of Life.

Once humans began finding the fossils of her prehistoric friends, she became not only intrigued but emotionally invested.   

Rio once tried to tell the humans about the ancient reptiles, appearing as a man to play to their rather patriarchal ways. They thought her insane, having taken the wrong approach. She watched from afar again and became infuriated with how wrong they were about everything.

Determined not to let her favorite part of history be remembered this way, the favorite members of the timeline done so wrongly. After watching so many mistruths being told, and those idiot men get so many things wrong, it was time for Death to join humanity and take a hiatus.

She ordered her millions of reapers to take over collections and appointed various gods of other beliefs to be in command. 

Death became Rio Vidal full-time. 

It was easy for her to magick up official papers and find a position on an archeological team in the late 1800s. Unofficially, she had been involved for centuries, pushing man in the right direction. Dr. Vidal aged herself as necessary, disappeared, and reappeared as her own daughter, creating a generational legacy. Planting memories in the minds of her “mother’s” younger colleagues as needed. 

Humans were highly suggestible. 

Eventually, she had to enter an actual university to earn an official degree. One she would update if deemed necessary, or simply alter the minds of professors so they remembered having her as a student. Changing the dates on the degrees and transcripts as necessary. 

Magick was a wonderful thing to have.

She found that she enjoyed working on these sites, finding fossils, ancient artifacts, and ‘theorizing’ with the scientists. Preferring to work with her hands and the available tools over her supernatural abilities. She reveled in sharing these somewhat gentle creatures with the world. 

Mankind kept getting things wrong, however, and she found herself moving into laboratory research in the late 1980s. She had published multiple articles, written books about her research, and became well-respected in her field.

It had been simple, though slow, infuriating, and arduous. Finally, Lady Death was making some progress.

Until she met Dr. Agatha Harkness.

🦕🦖🦕

July 2009, London, England

The first time Agatha Harkness met Dr. Rio Vidal was at a seminar in London. She had read the woman’s work. Of course, she had. Any paleontologist worth their fossils had read her research. 

Hell, Agatha had read the work of all the women from whom Rio had supposedly descended. 

Personally, she wasn’t impressed with any of it. In her opinion, every Vidal thought they knew more than the paleontological community at large. So what if their theories and research were correct? 

Every. Damn. Time. 

Or if that blasted family had accurately classified every single fucking species first. 

Agatha still wasn’t completely sold on the idea of some species having no scales at all.

The woman had not been prepared to be captivated by Rio. No, she wanted to hate her. They were the two most prestigious scientists in their field. 

It was truly criminal that they hadn’t met until now, a decade into their careers. Yet, here they were, both presenting at the most influential symposium and convention for all things related to paleontology. They were presenting classes on opposing views and were on a panel together. 

These two dinosaur rockstars were finally meeting each other. 

Agatha did not have high hopes that it would go well.

Dr. Harkness stood in the ballroom of the hotel hosting the conference, speaking with Dr. Ian Malcolm, a professor and longtime friend from Yale, when she caught a glimpse of the enemy from the corner of her eye. 

Rio Vidal wore a simple black dress, her dark hair loose around her shoulders, black heels clicking against the marble floor. 

A fucking goddess if she ever saw one.

Their eyes met, both with neutral expressions. They knew exactly who the other was, and they weren’t giving anything away.

“For two people who have never met, there’s a lot of animosity there,” Dr. Malcolm mused.

“Yes, well, she’s an arrogant nepo baby.” Agatha took a sip of her wine, and her eyes followed Rio.

“Have you looked in a mirror?”

“I’m not a science nepo baby. Now, if you’ll excuse me, Ian. I have to go take someone else’s ego down a few notches while elevating mine.” She knocked her drink back, setting the glass on a nearby empty table.

 

Rio had been looking forward to this moment when she would finally meet the stubborn, hard-headed woman by the name of Agatha Harkness. Continuing to speak with an attendee, she ignored Dr. Harkness’s approach.

“Dr. Vidal.”

Rio smiled at the man she was speaking with, who rightly took the hint as he glanced nervously between the two powerhouse scientists and left.

Rio turned her full attention to the woman with the glittering blue eyes and the brunette hair pulled up in a simple bun. Her eyes flicked over her twice, taking in the tailored black suit, the purple heels, and the white shirt, which was unbuttoned just enough to be enticing.

Death was suddenly very parched and reached for her water before she greeted the other scientist.

Rio was not used to having such an instant attraction to mortals. 

“Dr. Harkness, how wonderful to finally meet you.” Rio set her water back down and held out her dry hand, the other wet from the condensation on the glass.

“Pleasure’s all yours, I’m sure,” Agatha smirked, having noticed the reaction Rio had. One she took personal glee in. 

She gripped Rio’s hand, smirk still in place, and shook it firmly. “A shame it’s taken us this long to meet.”

Rio retracted her hand, her own lips quirked on one side. “Truly. I was pleasantly surprised to see that we’re on a panel together.”

“I look forward to refuting some of your theories.”

“I didn’t realize you were here to continue to provide incorrect information.” Rio raised one eyebrow.

“Oh, all of my research is correct, sweetcheeks.” Agatha stepped further into Rio’s space.

Rio scoffed, stepping closer to Agatha. “Tell me, Harkness, have you ever been right?”

“Only every day of my life.” Her eyes darted down to Rio’s mouth. Never had she felt an instant attraction like this, especially with someone whom she disagreed with on most fronts. However, Rio was passionate about her field, and damnit, she was sexier than Agatha had ever imagined. 

“Are those the days that end in ‘q’? Because, as far as I see it, when it comes to our field, you are misguided in a few matters.” Rio realized they were drifting to the entrance, as though a magnet were leading them upstairs. Rio had given in to carnal pleasures before, mainly out of curiosity, but this was something else. Something primal and animalistic. She felt it like a fire roaring to life in her belly. This was new, different, and exciting. Something that beckoned her to throw caution to the wind and give in to it.

“I think your family’s legacy has gone to your head, doctor.” Agatha sneered, carefully leading Rio out of the ballroom and away from the kickoff event. She may not care for Rio Vidal’s methods, her bloodline, or any of her beliefs, but Agatha had eyes and an unusually high sex drive. The woman had needs.

Across the ballroom, Dr. Malcolm handed another colleague, Dr. Ellie Sattler, a fifty. “You called it, Ellie. In less than fifteen minutes.”

“What can I say? I know Agatha.” Ellie grinned, pocketing the money, a knowing gleam in her eye.

Agatha watched the door close behind the stunningly wrecked woman with a soft click and quietly sprang into action. Her eyes scanned over the room as she quickly redressed. Her own suite wasn’t far, and she planned to clean up there. 

Shimmying back into her jacket, her blue gaze landed on the item she was looking for. She paused momentarily and chewed on her fingernail, rethinking her choices. Looking from the object on the dresser to the bathroom door, to the bed, and then repeating the action, she hesitated. 

Something was nagging at Agatha, telling her to stay. 

It’s nothing. She thought. You haven’t done this in a while. Silently moving towards the room door, taking the item from the dresser, placing something small from her pocket behind the dresser, and quietly walking out, heels in hand so as not to make too much noise, she completed her mission. 

Thoughts swirled around her head as she walked down the hall and around the corner to her room. Once inside, she leaned against her door and exhaled loudly, running her hands through her hair, a habit of both nervousness and comfort. 

Her hands were shaking, and her breathing was shallow. She pushed away from the door, shot off a text, and quickly freshened up. Now was not the time to focus on the frustrating and devastatingly beautiful Rio Vidal.

“Here,” Agatha spat.

Ellie raised an eyebrow, taking in Agatha’s flustered state, her lack of eye contact, and her shaking hands. Agatha was the poster child of sarcasm and control.

At the moment, she looked like a high schooler who had wrestled with Cupid and lost. “This isn’t like you. Love at first fuck?”

Agatha scoffed. “Absolutely not. I just…she’s nice. I don’t like using nice people.”

“Pretty sure it was the other way around, you hardened bitch.” 

Ellie rolled her eyes. Agatha had a point. “You’re going soft on me, Harkness. Are you still in for your part tomorrow?”

Agatha blew out a breath and nodded, eyes roaming around the lobby of the massive hotel and conference center.

The blonde grinned. “Remember, you need this.”

Ellie’s eyes dropped to Agatha’s mouth before looking back into her eyes. “I told you, if you ever want to get back together…”

Agatha leaned in next to the other woman’s ear, her voice holding a seductive rasp. 

“Baby, we are never getting back together.” Agatha pulled back and smirked. “Good night, Dr. Sattler. See you in the morning.”

With that, Agatha pivoted on her heel and returned upstairs to her room. She chewed nervously on her bottom lip, trying not to dwell on the way Dr. Vidal reacted to her touch or how they were perfectly in sync with each other. 

Walking through the door of her hotel room once again, as if on cue, her phone rang. She answered, smiling at the name on the screen. “Hi sweetie, I miss you!” She smiled into the phone before launching into a conversation.

The following morning marked the official start of the conference. Agatha had already set up the room where she would be giving her workshop, using her extra time to find the room where a certain raven-haired woman was presenting.

She was unsurprised to see it was located diagonally from hers. 

How convenient.

She breezed into Rio’s space and smiled as she leaned against the table where Rio was sorting out papers.

“Good morning, Dr. Vidal. Ready to lead other scientists down the wrong path?”

 

Rio glanced over at the blue-eyed woman of her bad dreams and stormed away to set out the needed materials for attendees, refusing to meet Agatha’s eyes. Where did this woman get the audacity to come into her class, insult her intelligence, and pretend like last night never happened?

The apex predator moved away from the table and toward Rio. 

Rio finished putting out materials and walked back to the front of the room. “Please just leave, Dr. Harkness.” Her voice was hollow, quiet. “You did such a good job of it last night.”

If Agatha were a different woman, she would take Rio in her arms and apologize with soft kisses and promises she meant to keep. 

But no, Agatha Harkness was many things; sentimental and soft was not one of them. She already felt uncharacteristically guilty about using Rio to get information for Ellie.
