She knew she needed to be somewhere far removed from everyone, a place where she could remain secluded, and eventually return to her duties as Death. A place she could still share her passions but not worry about prying eyes.

Because she was staring at a positive pregnancy test, and she knew that she could not tell Agatha Harkness.

Rio Vidal had a Sacred Balance to protect, and now a child. Agatha forced her children to do things they didn’t want to do. Lived by a blurry moral code. Broke the Sacred Balance like it was a New Year’s resolution. Led her children into danger.

Not to mention Agatha had just taken over a worldwide corporation and didn’t need this.

Waiting on her couch for the two people she knew she would need, she chewed on her thumbnail, a human habit. This was going to be tricky. She would have to actually truth-bind both Lilia and Jen. Not just spell them, but actually threaten them with their lives.

Sighing, she leaned her head against the couch, nibbling on saltines, while she waited.

 

Lilia Calderu stared at Death in shock. “Not only did you just non-consensually BIND us, but you threatened our wives! It’s pretty damn hard to keep secrets like this from the woman you love, Rio! Apparently not for YOU, but for those of us that aren’t idiots!” She looked at her, fully, taking in her body language, the way she was curled in on herself.

She looked human. Scared. Unsure of herself. Placing a hand on the embodiment of a concept’s knee, she looked at her like any mother would, voice softening. “We’ll help you through this, kiddo. You can’t hide it from her forever, and I will eventually find a way to tell her if you don’t. She’s in a very bad place right now, but when she’s healthier?”

 

Rio nodded. “What do I do? This was supposed to be impossible at the time.”

“Agatha’s a rule breaker, and you did say you were in your cosmic form. Anything’s possible.” 

 

Jen huffed. “I can’t believe you aren’t telling your best friend. However, I will learn everything I can. We’ll start with morning sickness and nutrition. Obviously, this isn’t a normal pregnancy or baby. We don’t know if that tiny raptor is going to be an entity, a witch, a mortal, or some hybrid. We also don’t know if you can give birth to a living child, Rio.” Her face was full of worry and concern. Jennifer Kale had advanced the medical field by leaps and bounds in her time, but this was a completely new path. She wasn’t even allowed to consult other doctors on this, and it bothered her. 

 

Rio took it all in, not sure how to feel. She was clear about how secretive they had to be about the baby and her reasons—positive, she was doing the right thing.

 

It wasn’t like Rio had forgotten about Agatha completely, either. She kept an eye on the various projects the other woman had her hands in. Performative if you asked Rio. 

She would never personally attend any of Agatha’s ‘cleanups,’ though she knew Agatha was leaving her gifts. 

While Agatha was ending lives early, Rio was ending them exactly on time, and something was slowly shifting. Not just her body.

 

March 19th, 2021 

Early Morning, Soria, Spain

Staring down at Amelia, Rio was overcome with a love she never thought she would experience again. It wasn’t the same type of love that she had felt with Agatha, but it was just as intense. She was anchored to this innocent, fragile creature on her chest.

As Amelia fed, her tiny fist curled over Rio’s scar. Heart-shaped with three simple lines through it. Lilia had told Rio it represented the three of swords—grief, heartbreak, and sorrow. 

It stood for Agatha. 

Rio was overjoyed to be holding their little girl, and yet, she knew deep down that Agatha was a missing piece of this. 

The almighty Death doubted her judgment.

No. Agatha and Wanda were acting as though they were gods, and who knew what powers Amelia would have. Would they use her as a weapon? Would they break the rules even further? Rio continued to justify herself at every turn, no matter how absurd her reasoning was.

“I’ll keep you safe.” She whispered to Amelia, a tear quietly sliding down her cheek.

 

End of May 2023

Los Angeles, California

Soria proved to be the perfect place to raise a secret child. She was still able to teach at Complutense University of Madrid, where she taught graduate students. The nearby ruins of stone villages and sparsely populated areas provided the perfect place for them. They learned who their neighbors were and built a sense of community. 

It was close enough to the capital to be within reach, yet far enough away that no one would come looking for them. When Rio decided to leave for a quick visit to see Nicholas and Lilith graduate, she trusted one of the neighbors to keep an eye on her sleeping toddler.

Her perfect Amelia.

She had done it for several other neighbors.

The entity knew it would hurt to be near her witch, but she wasn’t here for her. She owed it to Lily and Nicky to give them something. The keychains, especially. One day, they would be able to use them, just not now.

Gifting them the apartment made sense. It was warded, protected. They could portal or teleport in and out without problem; there were plenty of enchantments and places to store anything they needed. If they ever took on familiars, the place was already proofed against them, thanks to Victoria.

It was the perfect place for sweet Nicky and the mischievous Lilith. Plus, they would have freedom from their mother.

She so desperately wished she could tell them about their baby sister and how much she reminded Rio of them. Of their mother.

She had been close to telling Lily to take the swing. 

It had been a hard, painful reunion, but necessary. Being that close to Agatha was like a punch to the gut. Death had to remind herself that she was doing the right thing. While Agatha was chasing immortality, Death was making sure everyone left the world exactly when they were supposed to.



June 2025

Madrid, Spain

 

Lilia Calderu had had enough of keeping the truth from Agatha. She wasn't sure how Jennifer worked next to Pietro most days and still did. Of course, they had problems of their own. Medications meant to prolong life were no longer doing their job. 

People were no longer beating the odds. People were surviving freak accidents less and less.

The Sacred Balance was becoming too perfect. Too balanced.

Lilia had an idea what was happening, but there were other wrongs to correct before they could even touch that one. 

It was why Lilia had suggested that Lily study abroad that summer, specifically in Madrid. “Sharon and I have a home there; she can stay with me!” She had told Agatha.

“Well, I’ll be busy with the move, and it’ll be good for her…” Agatha had conceded.

Thus, Lilia’s plan had worked, and she just needed to get her to stumble upon Amelia. Thankfully for her, Rio and Amelia spent more time around campus during the summer, and the four-year-old was an adventurous one. She knew the campus like the back of her hand and often wandered away from the day camp she was supposed to stay with.

Lilith had opted to stay with her Nonna instead of on campus. While money wasn’t an issue, she still wanted to use it responsibly. She also wanted the freedom staying off campus provided. 

She sat near a fountain in the courtyard, eating the lunch she had brought, looking over the syllabus from one of her morning classes. 

Lily knew her Nonna wanted her to be here because Rio was a professor at the university. She had looked over the classes, thankful she wasn’t able to take any taught by her.

A fact she had not shared with her mother. Agatha was occupied with the move and refocusing on academia. Lilith was not going to let anything interfere with that. The elder Harkness was doing better than she had been since that night five years ago. Her mother's relationship with Darcy was flourishing, and she and Wanda were ecstatic for her. 

Rio Vidal could fuck all the way off.

Yes, the Harkness twins lived in an apartment owned by Lady Death. It didn’t mean that Lilith had to like the cunt that turned her mother into a shell of a human for all those years. The light had just returned to those piercing blue eyes.

A flash of wild brown hair caught the young woman’s eyes. Glancing in that direction, she saw a young girl in jeans and a shirt, climbing a tree on the edge of the courtyard, with no regard for self-preservation. “The fuck is her mother?”

Lily reached her just as the girl slipped and began to fall, straight into Lily’s outstretched arms. Words died in her throat as she looked at the giggling bundle she held.

The mystery child looked just like her.

Lilith set her on her feet, finally forming words. “You have to be careful!”

The little girl continued giggling. “I was okay! I broke my arm when I was three, I stop myself now.” She looked at the taller girl in front of her and tilted her head, snapping her mouth shut, a knowing look in her eyes.

“How old are you?” Lily asked quietly.

The shorter version of her shyly held up four fingers.

The twenty-year-old, who was supposed to be a functioning adult, swallowed thickly. “Is Rio Vidal your mom?”

The miniature Agatha nodded her head.

“Wh…what’s your name, sweetie?” Lily asked, kneeling.

“Amelia Jade Harkness-Vidal. You’re Lilith Harkness. I’ve seen you in mama’s dreams.” 

Lily choked on a sob.



Lilith wasn’t sure how Lilia had gotten there or how she had made it through her afternoon class. She was in shock at the revelation, and it had passed in a blur.

She was sitting next to Amelia in Lilia’s kitchen, coloring and laughing, when she felt it.

Rio had used a truth-binding spell before Lily had even thought to tell her mother. She had been so shocked that she hadn’t even thought to call her with the discovery. 

It’s why Lilia and Jen, she found out, hadn’t said a word in five years.

Rio hadn’t let them.

“Mama!” Amelia shouted, running and hugging her mother’s waist. “I finally met Lily!”

Her former mentor and friend didn’t even look at the college student. “So I heard, cariño.” Gathering Amelia in her arms, looking at her as if she held the world.

Lilith Harkness was unimpressed.

“Milly here, wandered away from her play group again,” Lilia explained, leaning against the doorway.

Rio hummed, kissing her daughter’s cheek and setting her back down. “What have I told you about that?”

If Lily hadn’t been so pissed, she would have been more amused when the young girl twirled around, and in a sing-song voice, as though annoyed by her mother, replied, “Stay with the adults and other children, it’s not safe to run off alone.”

“Exactly. Now, say your goodbyes; it’s time to go home, mija.”

Amelia tossed her wild hair behind her shoulder just like the mother she had never met, rolled her shoulders, and stood her ground. 

Lily raised an eyebrow, curious as to what this little menace was about to do. Looking up at Lilia, now standing at her side, she wasn’t sure either. 

The precocious and well-spoken four-year-old watched as her mother walked toward the door, finally speaking. “Quiero que Lily venga a cenar con nosotras. Quiero conocerla mejor, mamá.” Her arms crossed over her chest, fire and determination in her eyes.

Rio stopped, pinched the bridge of her nose, and slowly turned around. She returned to a more neutral and open position, smiling sweetly at her child, though she let out an exasperated sigh. “No esta noche, Amelia. Hoy ha sido un día largo. Mamá está cansada.” 

The littlest witch shifted her weight to her other leg and pouted, not giving in that easily. “Es mi hermana. Quiero estar con ella… ni siquiera sé cuál es su animal favorito.”

Lily covered her mouth to stifle her laughter. A DNA test wouldn’t even be needed. That child was Agatha Harkness through and through. While Milly’s defiance made her laugh, the conversation was also another knife to the back and proved that her little sister didn’t know her at all.

Like their mothers, Lily also spoke several languages. Spanish being one of them. 

It wasn’t something she would tell the child just yet, and when Rio looked up at her and opened her mouth, Lily was quick to cut her off.

“I’m still picking up Spanish. I studied other languages in high school, if you recall. Something about dinner and my favorite animal?” The look she gave Rio dared her to ruin this.

The entity slowly nodded her head. “Yes, Amelia wants you to join us for dinner.”

“Great! She was telling me that Mondays are family dinner night with Nonna.” She looked up at the woman still next to her. “Are you coming?”

Lilia smirked. “Oh, I wouldn’t miss this for anything.”

 

Rio was in a hell of her own making. She had been so busy parenting a cosmic hybrid who already had magick, monitoring and managing the Sacred Balance, and teaching grad students. It hadn’t registered that Lilith had hit campus for summer courses.

She knew Lilia was in town; she had offered to help with Amelia. She should’ve known the old relic was up to something. Lilia and Jen both had been growing more vocal about involving Agatha. Coming out of hiding. 

Rio had kept her daughter a secret for so long that she didn’t know how even to broach the subject with Agatha.

When Lilia called her to tell her that Lily and Amelia had met, Death was filled with fear. 

What if Agatha found out? What would she do?

She had bound the secret to Lily the moment she had seen her. Yes, Lily would be furious, but she knew the girl. The spell wouldn’t last long.

The thing she hadn’t seen coming was Amelia Jade being as manipulative and conniving as her other mother. If Rio hadn’t felt so damn guilty, she wouldn’t have caved so easily. How could she say no with the three witches looking at her as though she were the villain?

So she folded like origami. 

Lilith Harkness had decided to cook dinner for them. Continuing with the genetic trait of manipulation, she made an American breakfast for dinner. Even explained the fucking tradition to Amelia. 

“Now, Green Goblin,” Lily directed at the youngest Harkness, eliciting giggles.

Rio looked away. Yes, she wanted this connection for her daughter. Craved a house full of the entire family if she were honest with herself.

But wasn’t she doing what was best? 

Isn’t that what she had been telling herself all this time?

But here, with Lilia giving her that smug look that screamed ‘I told you so’, and Amelia hanging on every word, from this big sister she had only seen in Rio’s dreams, Death had begun to seriously doubt herself.

“You know I’m not your only sibling, right?” Lily continued, dishing cheesy scrambled eggs onto a plate for the wide-eyed preschooler.

The small menace nodded her head vigorously. “I’ve seen everyone in Mama’s dreams and memories! There are the adults, Ralph and Malcolm. They’re husbands. Ellie, she’s like an aunt, and then Agatha.” Rio sucked in her breath, waiting to hear how Milly labeled her. Instead, her voice remained neutral, and she hurried past the woman, of all the things her daughter could’ve done, that hurt the worst. 

“Then there’s us kids. Pietro and Wanda. Wanda is married to Zora, and they have twin boys. Nicky and you, then…” her voice dropped to a whisper, and she dug her fork into her eggs. “Me.”

Rio couldn’t bear to see the looks from Lily and Lilia, but she felt them.

For once, she heard the pain in her daughter’s voice.

Lily cleared her throat, placing a pancake on Amelia’s plate. “That’s right, Green Goblin. When mom and dad, that’s Agatha and Ralph, adopted Wanda and Pietro, the twins wouldn’t eat much. So mom made breakfast for dinner. It became a family tradition and one of the first things she taught Wanda to make. Wanda taught me to make it, and now, I’m going to start teaching you this summer.”

The little girl’s eyes shimmered in anticipation, nearly vibrating in her seat for the chance to bond this way with the family she had known about for her entire existence. "And I can teach you Spanish!" 

Lily sat down after serving everyone and placing various breakfast foods on the table. “Deal." Lily grinned. "Alright, kiddo, you want to know my favorite animal?” She leaned close to her, as though it were to be a secret.

Rio watched her every movement, bracing for impact, seeing the smirk from Lilia next to her, already knowing the college student’s intention. 

With a mouth full of chocolate chip pancake, the youngest woman at the table nodded eagerly, hungry for any tidbits of information.

In a loud whisper, Lilith, the demon that she was, locked eyes with Rio. “Triceratops.”

Rio sucked in a breath. Knowing damn well what Agatha’s fucking hell spawn was hinting at.

Milly gasped. “My favorite animal is a dinosaur, too!” She exclaimed around her next bite of pancake, this one including whipped cream. “Try guessing!

“Don’t speak with your mouth full, cariño,” Rio reprimanded, only to receive a swat on the arm from Lilia. 

The older sister leaned back in her chair slightly, her finger tapping her chin as though she were deep in thought. “Let’s see, a velociraptor?”

Brown curls shook back and forth.

“No, of course not. One of the long-necked species?”

A scrunched nose.

“No, too tall.”

Rio stood quickly to clear the plates. She knew exactly what her daughter’s favorite animal was, even though she hadn’t told her just how deep the meaning behind it went. She knew Lilith would pick up on it immediately and had to brace herself for impact.

“I know!” Lily said, slapping her knee. “Tyrannasaurus!”

“No, silly!” The chaos gremlin laughed. “It’s a Troodon!”

The scraping of Lilia’s chair against the tile, excusing herself to call Sharon, faded into the background. Rio gripped the sink, taking slow breaths. She could feel the eyes burning into her back.

“Oh?” Lily was trying to keep her voice even. “Why is that?"

Amelia’s voice had taken a most serious tone. “They’re small, and they know things, like I do. They also have feathers, and they’re climbers.” She nodded her head sharply. “Small and fierce.”

Lily nodded in agreement. “You’re right, just like you.”

“Why is the Triceratops your favorite? Is it because they’re horny?” 

Rio laughed when the older girl nearly choked on her water. 

“While the horns are cute, no, that’s not it. It’s because they always travel in packs, or herds.” She looked back at Rio when Death turned around. “They knew they were safer as a unit and always protected the children, keeping them in the center, close to their parents.”

Rio looked away from the accusatory glare. She knew exactly what Lily had been getting at the first time.

“They did?” Amelia finally laid her fork down, pushing the plate away. “The baby dinosaurs always had their older siblings around?”

“Sure did, kid.”

Amelia sighed. “I like them too.”



Rio walked onto the porch, leaning against the railing. “Where’s Lilia?”

Lily sat in an old, sturdy rocking chair. “She went ahead and drove home. I told her I’d apparate when I was ready.”

“Bold of you.”

Lily scoffed, taking a pull of her drink. “Amelia asleep?” 

“She is,” her words were careful as she crossed her arms and waited for the verbal blows. “Why did you stay, Lilith?”

“Thought I’d let you explain yourself before I suggest to my mother that she come for a visit.”

Rio’s mouth tugged up at one corner. “You know I’d leave before she got here.”

Lily crossed her legs at the knee. “Nonna and Aunt Jen may have been unable to break your little spell, but I have access to the Darkhold. I will make this right if you don’t, Rio.” Her eyes were cold as she looked at her with contempt. “The tightness of your jaw tells me you know I’m capable.” 

“You don’t get to come in here and tell me what is right and wrong with my child, Lilith. You’re still one yourself.” Rio’s words were hard.

“Oh, that’s rich, coming from you. You go around telling everyone what is right and wrong, what is morally just. That was the entire reason you left Mom, wasn’t it?” Her words were sharp, landing heavily in Rio’s chest. “I won’t lie and say you haven’t done a good job with her; she’s a great kid. But Rio, I spent half a day with her, and I can see how traumatized she is.” She placed her empty bottle on the ground. “She’s four, Rio. She longs for a family that she’s never met. A family she doesn’t know why she can’t be a part of.”

Amber eyes looked away. “I never thought she’d find out before I was ready.”

“Oh, that makes it better.”

Rio cut her eyes to Lily before continuing. “Her magick came in when she was around one. She would sleep with me around that age. Toddlers can exhaust even cosmic entities.”

“I believe it. I’m tired just from spending part of the day with her. I can’t imagine what she was like when she was younger.” 

Rio smiled fondly. “Well, apparently, I talk out in my sleep. She put a hand on my temple, and I realized she was in my dream. She can hide herself in them now. She’s seen everyone, all of my memories. If I’m crying in my sleep, she sneaks into my dreams to make it better.”

“Good job traumatizing her before she’s five.” Lily scoffed.

“Oh, get off it. I didn’t know she was going to dream hop before she spoke a full sentence, okay?” She pushed off the post and flopped into the seat next to Lily.  

“She’s half cosmic entity, no one knows what exactly she’s capable of,” Rio added.

“She’s hurting, Rio. She is four and a half, and she’s hurting.” Lily looked over at Rio and stated the facts. “I can’t let that happen, and I won’t. Family is forever, you bag of bones, and now that I’m here, now that I know she exists? It’s my job, my duty, to protect her.”

“Oh, please, Lilith. You think I’m going to let you waltz in here and allow you to—”

“Yes. Because you love Amelia and you want what’s best for her. I know for a fact that Lilia is trying to persuade you to take a position at Harvard. You’re going to send an email accepting the position. You have until the day classes resume for the fall semester to tell her, or I will.”

Rio stared at Lily, mouth agape. “Who are you to give me ultimatums?” 

“Someone who has the power to teleport with my sister, straight to my mother. Try a deity, not me.” Lily smirked.

Rio’s eyes narrowed. “Why Harvard?”

“Mom’s beginning a new position there, too. Don’t look at me like that. Makes co-parenting easier. The whole family is East Coast now. Oh, and Rio?”

The defeated entity hummed.

“You will be seeing me a lot this summer. Now that I know about Amelia, I will not be staying away from her.”

Lily grinned and teleported back to Lilia’s.

Rio let out a groan, head falling back in defeat.

These damn Harkness women. 

Every day, Amelia proved to her just how strong those genes were.

🦕🦖🦕

Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts

August 18, 2025

Rio had accepted the position at Harvard that night at Lily’s insistence, the little brat. It had always been hers if she wanted it, and she knew it. While she enjoyed the solitude and open space Soria provided, she was somewhat looking forward to the move. 

Amelia was ecstatic, and that’s what mattered. Her daughter had helped choose a house, getting Victoria and Lily’s opinions, of course. Decorating had been a family endeavor, and moving had been a chore. 

Amelia had insisted that Lily help her decorate her room, which Rio considered a bit of payback for pushing this in the first place. Lily had to be extra careful not to alert Agatha that she was in town, and had done much of it while portaling or Facetiming from Spain. She still had classes to attend and a GPA to maintain. 

Rio would be lying if she said she was upset that Amelia and Lily had connected. Amelia had never been happier, Lily having filled some of the gap left by the absence of her family. Milly had always wanted siblings, craved them. Especially since she knew she had four older ones roaming the earth. 

Her Green Goblin nickname stuck, and she became Lily’s shadow as much as possible.  

Rio hoped this didn’t come back to bite her in the ass.

She had already spent time on campus after moving back to the States. On that day when most professors were returning to their offices, she had met Lilia and Milly for lunch. Lily was finishing up in Madrid and would be back in New York at the end of the week before visiting them in Boston.

Dr. Vidal knew she would see Agatha today, and she planned to tell her ex soon. The longer she waited, the worse it would be for everyone involved. Agatha had a bit of a temper, after all.

 

Approaching her office after lunch, she noticed her door was open, and the light was on. Her stomach flipped. “No.” She whispered to herself, slipping inside.

Rio’s breath always caught when she saw Agatha for the first time after extended absences, and this time was no different. Agatha held the picture of Amelia with reverence and awe. The love of a mother etched into her skin, and the tears of a mother’s grief rolling down her cheeks.

For just a moment, Rio knew she fucked up.

“Agatha?” 

 

Now, in the courtyard, looking up at the darkened sky, she realized why she should have told Agatha earlier.

Mortals ran in chaos as the earth shook and fractured from Agatha’s earthquake.

She could feel the number of the dead rising.

Rio’s storm was moving in catastrophically fast.

A hurricane was forming in the Atlantic and hauling ass toward them.

When most couples argued, a few hurtful words were said.

Not them.

Death touched the back of her head, touching the sticky red blood at the base of her skull. “Well, at least I can’t die.” She mumbled to herself.

“What a fucking pity.” Agatha sneered, floating down from their floor above, her jeans and purple blouse seeming very out of place amongst the growing wreckage and increasingly high winds.

 

Agatha had been murderous before; this was a new feeling. She was seething with rage; she had been violated in an unspeakable way, one she didn’t know was possible. 

By the person who was supposed to love her more than anything. The person she was made for.

Agatha Harkness wanted to tear Death apart piece by piece with her bare fucking hands.

Yet she knew she would never do that to the mother of her child, and that realization kicked her in the gut. 

Reaching out, thankful for the limited visibility and the stinging rain now falling around them, she grabbed Rio by the collar and raised her into the air. “When were you going to tell me?”

Rio’s eyes stayed on her, watching her every move. “I was going to tell you before classes started.”

“That’s specific.” The murderous witch scoffed.

“Lily gave me a deadline.”

Agatha saw red.

She threw Rio across the quad into another building. The blaring storm sirens announcing the obvious tornado were disregarded. Pieces of the ground jutted up in places, and debris flew in every direction. 

Agatha advanced across the space to where Rio landed as if it were a sunny, windless day.

“WITCH! SHE’S A WITCH!” A student cried before being pelted by a rogue bike.

Agatha knew this would all be cleaned up, but right now, she didn’t care if she destroyed the entire damn planet.

A figure began to emerge from the rubble of the building where Rio had gone through it, Agatha blasting it with an electrical current.

She couldn’t kill death, but she could make her hurt.

“LILITH KNOWS?! Start talking, you Spirit Halloween reject!”

Lightning struck a tree near Agatha, felling it before her, only to cause the witch to apparate right next to the cosmic entity.

“Boo,” Agatha whispered into Rio’s ear before sending her across the lobby of the building.

Rio sent a harmless blast of energy into the witch. “I taught in Madrid; Lilia got her to take summer classes there.”

Violet eyes flared. “LILIA KNEW! The closest thing I’ve ever had to a real mother knew!”

“You used Lilia against me! AND JEN!” Rio shot back, flying across the space and pinning the other woman to a wall.

“Jen knows!” Agatha exhaled. “Did she…did she deliver?” Her voice wavered, despite herself.

Rio nodded.

Agatha pushed the darker-haired woman away before throwing a vase at her. “Them I can forgive, BUT LILY!?”

“I bound them all! Lily, it was an accident. They ran into each other. I said the spell before she could even call you.”

Dual storms raged outside, the building crumbling around them. Agatha knew there was so much to say, but right now, it was all hurt, anger, and betrayal.

“Why not make her keep it from me forever, then?” She dodged the dagger aimed at her.

“Because your fucking hell spawn threatened me.” Rio tackled her, and they rolled back into the roaring storm and flying debris outside, a vehicle landing next to them.

 

Agatha cackled, rearing her head back and slamming her forehead into the nose of the woman on top of her—blood gushing. Rio rolled off of her, and the witch scrambled to her feet. “Let me get this right, my twenty-year-old, mortal daughter, who, according to you, I did a terrible job parenting, threatened you.”  

Dodging a light pole that swirled in the air, she turned back to her ex, “From what I gather, and just knowing my daughter, she showed up, guilt-tripped you, threatened to find a way around your spell, bonded with her…” Agatha cleared her throat, “and out-smarted you.” 

Rio screamed and threw a slab of cement at Agatha, one that she quickly ducked down to dodge. “No! I had to keep her safe, Agatha! Protect the sacred balance! Protect her!”

“I have protected and kept my children safe, Rio! EVERYTHING I’VE DONE WAS FOR THEM!” She roared, sirens growing louder, the smell of smoke and death in the air.

“THEN WHY COULDN’T YOU DO SOMETHING FOR ME!” 

Agatha hit the ground when a bench crashed into her, the metallic taste filling her mouth.

“I HAD A FIFTH CHILD, AND YOU DIDN’T TELL ME!” She yelled back at Death, a clap of thunder echoing across the destroyed campus.

 

“STOP!” Lilia Calderu’s voice cried out, and they both turned to see the small woman close by. “What the fuck are you two doing? Do you know how much damage control we’re going have to do? Stop the storms, now.”

Her gaze swept over the rubble. “Reverse time, we’re going to have to reverse time.”

Agatha snapped her head back to Rio. “What’s her name?”

“Amelia Jade Harkness-Vidal.”

“You…” Her voice trailed off. “I didn’t hit you hard enough.”

🦕🦖🦕

Rio watched as Lilia used the hourglass of the fates to rewind the events of the afternoon. Undoing the damage from their fight. 

“Good job, you immortal moron.”

“Think she’ll cool off soon?” Rio glanced over at the petite woman, hoping for the best.

“No, but you have to fix this.” Lilia looked over the campus, satisfied with her work and vanishing the hourglass. “You get Lily, and the two of you take Amelia to the house once Lily senses her mother has cooled off. It’s time for them to meet. You have a more civilized conversation.”

Rio shook her head. “No. What if—”

Lilia sighed. “Agatha will not do anything in front of the kids, especially Amelia. It’s the best way, Rio.” She put an arm on her shoulder. “Think of Milly. Of what she needs.”

Her protests died in her throat, knowing Lilia was right. Amelia needed Agatha, always had. Milly had thrived that summer with Lily so close, and in turn, Lily had ripped into Rio quite a bit. It was one of the reasons Rio was secretly happy Lily had been so insistent all summer.

Rio grimaced, turning to Lilia, noticing the lack of news vans, feeling that death had been erased. “Did you completely undo everything? Erase the deaths? All damage?”

Lilia tutted, knowing she had taken a jab at Death’s recent goal of perfection. “Think of it as payback for making me keep that secret for five years.”

“But the—” Rio started.

“Stick it in your ass, Cryptkeeper.” Lilia vanished back to Rio’s house.

“No one respects my job.” Death muttered, returning to her office.

🦕🦖🦕

Agatha walked into her large home, tossing her bag onto the couch, and walked straight into Wanda’s waiting arms.

“How did you know to be here?” 

Wanda scoffed. “Time may have been rewound, but that was still quite a doozy. I stopped everyone else from descending, and thought it best to come here instead of ground zero.”

Agatha snorted. “You would have found a way to destroy her. So, it’s better this way.”

Wanda pulled away, reaching for a glass of water to hand to the woman before her. “Why is she here?”

“New professor at Harvard.”

“Okayyy…”

“She had a child, Wanda. Our child. She hid her from me.”

Wanda’s eyes flared. “I’ll fucking send her ass to the void.”

Agatha put a hand on her forearm, eyes dropping toward the younger woman’s abdomen. “No stress, that’s my granddaughter in there. I’m surprised Zora even let you come here, seven months pregnant.” She watched scarlet eyes return to green, guiltily look away. “You didn’t tell her. Sit down and put your feet up.”

Agatha led her daughter to the couch, making sure she was comfortable. She then restlessly began walking around the living room, still covered in dirt and blood, before telling Wanda everything she had learned.

“You need to take a shower, Mom.” 

The older woman stilled. She knew that when Wanda switched to one of those monikers, it was to make sure Agatha heard the care and love behind her intentions, not as her friend who wanted her to take care of herself, but as the daughter who would do anything to help her mother stay grounded.

Agatha nodded. “Do you need anything before I go? A snack? Pillow? Anything?”

The redhead chuckled. “I’ve done this before, you know. I’ll be okay.” Wanda watched her slowly walk away. “Is Darcy…” Her words trailed off, not needing to finish the sentence.

It was only Monday, and while Darcy was in New York this week, Agatha had sent her a text before leaving work. 

She nodded, voice shaky. “I texted her. She’s going to work from here this week; she’ll be here later tonight. I didn’t tell her everything, just that I, uh, needed her.”

Agatha knew it was a big step for her to lean on someone other than her family and her coven, both human and witch. Darcy was quickly falling into that group, wasn’t she? 

The truth was, Agatha had growing feelings for Darcy, and she knew she could rely on her. She needed her there to help her through this. Hers was the first message thread she had tapped on once she had gotten in the car.

 

Walking towards her bedroom and en-suite, Agatha looked over the pictures on the walls: the family pictures, the kids' pictures, the graduation pictures.

Amelia would have been two for the twins' graduation. She should have taken turns posing with her older siblings, just like Nicky and Lily had when Wanda and Pietro had graduated.

Her children had the same age gap. Sixteen years.

Five years of pictures missing from her walls.

Five years of memories.

The tears finally came in the shower, mixing with the water falling around her.

Of all the injustices, betrayals, and abuse she had faced in her life.

This was by far the worst. 

 

Lily had reached out, asking if they could come by. Agatha had hesitated, eyes darting to Wanda. She was actually nervous to meet her youngest daughter. She still agreed, of course.  

Wearing lounge clothes, damp hair in a ponytail, and no makeup, she chewed nervously on the skin around her thumbnail, lightly perched on the arm of the sofa. “What if she hates me?”

Wanda was texting Lily furiously. “Lily says she knows it’s not your fault.”

“What if Rio refuses visitation?” 

The Scarlet Witch gave her a look. “We find a way to cage her. I don’t know! We figure it out. Now, I will stay a bit longer, but they’re here.”

Agatha rose from her perch and silently crossed the room to the front door, Lily walking in before she could get there.

The young woman smiled and wrapped her mother in a hug. “Breathe, mom,” she reminded, moving aside.

Agatha’s heart nearly left her body. 

Standing next to Rio, a death grip on her hand, was a perfect mix of Agatha and Death. She grinned at Agatha, letting go of her Mama’s hand and taking a step toward her. 

Agatha knelt immediately, arms open. She wasn’t sure what either of their responses would be, but the second she laid eyes on this hidden daughter, it was automatic.

Amelia ran into her arms, grinning from ear to ear. The young girl was radiating joy, a touch of sadness still lingering beneath it. Agatha would yell at Rio for that later.

There wasn’t a drop of doubt in Agatha, not wanting to break in front of the tiny witch she now held in her arms. This was her daughter, a piece of her she hadn’t known existed, and the girl was perfect.

Agatha pulled away slightly, enough to look at her again. Her fingers traced the small face, mapping every part of her, committing the image to memory.

Amelia raised her eyebrows, attempting to raise only one but failing. “Why are you crying, mommy?”

Agatha nearly lost her balance at hearing the name from the little girl’s lips. Lifting her gaze slightly, she was met with Rio’s clenched jaw and defensive stance, as though she wasn’t the only one surprised.

Turning back to her daughter, her smile waterlogged, “I just have a lot of emotions right now.” Trembling fingers pushed unruly hair behind a small ear. She lowered herself into a sitting position, Amelia immediately crawling into her lap as if it was an old habit. They locked eyes, blue mirroring blue. “I am crying because I have loved you my whole life, even before I knew you.”

Amelia’s small hand came up to cup her mother’s cheek. “You might not have known me, but I’ve known you my whole life. I’ve always loved you, Mommy. I knew you’d love me too.” She wrapped her arms around the mother she had always craved to meet and buried her head in her neck. Taking her in.

They sat like that, mother and daughter, enjoying the moment.

 

The other three women had moved to the kitchen, though they could still see and hear everything. Wanda took her sister’s hand and squeezed it. “Mom told me everything. I’m so sorry you had to shoulder the secret on your own.”

Lily shrugged, keeping her eyes on their Mother. “I wouldn’t have wanted to add any stress to you or Pietro. He’s got enough going on with the medication breakdowns. Besides, I had Nonna and Aunt Jen. I so badly wanted to tell Nicky and Dads though. Once Mom okay’s it, I’ll let Nicky know immediately.” 

Rio cleared her throat and looked over at Wanda. “Congratulations, by the way.”

“I will give you a piece of my mind later. Raising my blood pressure isn’t good for the baby.” Wanda hissed.

Lily snickered. “Told you.”

Hearing the words Agatha spoke to the youngest Harkness, the older Harkness daughters exchanged a look. 

Rio looked between them. “What?”

Wanda wiped away a tear, “She said the same thing to Pietro and me.”

Rio rolled her eyes. “So she just used a line on my daughter?”

“No.” Wanda spat before softening. “It’s the words of a true mother, bones for brains. She said it again with Nicky and Lily, the boys too.” Wanda stopped, looking over Rio. “Did you not feel that way about Amelia? Like this was someone you were just now meeting, but you had loved your entire life?”

Lily sneered, muttering. “If you didn’t, Mom gets full custody.”

Rio sent an icy glare Lily’s way. “Yes, I just didn’t have words for it.” She turned, looking into the living room to see her ex holding their daughter on the floor.

“Lily, I need to talk with your mother. Will you watch your sister?” 

“I’ll help. I also want to meet my baby sister. You hid her from all of us, Rio. I can’t kill you, but I could show you your insides.” Wanda mused, eating yogurt she had procured from the refrigerator.

“Not in front of the kid,” Lily smirked as Death huffed and walked back to the living room.



Agatha heard Rio’s footsteps and knew she would have to let her baby go. Lifting her head, she opened her mouth to protest, though Rio spoke first.

“Cariño, I need to talk to Agatha. Lily and Wanda are going to watch you. I know Wands is very excited to meet you.” Her voice was light, loving, motherly.

It made Agatha melt and harden at the same time.

Amelia sighed, clambering to her feet. “Mommy, can we talk after? I have so much to tell you!”

Agatha’s chest tightened as she nodded. “Of course we can.” She watched as she ran to Lily and took her hand, already asking about American candy.

She rose easily to her feet and gestured for Rio to follow her into the office. Once there, she closed and locked the door, then magickally sealed it against her nosy but well-meaning daughters.

Standing at the window that looked into the backyard, the now mother of five watched Wanda take a seat and ask questions while Lily introduced Amelia to Señor Scratchy.

“March nineteenth, twenty-twenty-one. Morning, your time. Assuming you were in Spain.” Agatha stated, not looking back. 

“What? How?” Rio stood at an angle where she knew Agatha could see her reflection in the window glass.

“I hadn’t felt the pull as much, but it tugged so hard that night, and this fucking scar you left burned. It was like I was being ripped open. Or, it would’ve been if I hadn’t numbed it with a very large amount of alcohol.” Her lips twitched when Scratchy transformed mid-air into a medium-sized dog. “There were also multiple volcanoes that day, a tornado in fucking Alaska, and storms all over the place. I figured you’d finally lost it. Which, looking back, you kind of did.”

“I had a baby, Agatha, not a lobotomy.”

“I HAVE A FIFTH CHILD, AND YOU DIDN’T TELL ME ABOUT HER!” Agatha repeated the words from earlier, grateful she had made the office soundproof. “There were signs! The Divine Mother tried to tell me, and I ignored them.” She shook her head, eyes glued to the scene in her backyard. “She broke her right arm when she was three. I had sudden shooting pains in my arm. Thought I was having a heart attack. The first time she screamed through the night, Lilia sang her the old Witches Road ballad, and now you sing it to her every time she has a nightmare.”

“How do you know that?” Death raised an eyebrow in disbelief.

Finally turning from the window, her face was hard, cold. “Because it gets stuck in my head, EVERY SINGLE FUCKING TIME.” She padded barefoot across the hardwood, arms gesturing. “The bond that YOU broke was trying to tell me, because even IT knew you were in the wrong.” Inhaling deeply, her eyes landed on Rio. “As if hiding my child wasn’t enough. You swore Lilia and Jen to secrecy—MY OWN COVEN. THEN, you forced ANOTHER one of my children to keep it from me? I haven’t seen or heard from her all summer, only updates from Lilia. As I told you earlier, Lilia and Jen, I understand. BUT LILITH?!”

Looking around at the pictures, books, various knick-knacks, and artwork from her children, Agatha’s pain deepened. “She couldn’t even lean on her siblings! I hope she gave you hell.”

Rio hummed. “Oh, she did.” 

The witch let out a harsh laugh. “Surely you’ve realized by now that every single one of my children inherited something from me. Pietro’s eye for detail and his problem-solving skills. Wanda’s compassion for those she loves and her martyr complex. Nicky’s protective, sometimes overly so. Lily? Oh, she’s fierce. She’s smart, and she’ll rip you to shreds.”

Rio nodded. “She did. Amelia’s just like her, and you.”

Agatha struggled to keep her composure. “Why did you do it? Hide her from me?”

Rio poked her tongue in her cheek before taking a deep breath and explaining. “Watching you force Wanda to do things, knowing you’ve shaped the Scarlet Witch and allowed her to use dark magick, knowing the things you’ve taught your other children? Your disregard for the Sacred Balance? You’ve been playing as if you’re a god, Agatha. Deciding who moves on and who doesn’t. Ending lives early, running around, doing what you please. Stealing magick for your own personal gain, creating immortals. I couldn’t trust you with a child that’s half cosmic being and half extremely powerful witch from the start.”

The younger being’s voice was cold, too quiet, too even. “You couldn’t trust me with my own child? You have seen me with my children, Rio. Everything I did was for them. Stealing your power? That was to cure them of the curses Evanora had on them. Keeping Evanora’s soul from moving on? That’s for everyone’s safety. I know I’ve done terrible things. I’m Agatha Harkness, but you know what I never once did? I never lied about loving you.”

Rio gasped in shock. “I never lied about that!”

“Oh, really? Could’ve fooled me! You sure as hell cemented the connection and then BROKE it in less than a few hours, Rio. Do you know the hell I went through? I spent years cleaning up the planet, I’m still righting Evanora’s wrongs, and you know what? I. Fucking. Love. It.”

The entity turned away and huffed. “I saw the things you did, I thought they were a performative ploy to get me back.”

Agatha shrugged. “It was my form of a skeleton signal, yes, but I have enjoyed reshaping the company, fixing nature. I’ve been restoring balance. I actually enjoy the looks on kids' faces when they have clean water again. I don’t give a shit about the adults, but it’s nice the kids are safe.”

“I almost shattered again when you showed up at graduation, and it took all I had not to. How dare you, by the way. 

“And now? It took me nearly five years to piece myself back together, for my family to piece me back together. I practically lived with Ellie for a while. I couldn’t sleep alone because of the nightmares, I crawled into a bottle and had to be fished out.” She ran a hand roughly through her hair, wild blue eyes staring at Rio. “I lived with the consequences of my actions, AND yours. And ALL THIS TIME, you were hiding my child from me. Do you not see how much worse that is? I would have forgiven you for everything else. I am many things, Rio, but a deadbeat dad isn’t one of them.”

Rio’s head snapped around to face her ex. “You said I had to change to be with you! To be the old me! To be weak!”

Moving back to the window, the witch exhaled shakily. “No, Rio. I wanted you in all your cosmic glory, powerful, the one whom I was made for. But with your humanity. The one who saw the world in shades of grey, the woman who would never have questioned my parenting, never hidden a child from me, who once wanted to murder Evanora herself. Now, you are carrying out the Sacred Balance too perfectly, and the consequences are coming, Rio.” She fixed her eyes on three of her five children in the yard.

“The thing that hurts the most, Rio, is that you—the woman I loved, the woman who I was once destined to be with, robbed me.”

Rio hesitated, brows scrunched together. “I didn’t take anything, Agatha—”

“YES YOU DID!” The witch lashed out in fury. “Her first breath, steps, word! The first time she got sick, you were up with her all night, worried and not sure what to do! The first time she kicked, and you felt that flutter! Who was there to share that excitement, Rio?” Pacing in front of the window like a caged tiger, she avoided looking at Rio. “That specific mix of joy, fear, and anxiety of the positive test. The first time you heard the heartbeat.” Her words broke on a sob, red-rimmed eyes finally meeting Rio’s. 

Agatha Harkness finally breaking in front of Death.

“You stole that from me! Didn’t you know I’d want to be there?” She scrubbed her eyes with the palms of her hands. “I bet you were glowing, you celestial bitch. I would have taken such good care of you. You robbed me of that, too.”

Wiping her hands on her sweats, she looked back at the woman in front of her. “You bitch about the fucking Sacred Balance, about right and wrong, but YOU took the most sacred moments of our child’s life from me. How is that fair? How is that just?” She pointed a long, slender finger at the so-called entity. “You can’t claim to be above everything when you’ve stooped lower than I ever have, and I killed my own mother and enjoyed it.” She began to move toward the door, not wanting to waste anymore time that could be spent connecting with Amelia.

“You said that I was no better than Evanora, but what you’ve done is far worse than any cruelty I ever experienced at her hand.” Placing her hand on the doorknob, she took another breath, preparing to join the young women in the backyard. “Fuck you, Rio, and fuck you for ruining all the godsdamned peace that I fought tooth and nail to get back.” 

 

Rio watched Agatha unseal the room and walk out, words replaying in her head. She turned her head to look out at the sisters in the backyard. Lily and Amelia had built a strong bond over the summer, and Rio was thankful for it. Now, Milly had her hand on Wanda’s pregnant belly and was laughing. 

Rio knew Amelia had longed for this. She had watched her daughter project illusions of her siblings and mother when the younger girl didn’t think her Mama was watching. Listened to how she spoke to them. 

Lily was right when she said Amelia was having an identity crisis at four. 

Maybe everyone else had been right. Maybe Rio had made the absolutely wrong choice. She just hoped it wasn’t too late to fix it.

🦕🦖🦕

Agatha splashed water on her face, drank water, exchanged her contacts for her oversized glasses, and fixed her ponytail from where she had continuously run her hands through her hair. She would not go outside looking like she was falling apart, even if she was.

She strolled confidently into the backyard, her heart quite possibly growing a size at the sight of Amelia giggling with her hand on Wanda’s belly.

“¿Qué te parece tan gracioso, hija?” 

The little girl’s head snapped up, eyes wide in shock. “¡Mamá! ¡Hablas español!”

She smirked, exchanging glances with the other two women. “Yo hablo muchos idiomas y tus hermanas también.” Ruffling Amelia’s hair, she smiled affectionately. “Something kicking your hand?”

The smallest witch nodded in awe. “My niece!” She looked back at the door as her Mama appeared, before turning back to Agatha, chewing on her lip. “When can I meet everyone else?”

Rio interjected before Agatha could form a reply. “Cariño, I’m not sure when a good time will be; we don’t want to impose.”

“Nonsense, this weekend, I’ll call everyone in.” She watched the doubt and worry flicker in her daughter’s eyes. “None of this is your fault, I know that’s what you're thinking. Your mama has never had good timing, but yours is perfect.” She picked Amelia up easily, swinging her in the air before placing her on her hip and carrying her inside. “Now, I ordered pizza, and it should be here any minute.”

She shot her ex a look as she passed by, daring her to deny them a pizza night.

Rio sighed and followed behind.

When does Darcy get in? Wanda chose to use their link, looping Lilith in on the conversation.

Darcy’s coming? Shit. I didn’t say a word to Rio about her all summer. Lily closed the sliding glass door behind her, heading off to get drinks for everyone.

Yes. She should be here around eight. It’s been a very emotional day. I’d rather lean on my girlfriend and not revert to old habits. Growth. She set Amelia on the counter and tried to guess her favorite juice.    

“I bet it’s pineapple.” 

The little girl gasped, nodding. “How did you know?”

“Because it’s one of my favorites.”

Rio grunted. Agatha shot her a look.

Agatha noticed Rio’s silence as they ate, only speaking when directly addressed. Amelia led most of it, asking everyone questions about what they liked or certain memories.

A very perceptive four-year-old. 

All of Agatha’s children had been ahead of their peers, different, well-spoken. They had all been witches or super-powered. Amelia surpassed them, which shouldn’t have surprised the mother of five. This child was part cosmic being. Yes, Wanda was too, but she hadn’t stepped into that role until she was an adult.

Amelia was born into it. She already had magick and power. Like her mother and other siblings, she was highly intelligent. She also saw things, watched, and felt people.

Like Rio.

After dinner, Wanda excused herself to portal back to New York, knowing Zora was not going to be happy with her. Lily also had to get back to Madrid, promising she’d be back as soon as she finished her last final. 

They sat on the couch, music playing in the background, Amelia curled next to Agatha, head in her lap. Agatha’s legs were propped on the coffee table, her hand running through the girl’s hair. 

“Mommy?” Amelia’s voice was heavy with the type of exhaustion that comes after an exciting day.

“Yes, Milly?”

“Can I have my own room here?” She yawned, snuggling deeper against Agatha’s lap.

Agatha’s breath hitched at the question, refusing to look at Rio. She would not be asking for permission. “Of course. You can decorate it however you want.”

“Good.” She stretched, eyelids fluttering closed.

Rio’s eyes narrowed. “Agatha,” she hissed. “That’s a discussion.”

She nodded. “Yes, it is. Not right now.”

Rio stood, looking at the time. “I need to get her home.”

Agatha knew it was coming, but she knew there was nothing she could do about it yet. “Who’s watching her tomorrow?”

“Lilia.”

“Well, she partially raised me, and helped with the other kids, so I approve.” She rose, gathering the child in her arms. “Let me help you to the car.”

Rio reluctantly led Agatha to the vehicle, knowing this was Agatha’s way of approving everything. “You don’t get a say, Agatha.”

“Oh yes, I do. We’re doing this together, Rio. We may not be together, but she's our daughter.” She looked over the new Rav4 in her driveway and the booster seat with a five-point harness installed correctly in the backseat. “You looked up the carseat laws, I see.”

“I’m not stupid, Agatha. I even weighed her and measured her height.”

“Not with this, at least.”

Their heads turned when headlights washed over them, a young woman being let out of an Explorer. Her bags were taken into the house as she walked over to the two women, Amelia still in her arms.

Darcy Lewis was thirty, the same height as Agatha, with striking blue eyes and hair so dark it was nearly black, much like Rio’s.

“Dr. Vidal?” Darcy asked once she saw the paleontologist. “I haven’t seen you since that night on Isla Dunblar.” Her eyes flicked to Agatha and the child in her arms.

“Dr. Lewis. Odd to see you here. So late at night. On a Monday. Far from Jurassic Park.” Death deadpanned, her eyes hardening.

“Darcy works for Harkness Holdings now. She’s in charge of all things tech and IT. Since I’m still technically involved in the company, we have business matters to attend to.” Agatha explained.

Darcy nodded. “With Dr. H returning to academic life, I have to catch her when I can.” Her eyes stayed on Amelia. “I didn’t know you had a child, Dr. V.” 

“Nor did I. Until today.” Agatha quickly explained, knowing Darcy had already figured it out. Her girlfriend’s eyes softened, full of understanding.

“I’ll let you two finish up. I’m sure I’ll see you soon, Dr. V. Have a lovely night.” She nodded toward Amelia. “She’s beautiful, looks just like her mother.” Darcy turned and walked back to the house, the driver having already left. 

Agatha carefully placed Amelia in her seat and buckled her. The girl woke just slightly. “Love you, mommy.” She slurred sleepily.

She leaned in and planted a kiss on the head. “I love you, too. I’ll see you soon.” Exiting the car, she closed the door, turning to face Rio and the fury in her eyes.

“Darcy didn’t mean me.”

“She did not,” Agatha confirmed.

“How long have you been fucking?” Rio growled.

A harsh laugh ripped from Agatha’s throat. “I am ONLY answering because she will be a part of Amelia’s life. We have been together for six months, and you have no right to be jealous. Good night, Rio. We’ll discuss custody arrangements later.”

 

She stepped back into her house, closing and locking the door behind her, half expecting Darcy to greet her. She looked around, realizing she had walked into a darkened house.

Padding into the bedroom, she heard the water running from the bathroom. Although she had already showered, a relaxing bath was needed after the emotional turmoil of the afternoon. A bath with her girlfriend sounded even better. She stripped as she walked into the oversized spa-like bathroom, tossing her clothes in the hamper. Darcy sat on the side of the tub, already naked. 

“She never told you?” Darcy looked at her in shock.

All of the conversations, emotions, and events from the previous nine hours crashed into her as sobs racked her body. She rubbed the ugly skull-shaped scar over her heart, Darcy wrapping her arms around her and leading them into the hot water. 

Darcy carefully washed the older woman as she recounted everything from the day. Finding out, the fight, rewinding time, meeting Amelia, the second fight. She washed herself, dried them both off, and helped Agatha put on her pajamas.

By the time Agatha had told her everything, they were in bed, Agatha in Darcy’s arms.

“I was doing so much better, Darcy. I had healed, I had moved on.” 



Darcy smoothed Agatha’s hair, remembering the dark years when Agatha had been barely surviving. “You’re still healed, you’ve still moved on. You just allowed me to take care of you; you’re in my arms. You admitted that you needed me. That’s huge, Aggie. That’s healthy. It’s okay to revert a little. Anyone would in this position.”

“I’m not used to this, you know?” 

Darcy nodded. “I know. You only leaned on a few people, and rarely a romantic partner. Sattler, but that is completely different.”

“So this is what a healthy relationship is like? You’re not upset with me?” 

Darcy kissed her lightly and brushed a tear away. “I’m jealous she had your baby, and I am pissed at her, not you. But yes, this is a healthy relationship.”

She pulled the older woman close to her, tracing circles down her back. Darcy wasn’t angry at Agatha. It wasn’t her fault.

No, Darcy was worried about her relationship. She knew everything, the history, the severed bond. Was Agatha still in love with Death? She couldn’t compete with that. She would have to graciously step aside in that case. 

Agatha hadn’t told Darcy she loved her, but Darcy had said it to Agatha, had told her there was no pressure. There wasn’t, she knew how badly Agatha had been hurt. But now?

Now Agatha’s fucking soulmate was back with their kid, and that changed everything.  

🦕🦖🦕

Rio had woken Amelia enough to change her into pajamas and go through her bedtime routine. Now that she was back asleep in the canopy bed that she and Lily had picked out, Rio sat on her back porch, contemplating her life choices.

“Shouldn’t you be at one of your homes with your wife?” She asked as Lilia sat down in the chair next to her. 

“She’s already asleep. I’ll spare you the details. She’s always known about Amelia, but since I was able to confirm it, well.” Lilia took a sip of the tea she had made in Rio’s kitchen. 

“So happy for you. All of Milly’s blankets, clothes, hats…everything that Sharon’s made over the years? Yeah, she’s known.” 

“You’ve loved it.” The grey-haired witch smiled. “I take it you met Darcy.”

“Yep.” She popped the p.

“Did you think she’d wait around after you severed the bond?”

Letting out a deep sigh, she took a sip of the beer she had opened. She couldn’t get drunk, but she liked the taste. “I don’t know what I thought, Lilia. I didn’t know if I'd ever be coming back to her. It just feels so wrong.”

Her longest mortal friend nodded. “There’s your black and white thinking.”

“What do you mean?” 

“You’re soulmates, destined to be together. So, to you, that means she should only be with you.” She explained.

Death nodded. “Yes.”

“But you ended it. Agatha was not well for a very long time. Darcy came along, and they’re a good fit. Darcy’s been good for her. Even Ellie agrees.”

“Ellie Sattler. Agatha’s first love. Is okay with this?” 

Lilia nodded. “A lot of things happened over five years, but yes. Ellie and Darcy get along really well. Ralph and Ian? Adore her.”

“She’s younger than Pietro and Wanda!” Rio exclaimed.

“And they tease Agatha about it, but it’s out of love.” She explained. “You fucked up, Rio.”

Rio stood, leaning against the porch railing. “I gave Agatha special treatment. She’s always gotten by with so much. Ascending ranks, doing whatever she wants without consequence.”

Lilia raised an eyebrow. “She’s always had consequences. Not being with you, losing you, hell, not knowing about her daughter. She survives by any means necessary. She didn’t like what she was given, so she changed it. Just like an entity that I know.”

“That’s different,” Rio whispered, knowing that it really wasn’t.

“You didn’t want to be Death, so you put someone else in charge. She didn’t want to follow in her mother’s footsteps. She wanted a family, she wanted to be who she wanted to be.”

“She can’t just choose who can be immortal, Lilia.”

“She hasn’t. She hasn’t brought herself to do it.”

Rio looked down at her clasped hands. “Oh. I, uh, hadn’t paid attention to that.”

“No, you’ve been too focused on perfect balance. You used to have mercy, allowing people to live longer and escape death. This is risky, absolutes mess with time, that’s Eternity’s realm, Rio. She’s focused her sights somewhere else now, and it’s on you.” Lilia warned.

“She’s still in Asgard, she’s not getting out.”

Lilia hummed in disagreement.

“Those runes can stop working if time becomes resolute, Rio.”

“I’m going to bed, Lilia. I’ll see you in the morning.  

🦕🦖🦕

The week had gone by quickly. While Agatha was busy preparing for the upcoming semester, she made sure to spend as much time with Amelia as possible. She also introduced Amelia to Darcy. 

Amelia was not amused.

It was Thursday evening when it happened, Agatha convincing Darcy that it needed to happen sooner rather than later. “Baby, my other kids, and the grandkids, love you.”

Darcy looked at her, unconvinced. “She just met you, and she’s four. She wants her moms together.”

Agatha frowned, knowing her girlfriend was most likely right. “Even more reason to do it now, more time for her to get to know you.”

Darcy had conceded, only to be proven right.

 

Amelia looked Darcy up and down, sizing up the enemy. “How old are you?” She asked.

“I’m thirty.”

“You’re younger than Pietro and Wanda, but older than Nicky and Lily.” She considered the information for a moment. “Is Mommy adopting you?”

Agatha almost spit out the water she had just taken a drink of.

Rio swallowed a laugh. 

“No, sweetie.” Agatha corrected. “Darcy is my girlfriend. We’re dating.”

Amelia scrunched up her nose. The look from both of her moms, telling her not to argue. This was not how she wanted this to go. She stabbed her broccoli, her lower lip jutting out. “Are you a witch?”

Darcy shook her head. “Not like your moms or siblings, no. I can do a lot of things with technology and science, and I know a few analog spells.”

Amelia raised her eyebrows, still working on raising them separately. “Really?”

The woman who, in Amelia’s four-year-old opinion, was too young for her mother nodded. “It’s not much, but it’s something. I’ve also picked up things from your family over the years. I used to work around the dinosaurs.”

The young girl tried very hard not to sound excited. “Oh. So what’s your favorite?” 

Darcy leaned back in her chair, tapping her cheek with her fingertip. “Well, this might surprise you, but it’s a Troodon because they were most likely the most intelligent. They watch things and notice patterns, which is what a lot of science is.”

Amelia gasped. “That’s MY favorite dinosaur.” She quickly remembered that Darcy was trying to steal Agatha away from her Mama and returned to her pout. “I mean, cool.”

Darcy gave her a solemn nod. “Cool, indeed.”



Agatha and Rio exchanged looks at the shared dinosaur choice, Agatha groaning eternally. That damn dinosaur would haunt her for her entire existence. She considered the ethics of wiping out the species again.

Watching Darcy carefully attempt to form a bond with Amelia warmed her heart. They both knew it would be difficult, but it was necessary. 

Agatha had no intentions of reuniting with Death or breaking up with Darcy. It was important for this connection to form and strengthen. 

“Rio, help me clean up,” Agatha ordered, leaving no room for argument. “Milly, baby, why don’t you show Darcy the new dinosaur Lego we got for your room? She’s really good at them.”

 

Amelia stood, trying to sound indifferent. “It’s a T. Rex and has a gazillion pieces. You can try to help if you want.” She stood, leading the way to her room upstairs.

 

Death picked up a plate, staring at Agatha. “What the fuck are you doing, Ags?”

“Darcy is a large part of my life, and so is Amelia, now. I want them to bond.” Shrugging, she went about clearing the table as normal.

The entity huffed, moving around the witch as though they had done this for years. “No plans to break up?”

Agatha straightened, refusing to look at the woman who had hurt her so deeply. “I can’t believe you. You waltz in here with a child you hid from me, and what? Expect me to just fall back into your arms?”

“We’re dest—”

“Cut the bullshit, Lady Death.” The witch slammed the dishwasher closed and stormed toward the office, not wanting to argue the open. 

 

She closed the door behind Rio, once more sealing the room off. “If you cared so much about that, you wouldn’t have cut it off.” She turned, leveling a glare at the other woman. “What is your problem?”

“You act like such a child with your powers, Agatha! That’s my problem! You can’t just run around, doing what you want without checks and balances!” Rio shouted. “Do you not realize there are consequences?”

Agatha ran a hand roughly through her hair. “Is that what you think I’ve been doing? It’s why I siphon the way I do. It’s why I’ve been working so hard to balance out your need for perfection! I know there are consequences, Rio! It’s why my fingers are nearly completely ink-stained if they’re not glamored! We over-corrected when we restored your magick, and it’s my fault! Cancer treatments are no longer working, people aren’t surviving near-death experiences, and it’s MY fault, Rio. You refuse to give people time, and it’s MY FAULT. 

“Your need for a perfect balance? You not being with me? That’s on me, and I have to live with that. I have to fix it. So do not come into my house, thinking you’re going to win me back after I’ve come to terms with what I’ve done. After I’ve moved on. After I found love again, Lady Death. And DO NOT even try to use MY child against me.”

The entity looked away, stunned. “The research, the island…”

“I told you, I needed the plants, the DNA. We’re still growing the ancient plants we need for specific medications. An ancient strain of ginkgo that does more than the modern strain. Also? What better way to lure you in than an island full of dinosaurs, Rio? No, they weren’t bait. I really do need them. The research opportunities have been astronomical.”

“You really are a supervillain, aren’t you?” Rio raised an eyebrow.

“Sometimes. There would be even more benefits if someone would allow there to be.” Agatha took a long, hard look at Death.

“People are supposed to die when their time is up.”

“Oh, spare me your bullshit, Rio. You once believed in mercy, the extension of life. It’s why medications worked, why people walked away from car accidents, survived heart attacks, and why none of us died that night. You’ve gotten worse instead of better!”

Rio looked away, focusing on a picture from Wanda and Zora’s wedding. “Why didn’t you just ask me? All those years ago?”

Agatha’s lips parted in a faint gasp. “What?”

“When we first met, in London. You knew who I was, what we were to each other. Why didn’t you ask for my help?”

The witch scoffed. “You were trying so hard to be human, ignoring the connection between us. I only had analog Magick at the time. Would you have believed me? Would you have helped me? You wouldn’t even let me explain the situation when Ellie told you I was married. Then, in Oxford, would you have listened to or believed me then? Again, you didn’t let me explain. You took Eternity’s word for it against mine.”

Lady Death kept her eyes focused on the picture before her, eyes stinging, lips pressed together, saying nothing.

“Exactly,” Agatha answered. “I may have lied, omitted, hidden things. But I never once pretended that I didn’t love you, Rio. That was real, once.” She turned toward the door. “Now, I am going to join my girlfriend and daughter. I would like Amelia to stay here this weekend, since the family will be here. It will be good for her to spend more time around them.”

Rio cleared her throat. “You’ll have to ask Amelia. Will Darcy be here?”

Agatha’s eyes flicked toward the ceiling. “It’s a family weekend, Rio. Yes. She returns to New York on Sunday.”

“Oh.”

“You missed your chance.” Agatha turned the doorknob to exit the room.

Rio turned quickly. “She can’t make you feel the way I can.”

Agatha laughed. It was harsh and full-bodied all at once. “Yeah, that’s kinda the point.” She leaned her forehead against the door. “I never want to feel like that again, Rio. A walking husk? Watching myself going through the motions? Needing people to literally hold me together? No. She can’t make me feel like that, nor would she.” 

Stepping out into the hallway, she wiped the tears from her waterline before setting off to Amelia’s room.     

🦕🦖🦕

Rio Vidal once again sat on her porch, Amelia asleep inside. She enjoyed the late-night Boston air, even if her shirt was almost threadbare.

The shirt Agatha had left her all those years ago, spelled so that it still smelled like her. 

“How’d tonight go?” Lilia asked, taking her seat.

“She loves her.” The supposedly all-knowing entity said solemnly. “Agatha is in love with Darcy.”

Lilia nodded. “Of course she is. She hasn’t told her yet, though I’m sure she will soon. She’d be insane not to.”

“Not helping, you old bat.”

“You’re much older than I am, you fossil.” Lilia almost laughed before turning serious. “What did Milly think of her?”

“She’s conflicted. Wants to despise her, but they have a lot in common.” She grumbled.

“Are you ready for this weekend? It’s going to be a lot of people.” The Sicilian looked at her in concern.

“I think so? We’ll just see how it goes, I guess.” Rio chewed on the inside of her cheek. She had never been nervous around Agatha’s family before, and she would finally see Alice again. Though from what she had gathered in the past few days, they had done a lot to help Agatha after Isla Dunblar.

She did not expect a warm welcome, but maybe she could enlist someone’s help to win Agatha back.

Her Agatha. Her witch. The mother of her child. 

She absentmindedly rubbed the scar on her chest, remembering the pain she had caused herself that night.

“Milly is staying with Aggie this weekend.” She whispered.

A grey eyebrow raised.

“I just, I miss her, Lilia. Seeing her again? Seeing her with the kids? With Amelia? Neither of us is innocent in this.”

“You can’t force Agatha Harkness to do anything, Rio, and you know that. If you try to push anything, she’ll push back harder.”

Rio put her head in her hands and groaned. “I don’t know what to do.”

“No one ever does, muerta.”



Agatha climbed into her bed, straddled Darcy, and removed the book from her hands, placing it on the nightstand. She wrapped her arms around her neck and searched her eyes for any hesitation. Finding none, she leaned forward and kissed her softly. “Thank you.” She said softly between kisses.

Darcy leaned back, gazing into ocean blue eyes. “For what, Ags?”

“For being so great with Amelia. For not running. For taking this all in stride.” She planted open-mouth kisses along her jawline, hands carding through the younger woman’s hair.

Darcy gripped Agatha’s hips, holding her firmly in place. “I will stand with you no matter what, Agatha. We’re in this together. So what if your ex is Death and your daughter is part cosmic entity? We’ll acclimate together.” She gasped when Agatha bit down on her pulse point.

The witch leaned back, eyes scanning her girlfriend’s features. “You really mean it?”

The younger scientist cupped Agatha’s cheek. “Neither of us is perfect, Agatha, but we will adjust, no matter what that looks like. If your scar starts hurting again? We’ll figure out what that means. You’re not alone in this, and it’s not just me. If you want me to go home while Amelia is here this weekend, I can. I will do whatever you need.”

Agatha’s heart swelled for the first time in years. Yes, she knew she loved Darcy, but this? This was real. Not because the universe said so, but because they had built it from scratch.

She leaned her forehead against Darcy’s. “I love you. So fucking much.”

Darcy cupped the back of Agatha’s neck. “I love you, too.”

The witch kissed her again, needier, more passionate, as if sealing the words. “Now let me show you how much.” She breathed, sliding her hands under the shirt her girlfriend was wearing.  

🦕🦖🦕

Eternity’s Realm

 

The runes for the Asgardian dungeons had stopped working, just as she had both hoped and feared. Time was no longer endless, thanks to her sister and Agatha Harkness.

Eternity had simply left, teleported to her realm, and found out quite a bit of information.

The Sacred Balance had become too balanced. Timelines were no longer overlapping long enough to evolve; entire generations would never exist due to Death’s finality.

Eternity no longer wanted to absorb Rio’s powers and rule over a few planets. No, she had to preserve time. She couldn’t let any of the other cosmic beings do what Rio was doing. 

Then, there was the anomaly.

Amelia.

Amelia was the exception. A cosmic child, something none of them had ever seen before. She could rewrite all of the rules.

Cosmic beings weren’t supposed to have soulmates, and they certainly weren’t supposed to have children with humans, no matter how powerful they were. 

The Scarlet Witch was an exception. She stepped into her role. The gods, of course, had always procreated.

But the concepts? The major entities?

They were not supposed to procreate with humans. Who knew death could give birth to a living child?

No. Amelia was quite possibly the most dangerous being in the universe, and that was why Eternity was going to take her.

 

Chapter End Notes

So, how are we feeling?

Come yell at me on tumblr, submit asks, etc.

Agatha Harkness, are you actually taking responsibility for your actions? Who is your therapist? Because damn.

Will Agatha and Rio end up together? A second kidnapping?

I feel like the family will be giving Rio some hell.

How will they fix the whole damn universe?

Chapter 6: You’re Neither My Friend Nor My Foe (But I Can’t Seem To Let You Go)

Chapter Summary

It's time for the entire family to meet and bond with Amelia, and to mend things with Rio. Old grudges are put to rest, and the focus is on what really matters.

Amelia.

Yet the Balance is still too perfect, Eternity is still looming, Darcy is still in the picture, and our two main women still have things left unsaid.

Chapter Notes

Hannah is amazing, and we love her.

I had to split this chapter, so we will now have 8 chapters instead of 7.

TRUST THE PROCESS. TRUST IT.

Yes, people are changing drastically, because that's what happens in these situations.

Come yell at me about it on tumblr.

See the end of the chapter for more notes

Agatha Harkness hadn’t had a nightmare in months. She always slept peacefully with Darcy in her arms. They had been exhausted when they finally gave in to sleep in the wee hours of the morning. Agatha was sure that her alarm would have to wake them.

Yet here she was, fully awake at five thirty in the morning, wrapped in a floral robe at the kitchen table, drinking coffee and eating cheesecake. She had woken in a cold sweat, hair matted and stuck to her face. She had been back in that clearing, screaming, “Why can’t I feel you?” at Rio. Her scar burned with phantom pain, and her heart had been racing.

Darcy, her sweet, beautiful Darcy, had stirred slightly. Agatha kissed her on the forehead and told her to go back to sleep, that she’d be back to bed in a few minutes.

An hour had passed, she was on her second cup of coffee, the entire cheesecake before her, and the shaking had just subsided.

Her family was coming in today. Neither she nor Rio would be going to campus, with it being a Friday and still summer. The New York group would be flying in around noon on the larger jet, and the Yale group would drive in mid-morning. She had missed her family and coven, and was excited for them to meet Amelia.

She also knew they were upset with Rio. It wasn’t that they didn’t like her. Rio Vidal had hurt Agatha, and they were protective. But Lilia and Jen had known, Sharon and Alice had unofficially known, and then Lily had been with Rio all summer.

Between Rio, Lilia, and Agatha, they had plenty of room to house everyone unless someone wanted to stay elsewhere. It was the main reason Agatha bought the house she had. She loved when it was loud, full of love and family.

And now, there was another child, soon another baby. Pietro and Nicky had girlfriends now, but were leaving them back in the city, trying not to overwhelm Amelia too much.

The little girl had already been through a lot. Moving to a new country, meeting a few members of her family, her Mom, and the woman who that Mom was dating.

Agatha was overwhelmed herself: new city, new position, new home, new child, old flame.

Seeing Rio on Monday had crushed her, not because of Amelia, but because Agatha realized that after all this time, she still missed her. Every time they had argued during the week, she spoke in the past tense with Rio. Loved. Wanted. 

There was a piece of her that still longed for Death. Wanted to forgive her, apologize for all of her wrongdoings, try again.

She told herself it was a very small piece—the lone survivor from the years she spent in her own personal hell.

No. She had Darcy now. She smiled at the thought of the brunette upstairs tangled in wine-colored sheets. The woman who loved every part of her, even the wicked ones. The woman who had listened to her entire story and said, “damn, and I thought I had some scary exes”.

The witch sighed, draining her coffee and setting it on the table with a loud thud. Her youngest daughter would be staying with her that night. She was looking forward to it, hoping she had everything Amelia needed. Making a mental note to take the little one shopping, or, better yet, to have Ralph and Ian take her. Let her bond with the men who already considered her their fifth child. She stood, returning what was left of the cheesecake to the fridge and rinsing her mug and fork in the sink.

Toned arms wrapped around her waist, Darcy lay her chin on a silk-covered shoulder.

 

“You had nightmares.” A statement, not a question. 

She turned to face the younger woman, draping her arms over her shoulders. “I did.”

Placing a hand over the covered scar, Darcy furrowed her brows. “Why didn’t you wake me? You know you can always talk to me.”

“I know, it’s just,” she pulled her bottom lip in between her teeth, indents showing when she released it. “I didn’t want to worry you.”

Darcy used her thumb to smooth over Agatha’s lip. “That’s part of falling for Agatha Harkness, I fear. I’m going to worry, but it’s worse when you don’t talk to me.” She leaned forward, capturing her lips in a soft kiss. 

Agatha nodded, holding Darcy close. That had always been her problem, hadn’t it? Not talking. Not trusting. Not listening.

This was not the time for reflection. 

Instead, she tugged on the tie of Darcy’s robe, causing it to fall open, and smiled wickedly.
