It's another emotional rollercoaster, and the next chapter will be the (much shorter) epilogue.

Thank you, Hannah (Hannah-0730/CrystalsandLavender), for helping me make sure this was the right mix.

See the end of the chapter for more notes

November 23, 2025

 

“Well, it’s about fucking time.” Lilia sipped her tea as she regarded Agatha. Amelia was upstairs on Sunday morning, learning how to knit Magick into blankets with Sharon.

“I want to go about it correctly, so she knows I’m serious and not just interested in sex.” 

Lilia tutted. “You have a child together, Agatha. You could ask her to move in and marry you as soon as she returns from her realm, and she would.”

“I want to actually build a foundation this time, you kooky old bat.” Agatha partially scolded.

“The two of you are cosmically connected. I do, however, understand. I was terrified when I courted Sharon. It’s natural.”

“Did you say courted?” Agatha’s lips twitched at the corners from hearing the outdated word.

“Yes, I did, you infant.” Mahogany eyes glazed over as she slipped into a memory. “It was the early 1800s, here in Boston. She was barely 20, I passed for thirty-five.”

“Are you sure you’re not mixing up thirty-five with three hundred and fifty?” 

“Shut up and listen, you ungrateful brat.” Lilia scolded. “She came from a good family for the era. My story was believable—I owned an inn and tavern at the time that I claimed I inherited from my husband when he died in the Battle of Waterloo or some other dreadful skirmish.”

“You influenced that ABBA song, didn’t you?” the younger witch interrupted. 

Lilia leveled a glare at her. “Anyway, this young maiden would sneak into the tavern unchaperoned. It didn’t take me long to gather she was a witch. I could sense her Magick, not that I cared. That’s not what drew me to her. She was interesting, intelligent, well spoken, well read, gorgeous.” The older woman sighed, a warm smile on her face.  

“Are you telling Agatha about us?” Sharon asked, entering the kitchen, Amelia on her heels. 

“Yes, dear. I don’t believe she’s heard the full story, and there’s no such thing as too much love.” Her smile widened when she realized what her wife and granddaughter were up to. “Are you helping Mimi make cookies?” 

The young girl nodded from the stool she was now sitting on, shoving a handful of butterscotch chips in her mouth. “For Thursday,” she mumbled around the stolen ingredient.

Agatha’s lips curled upward at her youngest’s antics before turning back to the two older women. 

“As I was saying,” Lilia continued, eyes on the two bakers. “I knew I wanted to pursue her, though she had many male suitors at the time.

“None of whom I was interested in. Not really. There was one man I paid some attention to, but he was oddly trampled by the most mild-mannered horse in town.” Sharon smirked playfully at her wife.

“Horses are very easily spooked. What can I say?” 

Amelia gasped. “You killed somebody for Mimi!”

“The horse did, Plumita. It was a terrible accident.” Agatha added, holding back a laugh. “Do not give her any ideas.” She whispered to her surrogate mother.

“Your Mama wasn’t very happy with her,” Sharon added, handing her an egg to crack into the bowl.

“You knew Mama back then?” Milly questioned, focusing on her task.

“I sure did. We go back a very long time, back to my story.” She sipped the cooling tea in front of her. “Women didn’t really court each other back then. There were ways, but it was very subtle. I, like Agatha, am not a subtle person.”

“You don’t say.” Rio appeared, fresh from showering after spending several days in her realm. “Sorry for interrupting,” she quickly added, taking a seat on a stool next to her daughter after being silently scolded.

“As I was saying, I wanted to make sure Sharon knew my intentions. So I asked your Mama here to take letters to her.”

“I’ve kept every single one, some of them were very scandalous,” the other Mrs. Calderu informed Amelia while they put the cookies on the well-seasoned pan.

Amelia scrunched her face in disgust. “Eww, Mimi.”

Lilia’s laugh filled the room. “One day, Amelia. Of course, when I approached her father, he said no. So, Rio stepped in.”

Agatha whipped around to face Death. “How did you help?”

Rio’s ears reddened as she averted her eyes from all of them. It was one of the times throughout her endless life when she had crossed the line. She hadn’t broken the Sacred Balance; she had just used her role to benefit her friends. It wasn’t completely forbidden. 

“I appeared to Sharon’s father in my natural form and issued an unfriendly ultimatum. Something about dying in three days if he didn’t give Lilia his blessing. He bestowed it the next day.” Turning to her daughter, she attempted to be serious. “We do not use our powers to influence humans. It was an emergency.”

“Sure, Mama.” Amelia held back her grin.

 

Agatha exchanged a glance and a nearly imperceptible nod with Lilia. She had originally wondered why the old bat was sharing the story, and now it was apparent. Yes, love, but also, Rio was a hopeless romantic. Who knew? Agatha stored the new information away, knowing it would be useful.

Rio Vidal, Lady Death, used her cosmic role to help Lilia get her woman. Yes, it had been a different time, and Rio hadn’t been doing everything perfectly. However, surely, they could work something out even if Rio was set on being Death part-time.

It was the week of Thanksgiving. Amelia would be back and forth between houses while they prepared for the holiday and as the family arrived. Lily and Nicky had been there on Saturday but were back in New York for a few days. 

Agatha knew the holidays would be a rough time to start trying to win Rio back, but she wasn’t going to wait any longer. Lilia had been right when she said she could just ask Rio to marry her, and she would, but Rio deserved so much better than that.

Agatha Harkness had serious work to do.

🦕🦖🦕

Transporting into the middle of Lilia and Sharon’s storytime had brought back many memories for Rio. She remembered playing messenger for the two of them, delivering love letters back and forth and telling the frightened human that he would give Lilia his blessing to marry Sharon. 

Rio had blurred lines, and it had thrilled her. A rebellion, one she would gladly do again for her friends.

A romantic then, never having been in love herself, she enjoyed playing her part and bucking the system. It was one of the times she had felt the most human. 

Now, having been in love, experienced heartbreak, and fallen in love again, she found it excruciatingly painful.

She wouldn’t trade it for the world.

Love was complicated, especially when your soulmate was someone like Agatha Harkness. Rio thought about their relationship a lot, and while she wished she could move on, she found herself unable to. Now, they were in a place where they could potentially be together, and were choosing not to be.

For the right reasons, yes. No matter how much they both wanted it. 

The night they had spent together had reawakened something in Rio, but Alice was right. Returning to her duties as Death had been good for her, and she did have less time to dwell and focus on the distraction that was Agatha. 

She deserved someone open with their feelings. Who put her first, or as first as she could. Rio understood that they would always put family first. They needed to prioritize each other, and until they could do that?

It wouldn’t work.

She watched as Amelia giggled and listened intently to Sharon’s instructions for cookies. Flour across her cheek, sneaking unbaked cookie dough. 

These were the moments she had wanted for her daughter. She and Agatha had found a way to work it out amicably, share the holidays, and provide familial experiences for their daughter.

It still felt like her heart wasn’t quite whole. Chancing a look at Agatha, she confirmed that those mesmerizing blue eyes were already on her, barely listening to whatever Lilia was talking about now. 

It was going to be an exceedingly long immortality.    

🦕🦖🦕

On Wednesday, the day before Thanksgiving, the Harkness household was buzzing with activity.

“How many pies are you baking, Angel?” Agatha asked the redhead, surveying her kitchen.

“Everyone likes a different pie, and before you ask, I have it under control,” She replied, Gabby in the crook of one arm.

“As much as I love you, Mom,” Pietro interrupted, “You’re a cook, not a baker.” He kissed her on the cheek as he mixed ingredients for his twin.

“Here, I’ll take the baby.” Rio offered, scooping the infant out of Wanda’s hold and onto her shoulder, patting her back. “Now you have two hands. I know you prefer to bake without Magick.”

“You’re a lifesaver, thank you!” The Scarlet Witch looked around, “Where’s my wife?” 

Lily power-walked through the kitchen, snatching a jar of cherries and handed it to the giggling child on her back. “Phone call with someone in another country. Nicky and Aunt Jen are with her.”

“Lilith! Those are for tomorrow!” Ian exclaimed.

“But I want cherries,” Amelia pouted.

“Princesa can have whatever she wants. We have more.” Ralph interjected.

“Good thing you have a giant kitchen.” Ellie bumped Agatha’s hip as she watched the chaos. 

The witch hummed in agreement. “Where’s your girlfriend?” 

With a roll of her eyes, Ellie typed something on her phone. “Said she didn’t want to make it weird this year.”

“I really am over it, and I’m happy for you. Sorry you missed your forbidden professor/student arc by ten years.” Agatha’s eyes stayed on Rio as she walked around the space with Gabby.

“Be honest, you never moved past Rio.” Ellie glanced at Agatha from the side and then over to the woman in question. “She’s the only person you’ve ever looked at that way.”

She blinked, head swiveling to the blonde. “What way?”

“Like you’d risk it all. Set the planet on fire. Destroy every single universe if anyone got in your way, just to spend an hour with her.”  Wrapping an arm around her waist, she pulled her close and whispered, “And that’s when she isn’t holding a baby.”

“You talk to Ralph?” Agatha ignored the commentary.

“Of course. We’ve all placed our bets and are available to entity-sit.”

Agatha drew her bottom lip between her teeth. This was something they wanted. They had admitted that a few weeks ago. If clear boundaries were set, communication established, and they took it slowly, surely it would go right.

Standing there, watching Rio with Gabby, getting a glimpse of what she had missed out on, tore at her. Agatha was fascinated by the way the woman who led mortals to their afterlife, including children, soothed the infant and spoke to her sweetly in a mix of languages. The hands that reaped souls from their bodies gently cradled the newborn as though she hadn’t nearly taken that life away from all of them a few months prior.

It was a part of Rio’s job that Agatha couldn’t quite accept. The part of Rio that walked kids to the other realm. It was one of the reasons Agatha had been so adamant in making her family, especially her offspring, biological and adopted, immortal.

A mother should never live without her children, and she had made sure she and her family wouldn’t experience that kind of separation and grief. 

Memories from the night she had walked into Amelia’s memories flashed in front of her. Rio held Milly to her chest, crying silently, singing to her in Spanish, telling her stories—memories they should share. 

“She was, and is, an amazing mother, Agatha,” Lilia said softly from behind her.

“I know. Amelia’s proof of that. It’s just,” she took in the busy kitchen before walking towards the hidden library, knowing her office was in use. 

Once inside, she collapsed onto an oversized chair. “The kids have such large age gaps, and yes, I know the significance of the numbers. No need to say it.”

“I’m going to anyway,” Lilia warned. “Multiples of four, especially sixteen. Balance, my dear girl. You’ve been surrounded by it your whole life. Marry her before you get her pregnant again, please.”

“What makes you think we’ll have more?” She questioned, not looking up from her chair. 

“I know things, dear. It’s what I do. Sharon and I will be babysitting this weekend. You are asking Rio out, correct?”

“I thought we would do something as a family. Decorate the houses for Christmas, maybe?” Agatha answered, voice uncertain.

“You can do that during the day, go ask her on a date. Now. To her face.”

 

As instructed, Agatha went in search of Rio and found her seated outside. “Where’s the baby?” She asked, sitting next to her.

“Upstairs, napping.” Rio’s smile was genuine, reaching her eyes. “I sometimes miss when Amelia was that tiny, when I could protect her from everything.

“You’ll have to tell me about it sometime.” Agatha tried to keep her voice even, not wanting her emotions to deceive her.

Death’s hand reached out and took Agatha’s. “I really am sorry. I know you saw Milly’s memories and mine.”

“I did.”

“There were times I fought against myself not to call you, Aggie. I know I should have, I just…”

She squeezed Rio’s hand. “You were doing what you thought was right. I understand, to a degree.” Reaching out with her other hand, she gently lifted Rio’s chin. “Will you go on a date with me this weekend? Friday night? The grandmas have offered to keep Milly.”

The entity pulled away, looking at her skeptically. “Agatha. I thought we weren’t doing this. We have a daughter to think about.”

“We do, and I am. I’m also thinking about us. We owe it to ourselves to try, don’t we? No secrets, no schemes, no stealing powers. An actual, honest attempt.” She had taken Rio’s hands in hers, eyes searching hers.

“Okay, Agatha.” The cosmic being conceded. “We’ll start with ONE date, and go from there. What are we going to tell Milly?”

“That we’re going Christmas shopping?” She suggested questioningly.   

🦕🦖🦕

Thanksgiving Day was well underway in the Harkness household. The parade was over, football was on in the living room, the main and side dishes were being cooked, and Amelia Jade Harkness-Vidal was watching her mothers closely.

She had been informed that on Friday, she would be staying at her Nonna and Mimi’s house while her moms went Christmas shopping.

Amelia was not convinced.

They had been acting differently since Gabby had been born, but she couldn’t put her finger on it. Her mom had stayed in New York to help, and then Mama had returned to her duties as Death. Something was going on, and the junior cosmic being wasn’t sure she liked it.

How was she supposed to get her moms together if one was in another realm half the time? 

Then there was the way they looked at each other. Why couldn’t they just do this themselves? They were giving each other heart eyes constantly, and they didn’t even notice.

While the adults busied themselves with making dinner, her Mama stared at her Mom holding Gabby, as if she had never seen a woman with a baby before, just as her Mom had the previous day. She grabbed Lily by the hand and pulled her upstairs to her bedroom.

“Whoa, Green Goblin, where’s the fire?” Lilith joked.

“Moms are being weird. They love each other, so why aren’t they a couple?” She sat heavily on the edge of her bed and pouted.

Lily took a seat beside her, draping an arm around her shoulders. “Oh, honey. It’s much more complicated than that.” Brushing her little sister’s unruly hair behind her ear, she sighed. “Why does it matter so much to you?”

“Soulmates belong together, Lily.” She stated simply.

“I understand that part, but their history is a little messy. Too messy to explain to someone who isn’t five yet.” She quickly added the last part.

“Can’t they just say they’re sorry?” Her small eyebrows scrunched together.

“It’s not that easy, but they’re doing a lot better.” Smoothing over the girl’s hair, she saw the tears she was holding back. “What else is bothering you?”

“I want us to be a family, live in one house, have younger siblings. Like you did.” Amelia sniffed, though she tried to hide it.

“Ah. There it is.” Floating tissue from across the room and handing one to her sister, she nodded. “Things weren’t perfect. Yes, I had amazing older siblings, but they were much older, like I am. In fact, sixteen years. Just like us. My parents were married and were very close to being a perfect example of a loving marriage, but Mom still left for work a lot, and Dad was at the hospital for very long shifts. And on call a lot.” 

Lily thought back on her childhood, smiling despite the imperfections. “I wouldn’t change anything. Like you, we were surrounded by people who loved us. I always wanted a younger sibling, too, so that I understand.

“I was older when my parents divorced, but I always knew it would happen. Dad moved across the country to be with Papa, which was excruciatingly painful. I hated how far away he was. He had always been in the same house. We visited, but not very often, until I moved out this way for college. A lot of FaceTime calls.” 

Amelia leaned against her sister as Lily continued talking, still running a hand through her hair, “I’m always here if you need to talk about it. But just because you live in different houses, it doesn’t mean you’re not a family. We’re not a traditional family in any sense, but we are one. My dads are your dads. You have two moms who love you so very much and are doing their best.” Wrapping her arms around her sister, she hugged her tightly.

“I am also absolutely here for whatever you’re plotting, but trust me, those two cannot stay away from each other. They’ll come back together sooner or later. I’m always down to give them a push, though.” The older Harkness added conspiratorially. 

“Family is forever.” Milly whispered into her sister’s chest before summoning Sir Cuddles to her arms.

“That’s right.” Lifting her head, she strained to hear one of her dads announce that the food was ready. “Okay, kid, let’s go see how much pie you can eat.”

 

As they sat around the large table, Milly thought back on hearing her grandmothers’ love story the previous day. Maybe her moms needed more reminders of why they fell in love. Or inspiration. 

Taking a forkful of her loaded mashed potatoes, she looked at all the examples of love around the table, including her oldest brother and his new fiancée, and in her sweetest, most innocent voice, announced, “I want to hear how everyone fell in love.”

All eyes turned to the small entity; a few smirks and knowing laughs were scattered throughout.

“¿Por qué estás tan interesado de repente, cariño?” Rio inquired, sitting to Agatha’s left. The witch was, of course, at the head of the table.

“La historia de Nonna y Mimi fue dulce. Quiero escuchar la de todos los demás, mamá.” Milly replied with a shrug, taking another bite of her potatoes.

“I think it’s sweet.” Emma piped up, beaming down the table at her future sister-in-law. “I wouldn’t mind hearing the family history, either.”

“Perge te immergere, et huic familiae non iungeris.” Agatha mumbled under her breath.

“Buď milá, mamička.” Wanda scolded.

Emma looked around the large table. “How many languages do you all speak again?” 

“Some of us more than others, but Rio, Agatha, and Lilia speak roughly all of them,” Jen sipped her wine, amused.

“Including the lost and dead ones,” Alice added.

Growing impatient, Milly cleared her throat. “Love stories, please?”

“Of course, Plumita,” the matriarch smiled at her youngest before turning to her oldest and the young woman beside him. “As the recently engaged, you two first.” 

 

Milly flinched internally. This is not what she wanted to hear. She needed her moms to listen to her aunts, the dads, and even her sister. Not Speedy and Queen Frostine. She listened anyway and pretended to be interested. 

Emma’s face lit up as she turned to Pietro. “It’s simple. He saw me for me.”

That was not what Milly was expecting to hear, and she didn’t understand why her thirty-six-year-old brother’s cheeks were turning pink.

“As you all know, Emma is a brilliant neurosurgeon. We met while working at the hospital back in LA. She’s also a telepath, like a few of you, though she can’t read or affect any of you.”

“You’re all too powerful.” She interjected.

“When I moved to the company, I knew I wanted her on the team. She said she’d only accept if I knew of a place where she could keep her horse.” 

“Even though I essentially moved across the country with him, it was still a few years before we started dating. I did manage to get him to start riding with me. We connected over animals. He told me about the dinosaurs, but I’ll stick to horses. Less likely to get eaten and all.” She smiled at Pietro.

“They only eat the people that harm my children.” Agatha lightly threatened, to which Rio kicked her under the table.

“So I’ve heard.” Emma retorted, not missing a beat. “He finally built up the courage to ask me out two years ago. By then, I already knew he was the one. It was the little things that added up. Coffee in the morning, sitting with me after particularly difficult meetings or formulas not making sense. And of course, the way he makes an extra effort to make time for me, he was clearly raised right.” 

“My parenting methods are outstanding.” The head of the table suppressed a smirk.

“It was a community effort.” Ralph corrected, rolling his eyes at his ex-wife. 

 

Milly was not impressed and turned to her aunts. “How did you fall in love with Aunt Alice?” She asked Jen directly.

Jen shrugged. “Nothing big here, either, mini menace. We met when we were young and connected, and a few years later, we finally gave in. It hasn’t always been easy, though.”

“Someone worked with your Mom and failed to mention it. Then she kept a secret from me.” Alice took Jen’s hand. “She had her reasons, and it’s helped us communicate better.”

Milly scrubbed her hand over her face in exasperation.

 

Wanda looked over at her baby sister. “It was love at first sight for us. Well, trauma bonding, but mostly love at first sight.”

“Really?!” Milly leaned forward, gripping the table.

Agatha groaned from her seat.

Wanda pretended not to hear her. “It was that day on the island. I told her my secret, and Zora accepted it. We spent hours together that day.”

Zora laughed at the memory. “Craziest twenty-four hours of my life. Some of the best, too, if it meant meeting you.” She leaned over and kissed her wife. “I didn’t come back with you immediately. There was so much cleanup. I think I made it to LA a few days later.”

“They were so gross and in love,” Billy added around a mouthful of pie.

“Were? They still are!” Nicky added from across the table.

“Excuse me, mister, did you finish your real food?” Wanda asked her son, just now taking notice.

“Yesh,” Tommy answered around his third piece of dessert.    

Amelia giggled when she looked under the table and saw Victoria and Señor Scratchy in dog form, finishing the twins' plates. 

 

Lily leaned over and whispered into her sister’s ear. “Now those two, just like the old cronies, are one for the storybooks.”

The little girl nodded and looked down the table at Ralph, seated to the right of her mom. “Dad? Papa? What about you?”

“Oh boy,” Agatha said dramatically, standing. “I think I hear Gabby. Everyone, settle in for a story. I’m getting the baby.” 

 

Now with a large slice of chocolate pie in front of her and Gabby in Agatha’s arms, Milly looked expectantly at Ralph and Ian.

The man she called Dad drew everyone’s attention as he began his story. “I have loved two people, very differently, in my life, and while Agatha and I do not love each other romantically, we still fell in love.”

“We trauma-bonded as kids, Ralph.” She inserted with a sarcastic tone.

 

“I am telling this story, you hag.” His reply was nothing but loving. “We were sent to a conversion camp when we were teenagers, Princesa. What that means is our parents weren’t happy with us being gay, and were trying to make us straight.”

“That’s not very nice,” Milly said sternly, eyes swirling green and purple.

“Don’t worry, Plumita, all offending parties have already been dealt with,” Agatha reassured her daughter.

“I forget that places like that existed,” Emma whispered.

“Evanora Harkness was pure evil,” Pietro informed her, his arm around her shoulders. “Dad’s parents were misguided.”

 

“Continuing, we met on day one, scared, but determined. Agatha was creating a spell to hex a handsy counselor, and I was trying to find a place to read uninterrupted. We were fast friends. I was a very puny kid at the time, and her Magick had already been taken from her. She taught me how to draw protection runes, and I taught her how to sew said runes into her clothing.”

Agatha took his hand as the memories came flooding back. “We agreed to pretend to date so we could get out of there, though we ended up burning the place down instead. Very poetic if you ask me.”

“Agatha was the first person who actually cared about me, despite my flaws, and she honestly makes me a better person. We were shocked when we were sent to the same boarding school, though I have a feeling she was behind it.” 

“I have no idea what you’re talking about.” Her lips curved into a knowing smile. “Ralph was the first non-witch, and first man I cared about and loved. When my mother said I had to marry one of these things, I knew it would be him. We truly make each other better.” 

 

Ralph looked back at the tiny version of Agatha. “Love doesn’t have to be romantic, Princesa. Look around this table. This family is proof of that.” Turning to Ian, he brightened even more. “Then, in college, I met Ian. I didn’t think it was possible to meet someone as crazy about dinosaurs as Agatha, but Ian and Ellie came along.”

“Leave me out of this.” Ellie inserted.

“You’re a part of this, demon.” Agatha scoffed.

 

“I fell hard for Ian, but I was already committed to your mom. Love means making sacrifices, Princesa. It’s what we did. It also means compromise. We made it work, and I don’t think we would change it. It got us here, and it worked pretty well.”

Ian’s fingers were laced with Ralph’s. He leaned over and kissed his husband on the cheek. “I fell in love with Ralph because he was so supportive of Agatha and how much he loved her. I didn’t realize one person could love others as much as this man does. He’s a genius in his own right. His work in the medical field obviously spilled over into our work in paleontology. It’s fascinating what can happen when people combine their lives like that.”

Ian paused, turning to Ralph and letting out a contented sigh. “As hard as it was to wait to marry you finally, I was still able to be with you, and it was worth it. We still have the big family we wanted, we’ve done incredible things, and we’ve done it as a team.”  

 

Amelia looked around at the lack of dry eyes at the table and considered their words. She had a lot to think about, but now it was time to go in for the kill.

“Moms? What about you? When did you know?”

No one moved, not even the familiars under the table. 

 

Rio slid her eyes over to Agatha, who cleared her throat and spoke first. “I felt something when we met in London, but ignored it. I was on a mission.” Agatha turned and focused solely on Rio. “Then, in Oxford, we were bickering over theories, and I knew. It was July or August, maybe? You would get so frustrated.” She dropped her head with a small laugh before looking back at her. “I don’t know why I waited so long.”

Rio chewed on her bottom lip. “You were so infuriating. It was the way Wanda spoke about you. This maddening woman, with a frightening reputation, mind you, truly cared about other people. You could tell in the way you treated Wands and the way you truly love your family, Agatha.” 

 

Amelia was enthralled with the way they looked at each other. Her mothers were still in love, and now they just needed a push. She was about to say something when she felt Lily’s hand on her shoulder. 

“They need a moment, sis,” She whispered, nodding toward the doorway.

She pouted, but followed the others out of the room, thinking about her next move. “What do we do now?” She asked, looking at her older sister.

Wanda approached them both. “I don’t think there’s much we need to do, unless one of them decides to be stupid.”

“Something they both excel at.” Ellie reminded.    

🦕🦖🦕

Agatha still held Gabby in the crook of her arm, staring into Rio’s eyes as they reminisced about falling in love. “You do realize what our daughter was doing?”

Death nodded. “Yes. You’re a natural with a baby.” The words came out before she could stop them.

“As I said, I’m pretty good at the parenting thing. Are we still on for our date tomorrow?” Agatha’s eyes had fallen to Rio’s mouth and hadn’t moved.

“I wouldn’t miss it. We should probably help clean up.” She didn’t move, hand dropping to Agatha’s thigh.

“I have baby duty, there’s a bunch of witches in there. They can handle it.” She leaned forward, her hand cupping Rio’s jaw. “I meant every word I said, Lady Death.” Her thumb brushed over Rio’s bottom lip.

They had drifted toward each other, mere inches between them. “So did I, Agatha.” She breathed against the witch’s parted lips. Her nose crinkled, and they both looked down at Gabby.

 

With a frustrated sigh, Agatha leaned back and confirmed their suspicions. “Let’s take you to Uncle Pietro. Let him practice before he and Emma decide to procreate.” Standing, she looked back at Rio. “Dress comfortably for tomorrow, I think you’ll enjoy what I have planned.” 

Agatha looked back at Gabby. “You’re supposed to do these things with your aunts and uncles, not me. We never interrupt Nana when she’s about to kiss Rio, understand?”

🦕🦖🦕

Agatha couldn’t remember the last time she had been nervous for a date. Looking at herself in the mirror, she fluffed her ponytail more. No, that wasn’t true. The last time she had been nervous about a date was in Oxford, with Rio. 

Always confident around women, it came easily to her, except for this frustratingly beautiful entity. Mainly because she was usually lying or trying to prove herself to the cosmic being, she had become more comfortable around her this time, though now she was trying to get her back, and that was going to prove difficult. 

They had both hurt each other, and she was taking responsibility for her part.

 

This date wasn’t about that. It was about starting over, reconnecting without being at work or having Amelia as a buffer. Hearing the doorbell, she took a few steps to the entryway, opening the heavy door to reveal her soulmate.

“Hi.” She breathed out, eyes raking over the woman in front of her. A hunter green sweater clung to her underneath a black pea coat that hung open. Dark-wash jeans hugged her curves perfectly, disappearing into mid-calf, black-heeled boots. 

Suddenly, Agatha wanted to abandon their plans. Stepping to the side so that Rio could pass by, she snuck a peek at the back of the woman and bit her lip.

 

“Stop staring at my ass, Ags.” The entity held back her smirk before turning and looking her date up and down. “Why are you dressed like we’re about to go to the island?”

The witch glanced down at her own black cargo pants, long-sleeve shirt, and hiking boots. “Because we are. I said dress comfortably.”

 

“I did! You didn’t say dress for an excursion. Agatha!”

Stepping forward, she encircled Rio’s waist in her arms. “For what we’re doing, you look perfect.” Placing a chaste kiss on the woman’s waiting lips, she held her close. “I’ve already set everything up and cleared the space for transferring. Trust me?”

“Against my better judgment.” Death huffed. 

 

The house disappeared from view and was quickly replaced by the mountain's plateau, the one they had stood on that night all those years ago. Though this time, a picnic had been prepared, and the stars shone brightly above them.

Agatha reluctantly let her go, leading her to the blanket she had spread out earlier. “It’s nothing fancy, but I know how much you love dinosaurs. Most have turned in, but you can hear the nocturnal ones and see a few flying.”

 

Rio cocked her head to the side with a mischievous grin. “Aren’t you some type of billionaire, Harkness? You couldn’t have swept me off to another country and a penthouse suite?”

Agatha uncorked a bottle of wine and poured it liberally into two glasses. “This is another country.” She stated. “If you were interested in me for my money, Dr. Vidal, you could’ve just said that. It would have been much easier to impress you with trips, fancy cars, and houses than to fund this.”

 

Death sat beside her witch, taking the glass offered. “Is that really why you funded and supported it? To draw me in?”

“A sinister supervillain never reveals her secrets, Feathers.” Giving her a wink, she offered her an assortment of breads and cheeses. “I told you, medical advancements and needing the plants, DNA, and whatnot. Why get you flowers when I can provide you with actual dinosaurs to study?”

“You’re insane, you know that?” Rio reached out and trailed her fingertips lightly down the other woman’s arm. “But what else can I expect from someone who causes me to be sick to steal my spot on a dig, only to make me homemade soup?”

Agatha turned away, busying herself with their food. “I have no idea what you’re talking about.”

“Sure you don’t. In London, you planted something to make me sick! I called the hotel restaurant later about the soup, but they didn’t make it. It’s not even something they offer. The guy told me that Agatha Harkness came in and demanded use of the kitchen! Looks like someone used her last name when she needed to.” Rio’s eyes were alight with the accusation.

“You had some mysterious illness; someone had to take care of you.”

“Admit it, Agatha. You felt guilty.” Death leaned into her, their faces inches apart.

Agatha’s eyes fell to Rio’s mouth again, setting her wine glass down. One hand clasped the back of her neck. “Yes, Feathers, I did. I needed to be on that expedition, and you know why. I felt terrible about it. I felt terrible about using you. I knew who you were, and subconsciously, what we are to each other.”

 

Rio removed Agatha’s hand from her neck and put a few feet between them. “I’m serious when I say we’re taking it slowly, Ags. You and I both know what Milly was up to yesterday and the day before. With all the talk of love.”

She made a frustrated sound in her throat and picked up her wine. “Yes, I’m aware.”

 

They ate in silence, commenting on the sounds around them and falling into easy conversation about Amelia, their family, and the upcoming holidays. Having vanished their trash, Rio lay with her head in Agatha’s lap, long, slender fingers in her hair.

“Where do we go from here, Feathers?” Agatha whispered, afraid to break the trance they were in.

“We keep following this, Aggie.” Raising her hand, she turned the woman’s face down to look at her. “I’m sorry for my part in all of this, and I’ve forgiven you for yours.”

Agatha nodded, unable to push words out around the lump in her throat.

“Thank you for this, Aggie.” She sat up next to her, their shoulders brushing. 

 

Agatha stood abruptly, pulling Rio up with her. Vanishing their picnic supplies back to her house, she kept a strong grip on Rio’s hand. “We’re not done here.” With a snap, she transported them to the clearing.

Rio’s heart was in her throat immediately; her lungs felt as though they had deflated. The makeshift bed was still there, the fallen logs. Everything was still exactly as they had left it.

Finally, Agatha’s hoarse voice cut through the silence. “It was the last place I saw you. Spoke to you. Told you that…well.” She rolled her shoulders back and looked away from Rio, who was now staring at her. 

“I would come here, usually drunk, when things got really bad. I preserved it, of course.” She put her hands in her back pockets, rocking on her heels. “I sound like a deranged stalker, I know. I just needed to feel closer to you, especially when I was writing.” Turning back to Rio, she inhaled deeply. “Then you showed up, with Amelia. To think that amazing little girl came from that night.” Walking to the fallen tree, she took a seat, eyes still on her beloved Death.

“I don’t want this place to hold bad memories when it gave us Amelia.”

 

Taking a few steps towards her, Rio tilted her head back and forth, as if considering the other events of that day. “It also led to Wanda and Zora, which led to Gabby…”

“Rio.” 

Taking Agatha’s hand in hers, she stood in front of her, slotting herself between her legs. “I don’t want that either, Aggie. I regret a lot from that day, but not that. Not us, and never her.”

She pressed the side of her face against Rio’s stomach, hot tears starting to trail down her cheeks. “Where do we go from here?” She repeated the question. 

Guiding Agatha’s hands to settle around her waist, she placed her own on Agatha’s shoulders. “We go back to your house, and we talk.”   



Rio transported them back to Agatha’s, still in the same position. She stepped back, settling on the couch beside her, turning to face her. “I want to start dating, Agatha.”

Her witch propped her elbow on the back of the couch, her face against her hand, and pursed her lips as if thinking about it. “Like Oxford all over again? Though this time we have history, a child, and a custody agreement?”

“We’re also being truthful now,” Rio added. “Some ground rules. No sex, like we had in Oxford.” Rio began. “Not until we have a relationship built on us and not physical desire.”

“That was a completely different situation,” Agatha grunted. “But I agree. Can we have sleepovers?”

“No. We can, however, kiss. Two and three. We cannot tell Amelia, and…” Rio’s words trailed as she looked away, worrying at her lower lip nervously. 

Agatha arched an eyebrow. “What’s number three? You know you can talk to me. Communication is literally something Clea has us working on.”

“We should probably tell Clea, away from Amelia, at therapy next week,” Rio added as a distraction.

“Feathers.”

“You have to understand where I’m coming from, Agatha. I am a timeless being. Five years is nothing to me. I realize you’re new to immortality, that I disappeared, never reached out, that you were respecting my boundaries—”

“But how could I fall in love with someone else?”

Rio shook her head, “No. I’ve seen humans be in love with multiple people at the same time, I just…” She ran a hand through her hair and shrugged off the coat she was still wearing, tossing it on the other couch. “I can’t hear you say it until you know for sure that I’m the only one. That this is it. That there are no more games, lies, or trickery. I can’t do it anymore, and I promise the same thing.”

Rio’s hands were trembling as she made her request. There was one other thing she needed to ask of Agatha, but that would come later. She knew neither of them was ready for that particular test yet. They were just getting to know each other again. 

When Agatha had asked for sleepovers, Rio so badly wanted to give in. Waking up in Agatha’s arms was one of the things she had missed the most in the past four and a half years. She was, after all, a complete sap. It was where she felt safe, protected, even if Agatha was a cold-blooded killer.



The witch picked at the skin around her fingernails, processing what Rio had said. Agatha was already in that place, but she understood what Rio needed and why she was asking. Giving it more time wouldn’t hurt either of them, and if anything, it would only strengthen their relationship. It would also make them more solid and less likely to do anything that would disappoint Amelia. 

That was the most important part of all of this. It was why they weren’t telling their daughter. They knew how badly she wanted this, possibly more than they did. Getting her hopes up only for it not to work out, soulmates or not, would be devastating to all of them.

 

“Okay. I agree. Deal.” Leaning forward, she carded her fingers through Rio’s hair and slid their mouths together slowly. “Seal it with a kiss?” She murmured into Rio’s mouth, tongue dipping in to brush against Death’s.

“Custody agreement stays the same, and I’m still going to go back and forth,” Rio added as Agatha pushed her into the back of the couch and straddled her, hands roaming over her shirt. She was done with being serious for the night.

Death’s hands wrapped around Agatha’s wrists. “No, Agatha.”

The witch whimpered. “Want to take a cold shower with me?”

“It’s time to call it a night. I have to pick Milly up in the morning.”

“Fine, call me when you get home, please. I’ll see you Sunday?” Agatha cupped Rio’s face in her hand and placed a lingering kiss on her forehead before climbing to her feet and walking her to the door.

“Okay, Agatha.”   

🦕🦖🦕

December 18, 2025

 

Rio hated her own rules. She obviously hadn’t been thinking clearly that night when she had come up with them. 

They had never done a good job at keeping their hands off of one another, and now it was proving to be even harder.

However, Alice had brought up a very good point. Rio had to be sure that Agatha was no longer in love with her ex.

According to the people who knew her best, Agatha had never truly stopped loving Rio. While they had slept together over the Halloween weekend, Rio wanted to make sure that the next time they did, Agatha would communicate her feelings and that she was the only one Agatha had those feelings for.

As a cosmic being, Rio understood that love was a complex emotion and you could genuinely be in love with more than one person at a time. However, this was her soulmate, and she deserved all of Agatha’s romantic love, as she had told Agatha.

Alice agreed and constantly reminded her of the boundary she had set. 

So, no matter how hard it was, Rio stood by the rules. Returning to her realm every other week had helped immensely.

What didn’t help was working so closely with Agatha every day and participating in family holiday activities together.

They had taken to eating lunch together, doing their work in each other’s offices, consulting each other on lab results.

It truly was Oxford all over again. But this time, they weren’t hiding from each other. Agatha wasn’t siphoning her Magick in secret, and they were honest.

There also wasn’t any sneaking around or stolen moments on desks or in supply closets.

Beggars couldn’t be choosers.

During finals week, Rio sat on top of a lab table, a partially graded exam in her hand. Her focus was solely on the woman across the room.

“What is it, Feathers?” She asked, still looking over lab results from earlier that day.

“Milly wants me to take her shopping. For you.”

Agatha stiffened slightly, keeping her attention on the screen and papers in front of her. “I don’t need anything. The kids usually donate to a charity in my name, make me something, or we do an activity together. We’ve gone to several concerts, ski trips, that type of thing. Oh, there was the year they each gave me a scarf in a different shade of purple.”

“Why do I have a feeling you’re against the idea?”

“I’m not against it, necessarily, I just remember feeling this immense pressure to find the right gift for Evanora, and it never being good enough. I never pressured the kids for that reason, and if there’s ever anything I want or need, I get it myself. I wear my locket, some other jewelry, and I prefer to pick my own clothes and shoes. I don’t collect vehicles or any type of knick-knacks. Well, stolen artifacts, but she’s too young to get into that hobby. I prefer to spend time with my children, Rio. I do love items with their handprints, even if it’s just paper.”

“You know she’s going to insist on it, Aggie.”

Pinching the bridge of her nose, she turned, fully taking Rio in. “Please, don’t let her stress out about it. I’ll take her shopping for you. Is there anything you need? New scythe?” 

Humming her amusement, Death hopped down from her perch and crossed the room, placing her hands on Agatha’s hips. “We haven’t been able to spend much time together.”

“Someone keeps flitting off to collect souls,” Her arms snaked around Rio’s shoulders, pulling her flush against her.

Death brushed her lips against Agatha’s. “It is my week to do that.” 

Agatha closed the distance, taking advantage of Rio’s parted lips. Caressing Rio’s tongue with hers, she kissed her hungrily, flicking her wrist to ensure all the doors to the lab were locked. Effortlessly, Agatha gripped the back of Rio’s thighs and lifted her, legs wrapping around her waist. She carried her back to the table where she had been sitting and set her down.

Kissing down Rio’s jaw, Agatha’s words came out breathy. “Take Amelia to see Santa with me on Saturday.”

“Yes, Agatha.”

“Stay the night, Christmas Eve?”

“Yes, Agatha.”

Licking a hot stripe down Rio’s neck and back up, she kissed a line back to her mouth. Strong hands, with unglamored fingers, slid beneath the warm sweater and thin undershirt she was wearing. Moaning at the feeling of Rio’s smooth skin under her hands, she deepened the kiss before groaning and stepping back, hands dropping to her side, trying to regain her control.

“I’m sorry, I got carried away.” She panted, distancing herself from Rio.

“I, uh, yeah.” Rio’s words were nearly incoherent. Staring at Agatha, pupils blown.

Agatha dragged a hand through her hair, white streak catching in the fluorescent light, as she began gathering papers and organizing them neatly. “I’m going to pick up Milly and finish grading these at home. If I stay any longer,” Her eyes slid to Rio, “Well. We’ll need to disinfect a lot of surfaces, and that rule will be broken.”

 

With her bag packed, she kissed Rio tenderly, lingering just a bit longer than she should, pressing their foreheads together. “I’m working from home tomorrow, so I’ll see you Saturday.” Blackened fingers twisted at the collar of her sweater. “I,” she stopped herself again, stepping back, tongue darting out to wet her lips, she changed what she was going to say. “Be safe escorting souls, toots.” With a toss of her hair, she strutted out of the lab. Berating herself mentally and hiding the results of using the Darkhold.



Friday morning, Amelia woke up later than usual and looked at her star-shaped clock. Yesterday had been her last day of school for winter break, but she was still surprised to see that it was nine in the morning and no one had woken her up.

Padding downstairs in Rudolph pajamas, she found her Mom, dressed in joggers and a soft purple sweater, sitting at the kitchen island, surrounded by papers. “Good morning, Plumita. You slept late.”

Climbing into her mother’s lap, she looked at her curiously. “I thought Lily was coming in today to watch me?”

“She’ll be here next week. I decided to finish grading at home today, and thought we could wrap up the Christmas shopping this afternoon.” One hand continued grading papers while the other ran through her daughter’s hair. “Mama will be here tomorrow. We’re going to get pictures with Santa.”

Milly giggled, “Mommy, I don’t believe in Santa. I’ve met the real one!”

“Humor me.”

“If I must, but we’re going to the mall this afternoon and going to all of the stores.” The miniature menace compromised.

“If we must.” Agatha agreed.

 

Agatha conspicuously sent the third group of shopping bags back to the house as they entered yet another store. They had driven, but she wasn’t dragging their purchases all over the place. Her youngest was as much of a shopaholic as she was. 

Raising an eyebrow at the store they entered, she looked down at the tiny chaos demon. “Why are we in a jewelry store, my little love?” 

With a shrug of her little shoulders, Amelia looked up at her with innocent blue eyes. “You need to get a gift for Mama, right?”

“I was thinking new robes, not jewelry.” She replied dryly. The fact that she was already thinking of rings was beside the point, and her youngest didn’t need to know that.

“What about a necklace?” Milly suggested, leading the way to one of the cases. 

Or a collar. Agatha thought immediately, the image doing nothing for how pent up she already was. Not the time, Harkness.

“Plumita, I don’t know what we’re going to find here. I could have something custom-made that’s ethically sourced. I do know a guy.”

“Mommy, be normal.” The cosmic anomaly rolled her eyes.

“Look who’s talking,” she muttered, eyes focused on her daughter, who she swore was only chronologically a few months from being five. 

“May I help you with anything?” A woman asked, looking at Agatha up and down, her smile a little too wide.

Amelia answered before Agatha could. “We’re looking for a present for my Mama. Can we see that necklace right there?” She asked, sounding her age, pointing to an emerald-and-diamond dagger on a rose-gold chain.

The woman, whose name tag read "Madison," pulled out a key to remove the requested necklace. “Well, aren’t you just adorable?” Glancing at Agatha’s left ring finger, she presented the necklace. “She looks just like you.” 

“Thank you,” Agatha replied automatically, inspecting the necklace and turning to Amelia. “Do you think Mama will like it?”

Madison leaned against the case, attempting to show cleavage. “All of our diamonds and gems are ethically sourced, using Harkness mines.” 

Amelia giggled, “I think Mama will love it, but it has to be from you.”

Agatha gave Madison an amused smile. “Harkness mines, you say?”

The girl nodded. “Forgive me if I’m overstepping. I don’t see a ring. Are you divorced? You’re absolutely gorgeous.”  

Amelia crossed her arms in front of her chest and glared up at the woman. 

Agatha held out her hand. “I’m so sorry, I forgot to introduce myself. Agatha Harkness. Yes, that Agatha Harkness. You are overstepping, and my marital status is not your concern. May I speak to your manager?”

Leaving the store, purchase in hand, Agatha peered down at her daughter, arms still crossed over her small body. 

“Do you want to talk about it, Amelia?”

The youngest of five sighed and took a seat on a suspiciously empty bench, Agatha sitting beside her.

“Why do people do that? Try to ask you out? That’s the seventh time today!” 

Putting an arm around her daughter, she thought about how to word her answer and decided to rip the band-aid off all at once. “Plumita, I’m a very attractive woman, I can’t help it. I don’t have a wedding ring, and even when I did, it still happened. Just not as often.”

Sniffing, Milly turned to her, looking every bit her age. “But you and Mama!”

“I didn’t entertain any of those women, did I? Rio and I are not together, but my focus is on you and our family right now. Remember what Clea said. Worry about preschooler things. I’ll worry about the grown-up stuff, okay, Plumita?”

Nodding, she wiped her eyes on her sleeve. “That was our last gift. Can we go home and watch Christmas movies?”

“Of course, baby.”

On their walk back to the car, Agatha’s stomach churned with guilt. She and Rio were moving slowly, yes, but what was it costing them? What else did they have to do to know they were ready to be together, a family?   

🦕🦖🦕

December 20, 2025

The line for Santa was long, and Milly had agreed to this for one reason, and one reason only. She had one thing to ask Santa for, and she needed her Moms to hear it. Maybe they’d get the hint then. 

Climbing onto the chair next to the man in the red velvet suit, she looked at him and whispered, “I know you’re not really Santa, but you see those two women over there?”

He nodded, narrowing curious eyes at the little girl.

“Those are my Moms and they’re stupid. I’m going to ask for something, and I need you to go with me on this.”

The older man nodded, somewhat confused.

She smiled, nodding for him to continue. When he asked what she wanted for Christmas, she looked over at her Mothers innocently, then back at him. “A baby brother or sister.”

He chuckled. “My, that’s a big ask.”

“Well, I’m the youngest, and I know I would make a great big sister. My mommies are meant to be together. Another baby would be a perfect Christmas present.” Her voice was syrupy sweet as she watched the two women out of the corner of her eye, knowing they could hear her.

 

They both heard exactly what she said.

Rio turned and pinned Agatha with her gaze. That’s your daughter. 

Never denied it.

Asking for a sibling? Do you think one of the others put her up to it? Rio kept her eyes on their tiny gremlin.

They don’t have to. I’ve seen how she plays with Gabby.

Rio let out a long sigh. Should we tell her?

What? That we’re sorta dating? Are you ready for more, Feathers? Agatha stepped forward to collect their daughter. 

Rio’s lack of reply was answer enough.

“That was quite the request, cariño,” Rio mentioned casually as they walked back to the car.

Milly skipped ahead. “I would make a great big sister, and you give each other googly eyes anytime Gabby is over. You should have another baby.”

Agatha cleared her throat, the three of them getting into the car. “It doesn’t work like that, sweetheart. What do you want to do tonight?” She looked at her through the rearview mirror, trying to change the subject.

Milly huffed, turning to look out the window. “Movies, I guess.” 

🦕🦖🦕

Christmas Eve was usually spent with the entire family so they could spend Christmas morning together. However, Agatha had changed plans. No, this year it was to be her three youngest, the two dads, her, and Rio. The rest of the family would be there in the morning. However inconvenient it may be.

Even then, she had wanted it to be just the three of them, but Nicky and Lily were still relatively young and wanted to be with their parents. Agatha had given in quickly, especially when Amelia jumped at the idea of opening presents with two of her siblings. 

Having more people in the house was probably safer, as patience was not a virtue that Agatha subscribed to, and the situation with Rio was getting out of control. They had been on dates, communicating, spending quality time together, and growing closer. What more could they possibly need to do?

She shared as much with Ralph while the kids decorated cookies downstairs, and they finished wrapping gifts upstairs.

“Have you told her you’re ready for more? Or you know, asked her what else you need to do?” 

“We’ve kept the lines of communication open, but I haven’t done either directly, no. Should I?” She looked up at him from the box she was wrapping, trying to keep herself occupied and doing it manually.

“It’s a good place to start. There has to be a reason she’s still hesitant.” He finished the stack of presents he had left to wrap. “You’re still hesitant.”

Running a hand through her hair, she waved her hand to complete her stack as well. “A little. There are just a few little details with the whole soul reaping business. That’s all.”

“Agatha.” He reached forward and took her hand, their eyes meeting. “You’re never going to get the skeleton if you don’t tell her everything. You know that.”

“I’m just waiting for after the holidays, when everything settles down. Maybe she and I can go away for a weekend before the spring semester.”

“There’s a thought, just the two of you? I’m sure Princesa wouldn’t be suspicious at all.”

“Help me take these downstairs.” She grabbed a stack of presents and left the room, not waiting for him to follow.

“Where’s she sleeping tonight?” He asked playfully.

“Who are you, my father? It’s not your business.” Placing the gifts beneath the tree, she turned around, only to see Rio watching them.

“Where’s who sleeping?” 

“I’m going to check on the children.” Ralph excused himself.

“What a supportive ex-husband you are,” Agatha called after him, before turning to Rio. “He was teasing me about you, doesn’t matter.” Running her hands through her hair, she moved to step past the other woman.

“Agatha.” Rio clasped her hand around the other’s wrist, stopping her. 

Agatha turned her head, meeting Rio’s eyes. “How much longer are we going to do this, Rio?” 

“I—” The sound of footsteps thundering down the stairs caused her to drop Agatha’s arm and take a step back.

“Mama! Mommy!” Amelia shouted, running into the room. Looking at them curiously, she continued speaking excitedly. “It’s snowing outside!”

“There’s been snow on the ground all week, cariño.”

“I know, but it’s Christmas Eve. That makes it special!” She explained excitedly. “We have to go play in it!”

“Do we?” Rio’s brows knitted together.

“Of course we do, Feathers.”

 

Amelia had a reason for dragging the family out into the snow, beyond wanting to play in it herself. She and Lily hoped a friendly snowball fight would nudge her moms in the right direction. She didn’t think she would be interrupting anything when she had gone downstairs.

It was one of the downsides of being both a preschooler and a cosmic being: the thoughts and ideas of both ends of that vast spectrum.

Once bundled up and outside, they divided into teams. Ralph, Agatha, and Nicky against everyone else. While Milly thought their team of four would easily win, she was wrong.

Agatha played by her own rules, and Amelia quickly found herself unable to use her Magick, being tickled by her older brother. 

“Nicky!” She gasped, laughing. “That’s not fair!”

“All’s fair in snowball fights, kid.” He picked her up and put her on his shoulder. “I have captured a child!” He called out.

“I have Lily and Ian!” Ralph called back. “Everyone inside, snow’s falling harder.” 

Nicky raised an eyebrow and looked up at his little sister as he carried her toward the house. “Where’s Mom and Rio?” 

“Maybe they went inside?” She said innocently, pulling her hat down around her ears.

“There was only a small chance of snow today, Green Goblin.”

She shrugged innocently. “Freak storm?”

 

Rio could control storms, though she didn’t often interfere with winter ones. This was not her doing, though she had a feeling Amelia had a small hand in it. Not all of it, but the small being had at least increased the intensity. Death had made it her mission to pursue Agatha, knowing how competitive she was at everything. 

Ralph’s voice had carried to where she stood on the property, and just as she turned to head back to the house herself, a snowball caught her right in between her shoulder blades.

“Oh, you’re in for it, Harkness!” Turning, she scooped a handful of snow and lobbed it at the smiling woman a few feet away.

“I’ve seen toddlers with better aim, Lady Death,” She taunted, sticking her tongue out.

“How mature, professor.” Rio vanished in a puff of black smoke and appeared directly behind her, ready with snow in hand.

Whirling around, Agatha caught Rio’s wrist before she could do anything. However, with the speed at which she had turned, she continued moving, falling into the snow, taking Rio down with her. 

The laughter bubbling up died in Rio’s throat when she saw the way Agatha was looking up at her. Intense blue eyes pinned her there, even though she had landed on top of the witch. 

“Agatha,” Her voice came out ragged from the exertion. “Earlier, I,”

“Mom! Rio!” Lily’s voice pierced the air. “Where’d you two go?”

Something cold and wet hit the side of Rio’s face. 

“I win.” Agatha proclaimed with a grin, leaning up and placing a quick kiss on Rio’s cheek. “Let’s go, Feathers, before they send a search party.

Rio groaned at being interrupted, yet again. Moving to her feet, she held a hand out to Agatha, realizing how hard and fast the flakes were falling now. “I believe our child is meddling again.”

Agatha nodded. “This is one way people get sick.” She stood, brushing snow off herself as they transferred into the warmth of the house, immediately scanning the area for the shortest human in it. 

“She’s upstairs. I made her take a bath and put on her pajamas before it got too late. She’ll probably be down soon.” Ralph answered the unasked question.

“That sounds like a good id—” Agatha was cut off by the ball of energy traipsing back down the stairs in her Christmas dinosaur pajamas.

“It’s getting late! Let’s play games, read Christmas books, watch Die Hard, and do crafts!” 

 

Agatha laughed, Magickally switching her clothes out for dry ones. Her hot bath would have to wait. “Or, we can watch The Muppets Christmas Carol. Die Hard will have to wait another year.” Feeling how Rio was glaring at her, she amended her statement. “Another few years. We’ll eat whatever we have planned, and maybe do crafts. Then you’ll go to bed at a decent time so you’re not up too late.”

Amelia’s lips tugged to the side while she thought about it, but finally nodded, agreeing with her mother. “Okay, Mommy.” Glancing above where her mothers stood, her smile widened, showing off the gap between her two front teeth.

“You’re standing beneath mistletoe!” She exclaimed. “You two have to kiss!” 

The witch pivoted to look at Rio beside her, eyes slightly widened in surprise. “I don’t remember putting that there.” Both sets of eyes darted to their daughter.

 

Amelia shrugged, attempting to appear innocent. “It just appeared.”

Lilith attempted to cover her laugh with a cough, making a slight choking sound instead.

“It is tradition, wouldn’t want to teach the kids bad habits.” Ian choked back a laugh of his own.

 

They’re horrible, all of them. Agatha thought toward Rio.

Keep it child-appropriate, Harkness. 

“Well?” Amelia tapped her foot impatiently.

 

“Since when have you been so interested in us kissing?” Agatha quipped, leaning toward Rio to give her a quick peck.

“Mom! You have to hold Mama, and act like you like her!” Milly demanded.

Ralph snorted behind his hand.

“You’re bossy,” Agatha grumbled, sliding her hands around Rio’s middle, pulling her in. “I’ve never had a child direct me before.” 

 

Rio’s teeth dug into her bottom lip, watching the witch complain about the situation, arms crossing behind Agatha’s neck. Leaning in, she placed a chaste, yet firm, kiss against Agatha’s slightly chapped lips.   

 

It was over before Agatha had even registered it. She stopped herself from chasing Rio’s mouth despite the instinct to continue. A tingling sensation overtook her body, an eyebrow raising in the direction of a small demi-entity making small hand motions and mouthing Latin.

Our daughter is trying to cast a mended heart spell on us. Agatha’s amused voice traveled through the link.

 

Rio broke their embrace and wrapped her arms around her torso. “Dinner, anyone?”

🦕🦖🦕

The house was finally quiet, everyone having gone to bed, or at least to their rooms. Agatha and Rio had put a very exhausted Amelia to bed together, and then parted to take their showers to head to bed themselves.

It felt wrong to Rio. Yes, there were still matters to discuss and work out. However, this was their first Christmas together as a family, complete with matching pajamas. Shouldn’t they at least be in the same room?

 

They were two adult women completely capable of sharing a bed without anything happening. Amelia would prefer to wake both of them up in the morning anyway, and they could tell her they planned it. That Rio had moved to Agatha’s bed right before Amelia woke up.

Justifying her future actions to herself, she made her way to Agatha’s room. Letting herself in when the witch didn’t answer her knocking, Rio noticed the bathroom door was ajar, and the sounds of running water filtered into the bedroom.

Thinking Agatha would sense her presence, she climbed onto the side of the bed she knew Agatha didn’t sleep on and settled against the pillows with a book. Knowing how long Agatha’s showers could last, she was surprised when she heard the water cut off a few minutes later.

 

Agatha let the scalding water run over her body, having already bathed herself. She had been lost in her thoughts and memories underneath the steady stream. Thoughts of the interrupted moments with Rio throughout the day, the matching pajamas they were going to wear, the words that were still unspoken, and Amelia’s attempts at bringing them back together. 

Rolling her shoulders back, she let out a long breath, thinking about the question Ralph had asked her hours earlier. “Where’s she going to sleep?”

“Not with me, obviously,” she gritted out, shutting off the water. Why would they share a bed? So they could have that cute moment of their daughter running in and waking them up in the morning? They weren’t fucking, though it would be great if they were. At least then they’d be satisfying part of the bond. 

 

It wasn’t about the holiday for Agatha. It was about wanting Rio. She wanted to hold her soulmate in her arms, wake up next to her, kiss her shoulder. 

“Get a hold of yourself, Harkness. You’ve gone soft.” Shaking her head, she shrugged into her robe after drying off.

Stepping out of the bathroom, her breath caught in her throat at the sight of Rio in the violet, satin sheets, reading a book.

Rio looked up at her with an unsteady smile. “I thought it’d be easier this way, for Amelia. We can tell her I woke up early and came in here.”

 

Agatha stared at the cosmic entity she was forever bound to, waiting for her brain to catch up. “I agree. I just have to get dressed.” It wasn’t that she wanted there to be any fabric between them, but she knew it was best if there was. 

Judging by the quick flicker of disappointment across Rio’s face and the way her eyes ran down the length of her body, Agatha wasn’t the only one thinking that way.

 

Grabbing the dinosaur Christmas pajamas off the bed, she padded back to the bathroom to prepare for bed, both clothing-wise and mentally. When they had shared a bed on Halloween, she hadn’t been able to enjoy it. Instead, once they had given in to their exhaustion, she had lain there and overthought everything. 

This wasn’t about sex, or them really. It was mostly convenience, but also building traditions. Checking in with her heart, she hoped it was the continuation of what they were carefully rebuilding. Another gentle step in the right direction. 

 

It made her want to give Rio her Christmas present early. She wouldn’t, but she wanted to. Instead, she ran her hands down the front of the button-up top and blew a strand of damp hair out of her face. C’mon, Harkness, it’s not the first time you’ve slept next to her. She thought to herself, shuffling back to the bedroom and avoiding Rio’s eyes as she settled herself into the bed, careful not to touch her.

Lady Death was ultimately the only woman around whom Agatha was self-conscious and nervous. Granted, she wasn’t exactly a woman; she was a cosmic entity with whom she shared a rocky past.

 

Said entity had placed her book on the nightstand and pulled her pillows together, no longer sitting up. Surprising Agatha, Rio wrapped her arm around the witch’s torso, laying her head on her shoulder. “I missed this, Aggie,” she whispered into the fabric covering the woman’s skin. “I missed the way we would hold each other, the way our bodies fit together. I missed the way you cared about me, even if we weren’t telling each other the truth at the time.”

Pressing her lips to the crown of her head, Agatha pulled her reaper closer, arm wrapped around her back. “I may have had a terrible way of showing it, but I have always,” she paused, holding back the words, “cared about and adored you.”

“I know.” Propping herself up on her elbow, she met troubled blue eyes, twirling Agatha’s white streak around her finger. “What should we do about Amelia?”

“That’s also my concern.” She ran her fingertips lightly over Rio’s back. “Especially with that spell and the mistletoe.” Her lips curled upward in pride. “I want to schedule an appointment with Clea. I think it’s time to tell Milly what’s going on before she gives herself an ulcer. She’s four.”

“She’ll be five in less than three months.” Rio reminded.

“Not you, too. She’s started telling me she’s closer to five and is already planning her party.” The witch’s free hand dragged through soft, dark hair, lightly scratching the woman’s scalp. 

 

“I think it’s time to let her know that we’ve been seeing each other, romantically.”

“You say it so formally,” Agatha chided, cupping Rio’s cheek.

“How should I say—” 

 

Before Rio could finish what she was asking, Agatha pulled her down to finally press their lips together. A warmth having nothing to do with blankets spread throughout her body as her jaw worked in sync with Rio’s, fingers tangling in her hair. The kiss was slow, gentle, reverent. 

Rio lay partially on top of Agatha, sighing into her mouth, one hand firmly planted against her collarbone. Straying from Agatha’s lips, she left a trail of kisses down to the hollow of her throat and nipped at the part of her collarbone that was exposed.

A whimper left Agatha, the sensation of Rio leaving her mark going straight to her core. Disentangling her fingers from Rio’s hair, she dug them into Rio’s shoulders, encouraging her to bite and suck harder.

 

Releasing the skin between her teeth, Rio propped her chin on Agatha’s chest and gazed up at her, pupils blown. “As much as I want this, Agatha.”

“I know.” The witch huffed, partially in frustration. “Patience is hard. I’m a succubus, Rio.”

Returning to her original position with a smirk, she chuckled. “Think of it as growth, Aggie.”

“It’s suffering, is what it is. Alice is going to run out of violent offenders.” She grumbled, pulling Rio closer to her. They may not be having sex, but she had Rio in her arms and was breathing in her earthy scent. Hopefully, after the present she had for Death, other than the necklace, they could truly begin to move beyond just dating and spending time together. Sleepovers, living together, the L word. She wasn’t sure where all this was coming from, but it didn’t frighten her either. She wanted it, and she wanted it with Rio.

“You’ll survive, I’m sure of it.” Rio kissed her in a way neither dared to label as loving, draping her arm back over the witch’s waist and entangling their legs. “Goodnight, Ags.”

“Goodnight,” she paused, “Rio.”

🦕🦖🦕

Amelia woke up, grabbed Sir Cuddles, and tossed the covers off, waking both familiars. “It’s Christmas!” She whisper-shouted to the two Magickal beings. Making sure her clock read seven-thirty, the time all parties had agreed was appropriate, she ventured out of her room and to Agatha’s.

“Yes, I’m certain they’re both in here.” She quietly answered her mother’s rabbit. “I did the spell, remember?” Pushing open the heavy door, she ran in and jumped on the bed. “Mommy! Mama! Wake-up! It’s Christmas!” Smiling widely at her mothers sleeping so closely together.

 

Agatha groaned, wrapping her arms tighter around Rio and burying her face in her hair. 

“It’s time to get up!” Amelia urged again, trying to shake them.

“Five more minutes, cariño.” Rio tightened her grip on Agatha’s hands, a sleepy smile lighting up her face.

 

“I’m sending these pictures to everyone.” Ralph strolled in, phone in hand.

As much as she loved the way her family interacted, she was still almost five and knew there were piles of presents downstairs. “Mooooommmmssssss,” she whined, making the word stretch. 

“She’s more impatient than we were,” Nicky noted, entering the room.

“It looks like there’s something extra special under the tree,” Lilith announced, mischief in her eyes. “I already checked it out.”

 

Ian was the last to join them in the room, and the two women finally sat up. “I brought you both coffee. We might want to head down for that special present. Now.” His voice held a sense of urgency.

Agatha grunted. “I take it that’s from someone on your side of the family, Feathers.” 

 

Amelia jumped off the bed, racing in front of everyone else and nearly flying down the stairs to the living room. She scanned the presents, seeming to have multiplied overnight. Her sparkling blue eyes fell on a stack of presents moving on their own. She took a few cautious steps before the rest of her family joined her. 

“Careful, Plumita.”

The young entity reached out, and a small dog-shaped head with giant floppy ears surfaced, before the rest of its body came tumbling out. It was black, with a few copper-colored markings, blue eyes, and gigantic paws. Hinting at how big the dog would be. 

“Mommy! Mama! It’s a hellhound!” Amelia shrieked in delight.

Rio shook her head vigorously. “Absolutely not.”

“So not from family, but Hela.” Agatha cackled.

 

Ian and Ralph laughed. “We were going to get her one of the smaller critters from the island,” Ralph added.

“You told us no when we asked for a hellhound,” Nicky pouted.

“I don’t think Hela asked, you child,” Lily scoffed, also with a slight pout.

 

Amelia immediately scooped up the abnormally small pup of legends and carried her to Agatha, who had already taken a seat on the floor. 

“Look, Mommy! Some of her markings seem to be in a pattern!” She pointed them out and giggled when the puppy licked her cheek. 

 

Inspecting the wriggling animal in her daughter’s arms, and ignoring the glares from her love, she traced the pattern and nodded in confirmation. “It’s a constellation. Astraea to be specific.”

“The goddess of justice, innocence, and purity,” Rio inserted, joining them on the floor. “I still say no. She’s going to be the size of a horse, at least.”

 

“Oh, what’s one more animal, Ri?” The nickname rolled off Agatha’s tongue easily, as if she said it daily. “Once she gets bigger than you’re comfortable with, she can stay here. Right, Plumita?”

Milly nodded, turning her big blue eyes on her other mother. “Please, Mama?”

Rio gritted her teeth. “What are you going to name her?”

 

“Astraea,” They replied to Rio in unison.

“We’ll talk to Hela, and I’ll think about it.” She said tersely. 

 

Amelia sighed dramatically, looking around at her family, all pretending to be interested in their phones. “Present time!”

Effortlessly popping up from her spot, dog still in her arms, she skipped closer to the tree, passing out presents. Her siblings joined her in elf duty, setting aside the gifts for those who would arrive later.

Once those currently in attendance had their stockings and a pile of packages, Amelia began ripping into the paper at warp speed. Astraea chased the pieces of paper as they were tossed to the floor.



Agatha spent more time watching those around her than she did opening her own gifts, taking pictures with her phone to add to those on the walls or in albums. Her side was pressed into Rio’s from knee to shoulder, not that there wasn’t room on the couch, but because they simply wanted to be close. 

With the final seal of the bond on Halloween, they were more drawn together than ever. She also found that this was where she wanted to be, soulmates or not.

“Did Hela get back to you about the dog?” Rio murmured in her ear.

“What?” Agatha did a double-take, coming out of her thoughts and catching up. “Oh, yes. As we suspected, she’s the runt and has a few defects. Blue eyes instead of red, black, or even green.”

The dog in question, sitting in Agatha’s lap, perked her ears and looked between the two women curiously.

“Which means looking at any kind of flame isn’t good for her.” Death surmised.

“Correct. Copper markings instead of ash-colored ones, and she doesn’t have the smoky-flamy feature.” 

“Well, that’s helpful. So, she isn’t a full hellhound, is she?” She looked at Agatha in question.

 

Keeping her focus on their daughter and intertwining her fingers with her soulmate’s, she took a deep breath. “No. It seems that after the hellhounds bred, the mom bred with some other dog. She’s a hybrid. Hela thought Astraea would be perfect for Milly because of that, and not being fit for hel.”

Scratching Astraea behind the ears, she cooed. “But that’s okay, because you’re such a cutie. Yes, you are.” The puppy rolled over on her back so Agatha could give her belly rubs, and she continued showering the ball of energy with praise. “Who’s Mommy’s good girl? You are! Yes, you are.”

 

Rio eyed the dog with a hint of jealousy. “I used to be Mommy’s good girl,” she groused.

The witch cleared her throat, setting Astraea back on the floor, a slight blush on the apple of her cheeks. She eyed the stack in front of Death. “Are you going to open anything?”

“I was waiting for you, m’lady. I do have a more private gift for you. Nothing like that, Aggie. We do need to be alone for it, though.” She explained, pushing her hair behind her ear.

“I have something like that, too.” Agatha reached for one of the boxes in front of her and began opening the paper, eyes still partly on those around her, thoughts filled with Rio being her good girl.

🦕🦖🦕

Agatha was always grateful for her Magick when it came time for clean-up, especially after holidays. 

It was now late evening, and the rest of the family had portaled, transferred, or driven in. All gifts had been opened, the dog had received pets from all humans, both familiars had been given their gifts, dinner had been eaten, and the day was coming to a close. 

Finally. 

Ellie took her best friend by the elbow and steered her to the office. “You slept together last night?” She asked excitedly.

“We shared a bed.” Agatha deadpanned. “Nothing happened other than innocent kissing.”

“And what’s that?” The scientist pointed down to Agatha’s right wrist. “I won’t point out the obvious bruises and bite marks on your neck and collar bone.”

 

She looked down at the new bracelet with the triple-moon insignia, inlaid with the birthstones of each immediate and extended family member. “My gift from Rio, no doubt chosen by Amelia. Amelia picked out a dagger necklace for me to give her Mama.” She didn’t care about the marks; she wore them proudly.

“I saw it, very pretty,” Ellie smiled in amusement. “Your daughter is encouraging you two to buy jewelry for each other?”

She bit back a laugh. “She tried to cast a mended hearts spell last night, but it didn’t do anything. We’re doing that on our own, I think.” 

 

“Why won’t you two just admit that you belong together, get married, and live happily ever after?” Ellie dropped into one of the office's armchairs.

“Because they’ve hurt each other so deeply in the past, they’re afraid to make any sudden moves,” Ralph answered, slipping into the room.

Agatha gestured in agreement. “We’ve set the terms, we’ve been taking things slowly. We’re going to tell Amelia that we’ve been dating, with Clea’s help, of course.”

 

Exchanging glances, the other two adults looked at Agatha hopefully. “When?” Ralph put a grounding hand on Agatha’s shoulder, where she was leaning against her large desk.

“Tomorrow, mid-morning. She was able to fit us in, said the sooner the better.” Running a hand through her hair, she continued. “Rio and I each have a gift to exchange with each other that has to be done with just us,” Lifting her eyes to Ellie, she raised an eyebrow. “Did you perhaps give her my journals and scrapbooks?”

“Did she tell you she had them?” Ellie countered.

“No.”

“Then no, I didn’t.” The blonde answered, ignoring the way Ralph was looking at her.

Agatha hummed, not for a second believing her friend. “That solves that then.”

“I know the whole family is here, but you two need time for that. After Amelia goes to sleep, you two should go over to Rio’s, exchange gifts—”

“Have sex,” Ellie interjected.

Ralph glared at her.

“What? Have you seen how they are around each other? It’s like watching the raptors at feeding time.”

“That aside,” Ralph continued, eyeing Ellie. “I’m just saying, spend some time together, have your appointment with Clea tomorrow, and if you need all of us to fuck off, we can. You have plenty of volunteers if you need a weekend sitter.”
