Agatha held her fingernail between her teeth, considering the offer and looking between two of the people she loved deeply. “Thank you. Now to get the chaos gremlin to sleep at a decent time. She gets so excited when the house is full.”

“Takes after her mom.” Ellie stood and headed for the door. “Go get ‘em, slugger.” She winked over her shoulder.

Agatha leaned into Ralph, “What if I fuck up again?”

“You’ll work it out. You’ve both grown, and all roadblocks have been removed. The only thing stopping you now is the doubts you both have and your fears. We’re here to support both of you, all three of you, really.” He gave her a tight squeeze. “Let’s squeeze some more Christmas cheer out of this day, then you and your beloved grim reaper can exchange gifts and bodily fluids.”

“You’re lucky I love you.” She grinned, leading him out of the room.

“Very.”    

🦕🦖🦕

A few hours later, Agatha and Rio finally stepped out of Amelia’s room. They had carried her to her room after she had fallen asleep, downstairs, listening to stories of previous holidays. Now, she was cozy in bed with her stuffed bunny, blanket, and three animals.

Amelia was living her best life.

 

Closing the door softly behind her, Agatha reached for Rio’s hand. “Are Jen and Alice at your house?”

Rio slotted her fingers between Agatha’s, letting the witch guide them. “No, they have to work; they went back to New York.” 

Agatha ran her free hand through her hair, “I knew they did, I just didn’t know if they were going back tonight or in the morning.” They came to a stop in front of Agatha’s bedroom. “I want to give you your other present. We can do it here or at your place.”

 

Rio poked her tongue in her cheek, still holding her witch’s hand, standing directly in front of her. “I want to give you yours first,” meeting her gaze, she added, “my place is probably the better option. You’ll understand soon enough.”

Not giving Agatha enough time to disagree, black smoke transferred them directly into Rio’s bedroom. She had aimed for the living room, though a rogue thought had changed their course.

 

Agatha looked around at the change in their surroundings, tilting her head with a cocky smile. “I see why you wanted to go to your place.”

 

Rio huffed, still holding her hand. “I missed the living room.” Removing her hand from Agatha’s to grip her wrist instead, mirroring her movements with the other. She brought both hands to either of her temples, prompting Agatha to extend her fingers.

Having caught on, Agatha stared at Rio in bewilderment. “What? Are you sure? You want to let me in your mind?” Her mouth was agape, surprised at the gesture.

“I want to take you on a memory walk, Agatha. I can’t give you those years back, or the experiences, but I can give you this. Let me.” Her voice was thick with emotion, her eyes already glassy from the anticipation.

 

“Yes, Rio. I did see several of your memories through Amelia’s dreams, though I was hoping to see them through you.” 

“Good. Let’s get started.” Rio felt the jolt of purple Magick to her temples as she led Agatha to the first memory.

They stood in a doorway of what was now Lily’s bathroom in Rio’s New York apartment. Rio leaned against the counter, watching the word “Pregnant” pop up on the digital test, while several other tests showed two lines or a positive. 

Past Rio swore in multiple languages.

“Went a little overboard?” Agatha quipped.

“I had to be positive. My life was about to change forever.” Rio hissed, but not aggressively.

 

The Rio with the pregnancy test ran her hand down her face, picked up her phone, and for a split second, almost called Agatha before calling Lilia instead.

“Lilia, I need you here. It’s an emergency. Jen, too.” 

 

Agatha watched the scene play out, her thumb absentmindedly rubbing circles on the back of Rio’s hand that she was holding. Listening to how adamant Rio was about keeping it all a secret, she could hear the uncertainty in her voice. The fear and worry. The woman who had so decidedly rejected her months prior seemed heartbroken and scared.  

“You were reacting,” She stated plainly.

 

Rio hummed in acknowledgment as the scene changed before them, memories of the pregnancy: morning sickness, all-day sickness, restless nights, reaper duties, visits with Lilia.

 

Two moments stuck out the most. 

 

When Jen did the first sonogram, Agatha watched as past Rio held her breath until the distinct whooshing sound of the heartbeat filled the room. Automatically, she pulled Rio closer into her side.

“Do you want to record the sound?” Jen queried. “For memories, or just in case?”

Jen didn’t add to it; she didn’t have to. Past Rio lifted her phone and recorded a short snippet of the sonogram while the pictures printed, before furiously swiping at any hints of tears.

 

Agatha pressed a light kiss to Rio’s cheek as the memories continued, tasting the salt of her tears on her lips. “The first visit is something sacred in itself. Hearing that heartbeat and seeing those images finally makes it all real. To do it without anyone by your side, well, I can’t imagine. You were perfect.”

“I was selfish and acting out of anger. It was stupid.” Death muttered.

“You did what you thought was right at the time. You were perfect, baby.” Agatha held her close as they moved forward.  

 

The first time Amelia moved, Rio had been watching Real Housewives, her hand instantly moving to the spot she had felt the flutter. Looking down in awe, she huffed a gentle laugh. “Your mommy doesn’t like that one either.”

“I knew you were radiant.” Agatha flicked a tear away from her cheek.

“I was miserable. Excited, to be honest, and so thankful for Magick. I used it more in those nine months than I had in a whole decade. Preparing food, making myself comfortable, anything I could do to make life easier.” The entity reminisced, a small hint of regret in her voice.

“I—” Agatha began.

Rio cut her off quickly, “I know that now. These past few months have taught me a lot, Aggie.”

 

It was now the day of Amelia’s birth, as they stood in the house in Soria. Agatha took in the stoic yet miserable-looking woman in labor, refusing to show an ounce of pain. 

“Why are you holding back?” She questioned. “I saw this in Amelia’s dreams, but now, standing here with you, I want to know. You feel pain, so why pretend you don’t?”

“I was focused on Amelia, and I didn’t want the bond to pull too harshly or cause too much pain in fear you’d come looking for me.” She answered softly, cheeks turning pink from embarrassment.  

“Stubborn ass,” she bumped her shoulder, turning back to the scene.

While she knew it was but a memory, she watched in awe as the woman she loved brought their beautiful daughter into the world. Her hands itched to reach out and take the laboring woman’s hand, to wipe the sweat from her brow and breathe through each contraction with her. Her lips twitched, aching to kiss away the silent tears and whisper words of praise and encouragement. 

When Amelia’s cries cut through the room, she turned to face present-day Rio and kissed her gently. Nothing but two mouths moving against each other.

Agatha pulled back, leaning her forehead against her love’s. “You did amazing, and I am so sorry for the things I did that led to it happening like this. You did a beautiful job, and I promise that I will never miss something like this again.”

Rio closed the distance between them, tears mingling as their tongues collided in a heated kiss. “I wanted you there so badly, Agatha.” She breathed, finally pulling away. “Those five years were excruciating for me. I really thought I was doing the right thing.”

“I know you did, honey.” Holding Rio against her, she took in the image and embedded it into her own memory, determined to add to it with another child one day.

 

The thought stopped her as she stiffened in Rio’s arms. Had she really just imagined having another child?

Death ran soothing circles over the muscles of Agatha’s back. “Baby, are you alright?”

 

Clearing her throat, Agatha kissed her cheek and stepped back. “Of course, feathers. Just admiring how beautiful you and Amelia are.”

 

Rio hummed, not entirely convinced, moving them on. The first laugh, all nighters, teething, Amelia being sick, and Rio not knowing what to do. “It was gas, Jen sent Gripe Water. I didn’t know what to do. Lilia even told me to call you. I was determined to do it on my own, but I ended up calling Jen.”

“I thought you cosmic types weren’t susceptible to illness?” 

Rio smiled wryly, “Only if an extremely cunning witch hexes us or siphons our Magick.” She nudged Agatha’s arm to let her know she was joking before turning the conversation back to their daughter. “She’s still half human, Agatha. She usually heals quicker, but yes. She gets sick.”

Agatha sighed, “I was really hoping I wouldn’t have to worry about the flu, strep, or stomach bugs this time around.”

“Sorry, Ags.” Rio’s voice held zero sympathy.  

 

At nine months old, Amelia took her first steps along a riverbank in Soria. 

When she learned to crawl, it was because she was chasing Victoria. Now, there was a very curious otter having a staring contest with an equally curious Milly. Rio was watching both of them closely from a few feet away on a large picnic blanket. The infant rose to her feet, took a few tentative steps toward the mammal, then stopped, waiting to see what it would do. The otter tilted its head at the creature before it and also moved closer.

That was the day Amelia learned to walk and play with otters. Rio was fascinated by the connection her daughter already had with animals at such a young age.

 

Amelia wasn’t quite a year old, sitting in Rio’s lap while she made lesson plans on her laptop. A video of one of Agatha’s lectures came on the screen, eliciting an excited shriek from the infant. Pointing at the screen, she turned, grinning up at Rio, incoming teeth on full display. 

“Mommy, Mommy!” 

Rio gasped, peering down at her daughter. “How?” She questioned, but was unable to finish.

Amelia’s small hand ran over the screen where Agatha’s face was now paused. “My mommy.” Her eyes were now locked on the screen.

Rio wiped away her tears, standing and placing the baby on her hip. “Yes, Amelia, that’s your other mother. How you know that, I don't know. I think we should go for a walk.”

 

“I thought you said she learned about me through your dreams?” The witch raised an eyebrow.

“Mostly, yes. She learned about the family, our connection, everything that way. However, she just had this bond and automatically knew who you were, before that. She’s always been very intuitive, observant, and mature for her age. Since birth. That whole cosmic being thing.” She explained, gesturing with her hand.

Agatha’s lips twitched into her signature smirk. “I didn’t realize our child was basically Robitussin.”

Rio’s face scrunched into confusion. “What? She’s not cough syrup.”

“No, little Rigatoni, from the vampire books. Wanda was obsessed with them.” She elaborated further.

“Oh, those. Lilia tried to get me to read them, said they were highly entertaining and hilariously wrong.” She returned Agatha’s smirk. “I found it disturbing to think anyone would believe vampires sparkled like disco balls.”

“Humans,” they chuckled simultaneously before continuing their journey.

 

The witch continued to watch in wonder as Amelia grew, meeting milestones far ahead of those most children reached. Her heart ached at seeing Milly long for a family she wasn’t allowed to have, guilt radiating from Rio. 

“She traveled the multiverse to talk to our variants?” Agatha wasn’t sure if she should be proud, astonished, upset, or deeply depressed.

“She still thinks I don’t know about it, but yes. When Lily showed up, part of me was relieved, Agatha. That I finally had a way to end this, to give her the family she deserved. To find my way back to you, only to find—”

“That I was with someone else.” She caressed the entity’s cheek, Rio leaning into the touch.

 

“Not just that, Agatha. You were in love with her.” Amber eyes, flecked with gold, were filled with uncertainty and hurt.

“As much as I could be, I know. We both did things we’re not proud of, honey.” Her words held no malice, only honesty. “Neither of us is going anywhere this time. Understand?”

“Yes, Agatha.” 

 

The years continued, with Lilia or Lorna watching Milly anytime Rio had to do collections, when she went to the graduation, and when she had to be physically at the university for a lecture. Agatha was grateful that their daughter at least had that small bit of family.

A memory of Amelia and Rio playing with dinosaurs was particularly captivating. The little girl was slightly past two, listening attentively to her mama about different species and an island with real, living dinosaurs. 

Growing tired of science, she grabbed an allosaurus and held it up to Rio’s face. “RAWR!” She cried excitedly. “I know dinosaur!” She added.

Rio laughed brightly. “Technically, they didn’t,” She stopped, as though she were reminding herself she wasn’t in a lecture hall or a lab. “What did you say?”

“I love you!” Their daughter grinned, scrambling to hug Rio.

 

Agatha’s stomach flipped; the whole scene was deja vu for her.

“Rawr means I love you in dinosaur?” Her words were hushed.

“Not really, Agatha, you know—”

“Yes, I know that,” she interrupted, “is that what she was saying?” The witch clarified.

“Yes? Why?” 

“The older kids, they all used to say that. It was a thing between them all when they were younger.” Rolling her shoulders, she tried to shrug it off, memories continuing. 

 

At the age of three, even though she knew it was coming, she still gasped and tried to catch the tiny stuntwoman when she fell out of the tree and broke her arm. 

Milly had watched a cat fall from a tree and, at the last minute, flip and land on its feet. Amelia, ever the daredevil, decided that she, too, had cat-like reflexes.

Unfortunately for Amelia, she did not, and her arm paid the price. 

 

“I feel terrible because our little girl is hurt, and at the same time, she found out.” Agatha gestured at the situation. 

“You just tried to catch her to prevent it from happening, Aggie.” Rio deadpanned.

“I still want to protect her. I’m the same way with the other four. Do they sometimes need to find out? Absolutely. It doesn’t mean I don’t want to protect them from everything. It’s the hardest thing about being a parent: knowing they have to learn the hard way or face things on their own. All you can do is hope you taught them well.” She was turned away from Rio now, hands in her back pockets, looking on as Lily taught Milly how to flip a pancake. 



The last memory was the night before they left for Boston. Milly sat on her Mama’s bed, holding Sir Cuddles and her blanket tightly. “Mama?”

“¿Sí, cariño?” She continued to pack a few last-minute toiletries and clothes to keep her hands busy.

“Do you and Agatha still love each other?” It was barely a whisper.

Rio stiffened, placed a shirt in her suitcase, and sat next to her daughter. “It’s complicated, mija. We once loved each other very much. We wouldn’t have made you if we hadn’t. Then, there was a fight, and I walked away.”

“You’re soulmates.” A statement.

“Yes, we are.” She confirmed.

“And we’re all family. Everything will work out. I’m excited to finally meet her. I know you didn’t have to give in to Lily. Thank you, Mama.” Amelia crawled into her lap and lay her head on her chest.

Rio wrapped her arms around the precious child. “Of course, cariño. It’s past time for this to happen.”

 

Everything faded, Rio’s bedroom reappearing around them. Agatha was convinced her tears had etched permanent trails down her cheeks. 

“You really did want to come back.” She croaked before clearing her throat.

“Of course, Aggie. The illusions and warped realities she was creating were growing worse, the multiverse surfing. It wasn’t just that, now that we’ve fixed the universe, it’s glaringly obvious that we each did shitty things.” 

 

Agatha stepped toward her and placed her hands reverently on Rio’s hips. “Thank you for sharing all of that with me. I will always cherish having those memories now.” Closing the distance between them, she kissed her softly, moving one hand to cup her cheek, noses brushing against each other. She savored the way their lips moved together in perfect rhythm, tongues occasionally meeting. 

Agatha ended the kiss, trying to catch her breath. “I have something for you.” She held out the hand that had rested on Rio’s cheek, and a journal appeared in it.

“I know Ellie gave you my journals and scrapbooks. Here’s the latest one, which I think will answer many of your questions.” 

Rio took the book with a shaky hand and floated it onto her nightstand. “You’re not upset?” 

“If it were anyone else, I would be, but you? No. I’m not the best at admitting my feelings to people; this was much easier.” Agatha looked away and ran a hand through her hair. “Do you want me to leave so you can begin reading it?”

 

Panic flashed across Rio’s face. “No!” She nearly shouted before taking a breath. “I mean, it is my week, and Amelia’s asleep at your house. I could transfer her here. Or, I could spend the night at yours again.”

Taking a few steps forward and wrapping her arms around Rio’s waist, she hummed. “I would prefer to stay at my house, purely for safety reasons. Wherever would you sleep?”

“The guest room, probably.” The cosmic being sucked her lip between her teeth, looping her arms around Agatha’s neck. 

“And leave me all alone in my big, comfortable bed, in my room that is permanently soundproof?” She pouted, “You can always teleport over to the other room in the morning if you must.”

Rio smiled and looked down between them. “How dare you sleep alone, Dr. Harkness.”

 

She almost moaned at the way her title was used. Leaning forward, she brushed her lips against Rio’s. “You don’t have to, but it could be nice.” 

Rio was the one who took them back to Agatha’s. “Change for bed, Aggie.”

“Actually,” Agatha suddenly sounded unsure of herself. “Did you keep your stretchmarks? I didn’t get a chance to look at Halloween. We were a little busy.”

Rio’s lips parted in surprise, a small gasp tumbling out. “I…yes. I did.”

“May I see them?” 

 

Rio was shocked; it wasn’t something she ever thought anyone would ask her. “Yes.”

Still holding each other, Agatha stepped out of Rio’s embrace. Taking the hem of her sweater in her fingers, her voice held a slight tremor, “May I?”

“This does require some state of undress, Aggie,” she whispered, caressing her witch’s cheek. 

Removing the garment and letting it fall to the floor, Agatha’s fingertips quickly found the faded marks on the sides of Rio’s abdomen. “Are there any lower?” 

“A few, and yes, you may.” She anticipated the next question.

Making quick work of Rio’s jeans, she pulled them down her thighs and helped her out of them. Dropping to her knees, she returned her hands to the faint marks in front of her. Tracing every line she could find, she leaned forward, pressing kisses across her stomach and lower abdomen. 

    

Rio watched from above as Agatha showered her body in praise and what felt a lot like love. They had already shed their fair share of tears for the night, yet more pricked at the corner of her eyes as the sob she was holding back snuck out. 

Agatha immediately stopped and looked up, eyebrows knitted together in concern. “Honey, what’s wrong?”

Rio shook her head, hand over her mouth as though she could keep her emotions contained.

Just overwhelmed, she sent instead.

“You carried our daughter, brought her into this world, and raised her for a little over four years, mostly by yourself. You deserve a bit of worship for that.” She explained, standing and backing her into the bed, sitting her on the edge.

But with what I did—

“Baby, I’m Agatha Harkness. I come with a laundry list of bad deeds, I’m morally dark grey, and most laws are merely suggestions. We’ve forgiven each other, and we’re creating something wonderful, sturdy.” Taking Rio’s chin between her thumb and index finger, she leaned forward, kissing her passionately, determined to convey how much she meant every word she said.

 

The kiss turned sinfully heated. Agatha straddled her lap, tongues tangling together, and teeth clicking. Death’s bra was discarded, Agatha’s hands kneading the soft flesh, rolling, pulling, and pinching the hardened nipples.

Rio used Magick to strip Agatha down to her boy-cut underwear featuring dinosaurs in Santa hats. 

You said I deserved worship? Rio asked through the connection.

Of course. Agatha answered, moaning when Rio began to work at her throat.

Then on your knees. Put that pretty mouth to use. She growled.

 

Agatha pulled back, pupils blown. “Did you forget who calls the shots?”

“You said you wanted to worship me. Prove it.” She leaned back on her hands, brow quirked.

Shocked at her lover's boldness, Agatha returned to her knees, peeling Rio’s own dinosaur-themed underwear off. The fabric clinging to Death’s dripping folds. “Never beating the soulmate allegations.” She mused.

“Good,” Rio bit her lip in anticipation.

Strong hands ran up Rio’s legs and spread them apart, thumbs tracing circles into her thighs. She leaned forward, kissing her abdomen once more, tracing the silvery lines with her tongue. “You’re gorgeous, Lady Death.” Swallowing audibly, she worried her lip before looking up. “Are you sure we’re ready?”

 

Her hand gently scratched against Agatha’s scalp. “Yes, Agatha,” she breathed before gasping when the witch’s tongue moved through her pussy. 

“Lie back,” Agatha commanded, draping Rio’s legs over her shoulders and burying her face in her sopping cunt.

Obeying the witch between her thighs, she lay back, propped on one elbow, her other hand remained entangled in Agatha’s hair, not that she needed guidance. Hips moved in sync with Agatha’s tongue as she thoroughly explored every part of Rio, relearning her body and what she liked. 

“Agatha, please, baby. I need,” She panted, teetering on the edge of her release.

“I know, honey, I know,” She cooed, flicking her tongue over Rio’s hardened clit before closing her mouth around it and sucking, gently grazing the small bud with her teeth.

 

Rio’s back arched, legs closing around Agatha’s head while screaming her name. The witch’s tongue circled her swollen clit, both soothing and working Rio up again.

Give me another sweet girl? She purred into Rio’s mind.

Yes, Agatha.

Look at me, my love.

Rio’s breath hitched at hearing the term again, eyes locking on Agatha’s. Two fingers entered her with a delicious stretch, tongue continuing its circular motions. Agatha’s eyes reminded Rio of the vast expanse of space, but were still filled with admiration, desire, and love. Fingers curled perfectly against her G-spot, and she fought to keep her eyes focused on the woman below her as her vision blurred and white spots filled her vision. 

Slowly pulling out, Agatha sucked her fingers clean before kissing her way up Rio’s body, pulling her into the middle of the bed and into her arms.

“Aggie, I wanted to do that.”

“Next time, my love.” Leaning forward, she kissed her instead, allowing Rio to taste herself on Agatha’s lips.

“Next time? What about?” Rio searched Agatha’s face for a hint of what was happening.

“This was about you. Remember?”

“You called me your love. Twice.” Her voice trembled, eyes darting away.

Dragging a hand down Rio’s side, she placed a kiss on her cheek. “I mean it, just like Halloween. I know we’re not saying it yet, but you are my love.”

“Take a bath with me?” She asked, a lazy smile appearing.

“I would be honored. Wait here.” She smiled at herself as she went to prepare the bath. Agatha knew Rio still had to read the journal, but in that moment, she was happy, not due to sex, but because they had been able to share memories and emotions.

Merry Christmas, indeed.

🦕🦖🦕

December 26, 2025

 

“Mommy!” Amelia called, running down the stairs, ready to leave for Clea’s office. “Can I bring Astraea to therapy?”

“Of course you can bring her! We have an old collar and leash of Scratchy’s that will fit for now. They’re in with his things in the basement. If you ask him nicely, I’m sure he’ll help you find them.” She instructed, standing at the kitchen island, as she packed snacks.

“Okay, thanks, Mom!” Her footsteps disappeared towards the basement door, the aforementioned puppy close behind.

The witch continued her task, gathering everything they could need for the few hours they would be out. 

Rio stepped behind her, arms encircling her waist, and placed a light kiss on her shoulder. “She can’t bring the dog everywhere.”

Turning in Rio’s arms, she placed the tip of her index finger in the middle of the entity’s chest. “Maybe if you let her, she won’t get kidnapped on your watch again, Feathers.” The side of her mouth twitched up into a smirk. “Besides, we need to go to the pet store afterward, and before you say anything else, I ran it by Clea already. She wants to meet the little mutt.”

“Of course she does.” Rio sighed, feigning frustration.

 

Agatha ran her finger up and hooked it in the collar of Rio’s shirt, tugging her closer and kissing her quickly. Faster than either would like.

Stepping back, Rio took the snacks and carried them to the utility room to separate them into their respective bags. “Are you ever going to let me live that down?”

 

Ian walked into the kitchen, overhearing the conversation. “The kidnapping? Oh, absolutely not. I still hear about both the Pietro incident and Lily’s kidnapping. I remind her that Ellie crashed a damn plane, but apparently, she gets a pass.”

“Because she SAVED Lily.” Agatha reminded, turning to Amelia, who was attempting to walk a hellhound pup on a worn collar and leash, Scratchy fussing at them both.

 

Agatha clapped her hands together, smiling at the sight. “That’s our cue to load and leave. Ian, let the others know we’ll be back this afternoon.”



Walking into the small brownstone Clea used as an office, Rio thought about the previous night and how right it felt. While Halloween had been angry, raw, and overwhelming, last night was about reconnecting. A step forward in building toward the future she so badly wanted. 

Agatha hadn’t allowed anything more to happen, telling Rio that she had to start reading the journal first. The terms were acceptable, but they didn’t make it easier. Rio needed to feel every inch of her witch and treat her with the same care she had been.

Looking around the waiting room, she thought about the past few months of sessions. They had already grown individually and as a family. Amelia was gaining friends at school and at least acting her age around them. Home was another story, but small steps were better than none.

 

Rio had spent most of her week with Agatha, celebrating the holidays as a family, including the past two nights. To her, it was the way it should be. It felt natural, what she and Amelia had been missing the past several years. Everything was falling into place.

Now, they were about to let their daughter in on the changes in their relationship. They knew Milly wanted them together as a family; the kid had tried an ancient spell. So why was the entity nervous?

 

“Plumita, honey, stay here with Astraea, while we speak with Clea real quick, okay? We’ll call you in when we’re done.” 

Milly beamed up at her, already sitting on the floor playing with the pup. “Yes, Mommy!”  

Clea stepped out of her office, long white-blonde hair pulled back in a ponytail, sparkling green eyes landing on the duo playing on the floor. 

Rio took in her appearance. Black leggings and an oversized maroon Strange Academy sweatshirt. She was clearly meeting with them the day after Christmas as a favor.

 

“You look comfortable,” Agatha observed.

“You have hellhound hair on your overpriced denim,” She retorted, looking at Rio. “You, however, look perfect.” She glanced back at the youngest, mostly human, in the room. “Who did you bring with you, Amelia?”

“This is Astraea! Hela gave her to me for Christmas!” She explained excitedly, rolling a ball for the hound to chase. 

“I look forward to meeting her. I’ll see you in a few minutes. Be good, both of you.” Gesturing for the adults to enter the office, she closed the door and sat in the chair opposite the couch where the couple sat. “Good Morning, let’s get right to it. You’re ready to tell Milly you’re dating?”

 

Rio nodded, taking Agatha’s hand. “She tried to cast a Mended Hearts spell on Christmas Eve.”

“Her form and some of her Latin was wrong, thankfully I had the counter spell memorized,” Agatha explained.

You did a counter spell? Rio’s outer demeanor remained the same, though her heart sank internally. Did Agatha not want this?

We don’t need it, nor do I want us to be Magickally manipulated by our preschooler, love. We’re doing the work ourselves. Releasing her hand, she snaked her arm between Rio’s back and the couch, settling her hand on her hip and pulling her into her side.

 

“However, we don’t need it.” Rio voiced, suppressing a smile as she settled into Agatha.

Clea jotted down a few notes, a laugh slipping out. “Now I owe Lilia money.” Skimming over her comment, she returned her attention to the immortal women in front of her. “How do you want to present this to Amelia? Where do you plan on going from here?”

 

“We’re still moving slowly. We haven’t redeclared feelings yet, so we’re still just dating and intimate.” Rio explained first.

“Spending more time together, but following the custody arrangement. Potential sleepovers? We’ll see how it develops.” Agatha added, catching Rio’s gaze, a silent question.

“Agatha gave me the journal she kept during the past year as a Christmas present. I plan on starting it tonight.”

“Rio’s gift to me was the memories of Amelia’s life that I didn’t have.” Gazing down at her lap, her voice softened. “I missed so much, but I understood.” 

Clea exhaled sharply. “I’m going to speak candidly.”

“Don’t you always?” Agatha raised a brow.

“Stop being idiots.” The therapist replied. “I understand the hesitation and why you’re being so careful, I do. Agatha, I’ve seen you individually, all of your kids, and as your family therapist. I know you almost as well as Calderu does. That is your person.” Her eyes shifted over. “Death, I’ve known you for quite some time, just never in this capacity, but I can see how your souls are entwined. Get your heads out of your asses. Both of you.”

Standing, she glided to the door to call Milly and her sidekick in. “Now let’s explain this to Amelia, and I swear to all things holy and unholy if you upset this child and fuck it up, I’ll blast you both to the Dark Dimension.”



Milly skipped into the room with Astraea by her side. She had been trying to find nicknames for her, but she hadn’t liked any of them. The ones she had come up with hadn’t fit how special she was. Ass and Assy were out; they sounded too close to adult words. Then there had been Stray, Tray, and Ray. She hadn’t liked any of them and was still trying when Clea came out to get her. 

Now she entered the room, and her focus immediately landed on her mothers, sitting on the couch, wrapped in each other. “The spell worked!” She squealed in excitement, Astraea’s tail wagging in agreement.

They shared a look and broke apart, patting the spot between them. 

 

Instead, Amelia dragged a unicorn bean bag to the side, angling it to where she could see her mothers and Clea. Flopping down, she pouted, her puppy climbing atop her to provide support.

“Amelia, you have been doing very well with adapting to all of the changes in these past few months. It’s overwhelming for any being of any age.” Clea began, angling to face her.

“I’m almost five, and I am a very mature cosmic being,” she said petulantly.

“Yes, you are.” Clea agreed, lips curving slightly. “We are all here to support you.”

“Plumita, your mother and I have put the arrangements we have in place to help with the transitions. The spell you tried to cast the other night, I cast a counterspell because—”

Milly lept to her feet in anger, eyes swirling, guard dog growling. “WHAT?! You and Mama LOVE each other! Why would you—”

“Because we’re dating, cariño,” Rio interrupted, before either Harkness woman could destroy the building. 

“What?” Amelia looked between all three women, sounding her age. “Why didn’t you tell me?”

 

“Remember how we have been working on worrying about normal four-year-old problems?” Clea asked, not chastising. 

The young girl nodded, hugging herself.

“Your moms love you very much, and they didn’t want to put that on you. They want you to focus on making friends, coloring, and bonding with your siblings.” Clea both explained and reminded.

Amelia peered at her mothers, hair covering most of her face. Astraea remained in her protective stance, ready to pounce. “Why tell me now?”

“After you attempted that spell, we decided it was time so you wouldn’t stress anymore. We want to discuss a few potential changes with you if that’s okay.” Agatha motioned toward the couch again.

 

Giving in, Amelia sat between the two women who loved her, petting the hellhound in her lap.

“Like what?” She asked curiously, fingers tracing the constellation on the pup's back. 

“Sitting closer, hand-holding, cuddling on the couch. The usual.” Agatha shrugged, eliciting an eyeroll and a smirk from their daughter.

Rio took over in a more serious tone. “Spending more time together, Mommy and I will go out more, and we might stay over at her house.”

“Why didn’t you tell me?” Pushing her hair back, she took a moment to look at each of her parents.

 

“Well, Plumita, we wanted to make sure we were doing the right thing. That it was going to work out this time before we put that on you. We know how badly you want us to be a family. With the past week, well, we thought it was time.” She reached over and smoothed her hair, testing the waters.

“You’re in love with each other and fated. Of course, you’re doing the right thing, it’s literally in the Darkhold.” Her small shoulders tensed again, clearly agitated.

 

“Milly, remember, your Moms didn’t want you to have to worry about adult business. They love you very much and didn’t want to disappoint you.” Clea asserted, furiously scribbling notes.

Amelia took a deep breath, scratching her dog behind the ears. “Okay. We’ll do this. I am glad you’re dating.” She smiled up at both of them.

 

Really, Milly was just relieved neither mother asked her how she knew what the Darkhold said. Hopefully, they’d just assume she’d overheard from a sibling, or it was common knowledge at this point. 

The Darkhold hadn’t turned her fingers yet, as she had only accessed it a few times. The stupid book hadn’t even given her what she wanted yet, and was as sassy as her Mom. How was she supposed to outsmart a book?

Instead of risking being caught, she focused on the possibility of her family coming back together. “I love you, too. Both of you.” She just prayed to the Divine Mother that they didn’t see right through her. “Can we go to the pet store now?”



Agatha kissed the top of her daughter’s head. “Of course, Plumita. Why don’t you go back to the waiting room, and we’ll be right out.”

Hopping off the couch, she slipped out quietly, puppy at her heels. 

 

“Well, that wasn’t awful,” Clea concluded.

 

Rio had already turned to Agatha, concern clouding her eyes, “How does she know what the book said? Did she overhear it from the kids?”

“Maybe.” Agatha bit the inside of her cheek. She seriously doubted it and would check when they got home. She didn’t want to worry her love at the moment. “Where do we go from here, Clea?”

 

“Follow your instincts. You were doing the family thing really well before you two decided to take twenty steps back. Rio, read the journal. I suggest spending a few nights apart while you do that. Other than that, enjoy your new beast dog, and I’ll see you after the new year.”     

🦕🦖🦕

Rio had to admit she enjoyed a good trip to the pet store. Having a familiar that could transform into different species made it all the more fun, especially with her girlfriend and daughter.

Girlfriend. The word seemed all wrong for what Agatha really was to her. Soulmate seemed cliché, and even partner didn’t seem quite strong enough. She shrugged it off and followed her other half through the store. No, that didn’t seem quite right either. 

Agatha had taken them to a store owned by a fellow witch. While it looked like any other pet store, there was a glamoured entrance to the Magickal side of the store at the back.

Meandering through the regular part first, they were constantly stopped by well-meaning customers asking what breed Astraea was. Rio was impressed each time with Agatha’s quick thinking.

“She’s a rottie mix, but with the way she chews and eats, you would think she was part raptor!”

“With as sneaky as she is, we suspect she’s part hellhound!”

“Harkness.”

 

Rio smirked at the last one. She had absolutely referred to Agatha as a hell bitch more than once. 

Rio pushed the cart, dog in the built-in seat, Agatha’s hand covering one of hers, Amelia beside them, pointing out various toys, pet clothing, and other accessories. It felt like they were a complete family, as it should be. 

“If I didn’t know any better, I’d say you’re enjoying this, Dr. Vidal,” Agatha commented, amused.

“I am. The dog is even growing on me.” Stopping the cart, she picked up a rope toy. “Think this would last long?”

 

Amelia took it from her Mama and gingerly turned it over in her hands, thoroughly inspecting it. “I think it would be okay while she’s young, though a bit older, and she’ll make short work of it. Is there a Magickal vet nearby in case she accidentally ingests something she shouldn’t?” Cosmically wise eyes met theirs.

Looking around to make sure no one had overheard, Agatha gave her a reassuring nod. “I know a witch, plus we can hound-proof whatever we get. Scratchy went through a griffin stage once. Only lasted a few days. I had to claw-proof everything.” 

Amelia giggled, though she didn’t seem completely convinced. 

“There’s another section of the store that has exactly what we need, cariño,” Rio resumed, pushing the cart and heading to the Magickal side of the store, noticing how Amelia relaxed immediately when they entered it.

 

“They have hellhound food! I wonder if we can subscribe and do delivery,” Agatha mused.

I’m worried about Amelia. She seems tense, especially when she does the wise cosmic being thing. Agatha’s voice came through their connection.

“That would be highly convenient. What flavor do you think she’d like, mija? Salmon? Buffalo?”

 

The small entity gathered her new friend in her arms and looked at the refrigerated case of food, whispering the different mixes in her ear.

Maybe it’s just from this morning and the changes? We’ll pick up lunch on the way home and see what’s bothering her. Rio sent back.

“The salmon and felon mix, buffalo and crooked politician mix, and hydra with unethical billionaire.” Amelia rattled off, her and Astraea having made their decisions.

 

“Good choices, you two. I wonder if I knew any of these people, or if I murdered any of them. Probably.” Agatha shrugged, placing several containers in the cart. “We’re going to have to get a separate fridge just for the dog.”

“Want me to text one of the kids and ask them to do it?” Rio asked nonchalantly. 

“Yes, my love.” Agatha leaned over and kissed her on the cheek.

 

Neither of them missed Amelia turning around and making a slight face at their blatant display of affection and how natural it all was.

It was Rio who found the Cerberus chew toys and tossed one in the cart. Agatha reached for a Grim Reaper one when Amelia cried out uncharacteristically.

“No!”

They both turned to look at her, surprised.

“I just don’t think it’s nice,” She avoided their eyes and shifted her weight between her feet. 

 

Something is wrong. Rio agreed.

And she won’t tell anyone, including Clea. Agatha surmised.

“We can grab a few hydra hides and liver treats and find a collar, okay, Plumita?” Agatha asked softly.

“Yes, mommy.” Her voice was barely audible, hugging Astraea to her chest.



Milly hadn’t said anything on the way home, even with lunch. Agatha pretended not to notice her daughter giving most of her food to one bottomless pit of a puppy. 

She was worried. If Amelia was accessing the Darkhold, what was she trying to do? What spell was she possibly trying to find? Her aversion to the chew toy had raised a red flag, and now Agatha wondered if her daughter had PTSD from what happened in Eternity’s realm. Or maybe it was because her mother was Death.

Returning home and dividing Astraea’s things between the two homes, Agatha had a brief moment of sadness. Why were they still living apart?

Because you’re not there yet. She thought to herself.

 

Sighing, she looked toward Wanda, who was eyeing the multiple bags. 

“Wow. You’re taking this dog thing seriously.” She observed, pulling out a bag of liver treats. “Well, having a hellhound around will make your body disposal easier.”

“I’ll still donate the good organs to those who need them. Has the new refrigerator been delivered?” She pulled Wanda into a hug.

“Yes, already set up downstairs, rush installation, the Harkness way.” She squeezed Agatha and stepped back. “Are you okay?”

“Help me put these away, and then I need your help in the other basement.”

 

The witch stared at the Darkhold. She could feel bits of Amelia’s Magick on it. She had indeed touched the sacred text. 

“You didn’t sense a glamour on her?” Wanda asked, standing beside her.

“No, so she hasn’t used it very much, maybe only once.” Agatha bit her fingernail, thinking about what to ask the book, and afraid to do it.

 

“What did you show Amelia Jade Harkness-Vidal?” Wanda asked.

The book opened and turned to the familiar soulmate page. 

“Okay, that’s not bad.” Wanda put a hand on Agatha’s shoulder, much like the older witch had done countless times before. “What did Amelia ask to see?” Wanda asked, turning to the book.

 

Pages turned again, landing on a mind-wiping spell. “How to forget a memory,” Wanda read in a whisper. 

Green eyes darted to Agatha. “Is she trying to forget that night?” 

Agatha pinched the bridge of her nose. “Yes, she is. She must have had nightmares. How have we not realized what’s been happening?”

“We’ll all be back for New Year’s. The three of you need time to yourselves.” Wanda concluded, leaving zero room for argument from her mother.

“Angel, you know I want you all to spend more time with us,”

 

“Agatha.” Wanda’s tone was firm. “Anytime Pietro and I showed signs of flashbacks, anxiety, or even remotely not adjusting to something well, you did the same thing. Not only did it help in our healing process, but it also gave us time to just be. She’s still getting to know you, most of us really. It’s what the three of you need. We’ll be back in a week.”

Agatha knew how lucky she was to have Wanda in her life, not just as a daughter, but as her friend. Wrapping her in a hug, she smiled into her hair. “I am so proud of the woman you’ve become.”



Milly had said her goodbyes to her family and watched the door shut behind them. She knew she wasn’t in trouble, not really. Her moms were worried. Lily and Wanda promised her a weekend in New York after the New Year and the Dads, a weekend on the island.

She also knew that her mom had figured out her secret. She just didn’t know how much of it she knew. 

Thankfully, she could shield her mind from her mothers, and neither of them would go feeling about it without permission. She just needed to know how much they knew. She walked back to the living room and curled into the corner of the couch, wrapping an arm around her four-legged companion. Her mothers took on a united front on the sofa caddy-corner from her, their respective familiars next to them. 

 

They were anxious themselves.

“Amelia, honey,” Agatha began. “I found your Magickal signature on the Darkhold.”

Amelia sucked in her breath. Well, she would confess to knowing the prophecy bit; she had accidentally let that slip anyway.

 

“What were you looking for?” Rio asked with the confidence of someone who already knew the answer.

“I wanted to know about your soulmate bond. That’s how I know you being together is written there, and you’re supposed to have more kids.” Her voice trembled, eyes trained on the floor. 

She felt her Mama’s hand on her back. “Mija, you’re not in trouble. We just want to understand.”

 

“What else did you ask to see?” Agatha’s voice was soft, though Amelia could tell she knew. 

“A memory loss spell. I just want to forget what happened. The souls, and killing.” Her lip trembled, looking between both parents. “I talked about it a little, but I just want to forget, and I can’t.”

Rio moved her into her lap, Agatha joining them. “It’s not your fault, Amelia.” Death reminded her.

Astraea had jumped down and now lay at Agatha’s feet, whining softly. 

 

“Raya agrees,” Agatha leaned over and kissed her forehead. “Plumita, my little love, it’s never going to hurt less if you don’t talk all the way through it. You experienced things no child should ever go through. It’s normal to need some time to process that, no matter how wise or ahead of your years you are.”

“I just thought I was supposed to adapt and move on. I’m this cosmic being and the daughter of Death and Agatha Harkness. Isn’t this supposed to be normal for me?” Her eyebrows stitched together.

“No,” they said in unison.

“I’m in charge of collecting and releasing souls, but that’s not your job. You did it that night, and we were very proud, but we don’t expect you to do it again.”

“And we may be Spirit Witches, but you don’t have to commune with the dead right now, nor do you have to kill people. I’m a succubus; I kill people to gain energy. It’s complicated. You don’t have to do that.” Agatha explained as simply as she could.

 

“We’ll get through this together, the three of us,” Rio assured, squeezing her.

Sniffing, she nodded slightly. “I like the nickname, Raya, and I’ll start talking about that night more.”

“That’s the only way we can move on. I know it’s hard, but bottling everything up only makes things worse. Trust me, I know.” Agatha smiled sadly.

 

Taking the chance to change the subject before they pried any further, she looked up at her Mama, “Where are we staying tonight?”

“Our house, mija. We’ll come back here on Sunday and resume our regular schedule, with a few deviations for New Year’s.” 

“Raya is coming with us, right?” Amelia asked, slightly worried her Mama would say no.

“Of course she is. She’s still small. Go ahead and gather anything you want to take back to the house.”

Knowing that meant her mothers wanted to talk, she went up to her room to gather her belongings.



Rio turned to Agatha immediately. “Well?”

“I’ll see what I can do about better securing the Darkhold.” Leaning into Rio, she lay her head on her shoulder. “She’s more like me than the other four, and it’s terrifying.” 

“I’m well aware. I can tell it’s bothering you that you’re not in control of the situation and that you’re worried that she’s quite possibly smarter than you.” She ran her nails through her hair, lightly scratching her witch’s scalp. 

“You know me so well, my love.” Lifting her head, she kissed the entity firmly.

Rio deepened the kiss, knowing it’d be two days before she’d get to do it again. At the sound of their daughter’s footsteps, she slowly ended it and pulled away. “I’ll see you Sunday.”

🦕🦖🦕

Rio sat against the headboard of her bed, journal open in her lap. Milly was sound asleep in her bed, curled up with Astraea. Victoria was off in her enclosure for the night, and Rio was already into June in the journal.

She’d cried several times as Agatha had gone into detail about her decision to date Darcy and her growing feelings for her. Lily had left for Soria already, and the witch was settling into Boston, adjusting to the changes.

June 30th 2025,

She told me she loved me today.

I couldn’t say it back.

She told me she understands and that I can take as much time as I need. What scares me is that she's someone I could fall in love with, but I know it won’t be the same. 

I will never love someone the way I love loved Death. I suspect that Darcy knows that. 

I have been honest with her since the beginning, and she accepts it all and loves me anyway.

She truly deserves better.

The good thing about it all is that she can’t hurt me the way Rio did. 

She can’t love me the way she did, and can, either.

I still dream of her. I still have nightmares of what happened.

Lily is in Spain with her. I haven’t heard from my youngest at all, and Lilia doesn’t tell me much other than “she’s having a life-changing summer”—cryptic loon. 

I always thought that when/if Rio and I were ever to marry, she’d be Lily’s favorite mom, much like Ian is her favorite dad. 

I often wonder if I gave up on waiting too early. If I should have chased after her harder. Clea says I had to do what was best for my personal healing journey. 

I have to remember that it was pretty damn final.

I still miss her. I’ve just learned to accept it.

 

Rio turned the page, her chest tightening. It had already been obvious how much Agatha had been gaslighting herself, but reading the entries and feeling the pain in the words was different. It drove it in that much further.

Rio quickly realized she had nothing to worry about when it came to who Agatha was in love with and where her loyalty lay.

 

When it came time for Amelia to return to Agatha’s, Rio had read up to August. She had stopped right before they reunited, knowing that she needed to wait until she was completely alone for that. So far, she had read about how much care and love that damn infant had put into piecing Agatha back together. At the same time, Rio’s name kept coming back up as Agatha accepted it.

Somehow, she hadn’t been upset with Agatha, reading it from her point of view. No, the only reason she was currently upset as she walked into Agatha’s house was that their daughter had been allowed to have a demon dog.

“Agatha Harkness!” Rio yelled out, annoyed. Amelia took her cue, running straight through the house and into the backyard with the blasted mutt at her heels. 

Rio, however, went straight to the kitchen table and dumped a bag of assorted items onto it. 

 

Walking into the kitchen from her office, Agatha surveyed the random debris on her table. Glancing at Rio with a slight look of confusion, she gestured toward the pile. “What the fuck is all this, Feathers?”

“THAT,” Rio pointed, “Is what that hellbeast has chewed on, slobbered over, and otherwise destroyed in the less than forty-eight hours it has been in my house!” 

“She has a name, Rio.” Her voice held a hint of amusement. “Astraea’s a puppy, my love. She’s going to chew on things. What did you do when Milly was teething?”

She huffed, hands on her hips. “I gave her teethers, Agatha. A dog is different. Our daughter didn’t chew on three Mastodon teeth, my favorite Docs, shred a set of robes, splintered the bed frame WHILE I WAS SLEEPING, tore through my favorite pillow, destroyed gardening tools and gloves, ate some of my garden while destroying it—”

 

“I think you owe her an apology,” Agatha interrupted. 

“That’s not even all of it!” Rio threw her hands in the air. “She tore the garden up to bury the femur of Patagotitan mayorum!”

Agatha’s jaw dropped. “She’s barely fifteen pounds, Rio! She’s the size of a standard Pomeranian! That’s an eight-foot-long bone! Where did you stash that thing anyway? No wonder she needed a snack!”

“It doesn’t matter, and I don’t know how, but she did! Then, she brought me a dead rat!” Rio was seething, black mist rising around her.

“She brought you a present!” The witch stepped closer and snaked an arm around her waist. “I’m sure she’ll do the same things here, and you can gloat all you want when you return on Wednesday.”

“I hope she eats your shoe collection,” Rio grumbled, relaxing into Agatha’s arms.

“No, you don’t, you’re just upset. In a few days, the roles will be reversed.” 

🦕🦖🦕

December 31, 2025

The roles had not been reversed. 

“What do you mean she was a perfect angel?” Rio gritted out, having returned from her realm. 

“Just that. She didn’t chew on anything she wasn’t supposed to. Perfect little lady, right Amelia?” Agatha asked, chopping up vegetables for a salad she was making. 

“She played with her toys and chewed on her hydra hides.” Milly’s head turned sharply when she heard the door open, and her nephews call for her.

“Go play, Plumita.” 

 

Three sets of feet thundered up the stairs, followed by the unmistakable thump of oversized paws.

Wanda and Zora joined them in the kitchen, and Agatha immediately washed her hands and stole Gabby away from her daughter-in-law.

Rio leaned against the counter, arms crossed. “Why does that demon hate me?”

 

Taking over meal prep for Agatha, Wanda smirked. “If I recall correctly, you spent most of Christmas trying to send her back. Aggie gave her liver and belly rubs.”

“She likes you because you spoil her,” Rio concluded.

Zora exchanged a look with her wife. “It’s so cute when they bicker.”

“Should we plan a spring wedding or are we thinking summer?” Wanda wondered out loud.

 

Agatha rolled her eyes, emitted a low whistle, and waited a few moments. 

Astraea came trampling down the stairs, nearly tripping over her own paws, and stood directly in front of her, tail wagging.

“Sadni si pekne.” Agatha commanded, still holding Gabby.

The pup’s tail stopped wagging as she sat perfectly, awaiting her next command.

 

“So you taught her basic commands in a different language? Probably in a sequence?” She stood beside Agatha, “Astraea, lay down.”

 

Astraea didn’t move, eyes trained on Agatha.

“Guāi nǚhái. Zhuànquān.”

Turning in a perfect circle and returning to her sitting position, the well-trained hellhound looked very pleased with herself when Agatha rewarded her with a piece of kidney.

“We haven’t worked on supernatural powers. Amelia seemed hesitant to do so just yet. We’ve covered the basics in fifty different languages, including Latin, Greek, Sumerian, Norse, sign language, and Reaper.” She explained. “Plus a few non-basics. She’s such a good girl for mommy.” Kneeling, she scratched behind Astraea’s ears and whispered praises in Spanish before sending her back upstairs. 

 

A laugh finally burst forth from Wanda. “Rio, I think Mom wins this one.” 

“I’m going to see where everyone else is.” Rio turned on her heel, face flushed, and stormed from the room. 



Lilith was concerned for her little sister. Agatha shared everything with Wanda, who in turn shared everything with her. She was also very observant, like the rest of the Harkness clan, and had ears everywhere. 

One of the things she had noticed during recent visits was Amelia’s rare use of Magick. She had once used it constantly, and now, that was dwindling. 

Like most witches, Lily’s Magick hadn’t come in until she was thirteen. However, like her mother, she had taught herself analog Magick and runes and was determined to be as powerful as Agatha and, one day, Wanda. She couldn’t imagine what it would have been like to have full powers at her baby sister’s tender age.

Amelia, however, was more powerful than she would ever be. She wasn’t jealous, however. She worried about her the same way she worried about Wanda. Cosmic beings had their own set of problems, and to be handed that at birth, not to mention the first four and a half years of her life? 

Lily needed therapy just thinking about it. Then there was that whole 'much older mind in a preschooler's body' issue. Sometimes, even she wasn’t sure how to approach her baby sister. Right then, she went with the regular older sister approach.

She found Milly taking a break in her room. It was after dinner, and while most of the family was downstairs, her sister had snuck away with her new best friend.

“She’s gotten bigger since Christmas,” Lily said softly, entering the dimly lit room.

“That’s what puppies do, Lilith.” She retorted from where she was curled up in a pile of floor pillows and stuffed animals.

“Spoken like a true Harkness.” Taking a seat next to them and pulling her knees to her chest. “You seem off lately.”

“There’s no right when you’re an abnormality,” She said dryly.

Lily flinched. “Straight to the point. I see therapy’s going well.”

“Did you ever want to be normal? No powers? Normal brain?” Looking up at her sister, her blue eyes were full of questions.

Lily thought about it for a moment. “I didn’t grow up with my powers; I taught myself spells and old school Magick, and runes instead. Nicky didn’t care. He could mix a potion, but that was about it. I was precocious, like you, but not as wise. I wanted to be normal in the sense that I didn’t want to be sick all the time.”

“You were sick?”

The older Harkness nodded. “Evanora, Mom’s mom, she bound Mom when she was still young, took all of her Magick. I know you’ve been told some of the story, but she was under all of these different rules. Evanora used us against Mom, kept us sick all the time. Anytime Mom did something against Evanora or out of line, she activated the illness. It’s why Mom stole Rio’s Magick to restore hers and break the curse. It was the only way she could.” Lily flicked a tear away. 

“It’s more complicated, Eternity got involved, the island happened, anyway, yes. I wished then that I had been in a normal witch family so Mom and Rio could be together. However, if Evanora hadn’t been the way she was, we wouldn’t be here. Mom wouldn’t have been forced to marry Ralph; we wouldn’t have this family. So, yes, and no. 

 

Milly pulled Astraea closer to her, petting her short fur. “I wish I were normal. Or at least a normal witch kid.”

Lily tilted her head, “What do you mean, Green Goblin?” 

Clutching Sir Cuddles close, she sighed deeply. “I love our family, and I wouldn’t trade it, but I don’t like having Magick this young, and being an entity isn’t fun. Do you think I LIKE being on par with adults? I can do calculus. I don’t want to do calculus. I want to be mystified when two different colors make a third color, Lily.” Swallowing hard, she looked up at her sister, blue eyes shining with tears. “I have access to the majority of knowledge in the universe. I want a sense of child-like wonder. I try so hard to think like a four-year-old and be four, but it’s hard. I want to watch Sesame Street and actually learn something.” 

 

Lily was positive that the pieces of her heart needed to be swept off the hardwoods of her sister’s bedroom floor with her admission. She had never once thought of just how grown-up being a cosmic entity actually made Amelia; none of them had.

Entities were made, they weren’t born.

Lily knew the answer, but she asked anyway. “Have you told—”

“No, and you won’t either.” Amelia’s eyes swirled purple and green.

She held her hands up in surrender. “I’m not going to, but you need to. Two questions, you seem to have matured more intellectually. You still seemed somewhat child-like before, and now it slips in and out. Why?”

“I went to a cosmic realm. I used cosmic abilities. It was a slow activation, but it activated.” Amelia shrugged.

“Why hasn’t anyone else noticed? Lorna hasn’t said anything, Lilia hasn’t seen anything.” Lily’s mind was racing, trying to come up with answers.

“I’m the first and only of my kind, Lilith. Why would they? I’ve also tried to keep up the front, it’s just getting harder.” She turned her attention back to her stuffed rabbit, still clutching him like any other child of her chronological age.

“Why haven’t you told them?” Lily’s voice had softened even further. She knew why.

“I don’t want them to blame themselves and drift apart again. They deserve to be happy,” came her simple response.

 

Lily moved to sit next to her sister and wrapped her arms around her. “They won’t. They love you and each other very much. They will come up with a solution that will make everyone happy.”

“Do you think that’s possible?”

“They brought dinosaurs back to life, of course, it is Green Goblin.” She tickled her sides. “We have to tell them tonight.”

Amelia pulled back and scrunched her nose. “In the morning, when everyone is rested.” 

Lily narrowed her eyes. “Okay, fine, but only because it’s getting late and we know they’ve started drinking and telling stories.”

“They’re also probably kissing.” Amelia stuck her tongue out in disgust.

The young woman laughed. “See, you’re not all grown up and intellectual.”          

🦕🦖🦕

Rio was too old to stay up until midnight. Well, she wasn’t, but she was growing impatient. She had read up to October in the journal and knew she was staying the night. So what if the house was full of people? It was time for Agatha to take her to bed. 

Reading the entries from August had been difficult. Agatha had been ecstatic about Amelia, more so than she ever showed to Rio. In her journals, she only blamed herself, and while there was mention of her ex and the feelings she had for the mortal, it was mostly Rio. That continued in September and October. So much had been about how much love and concern she had for her family. The fears she had, the dreams she had for the future. 

Rio was sure her tears had stained the pages, and Agatha? She was a master manipulator and liar, but the woman’s writing was where she told the truth. Agatha’s comfort emotion was always anger. It’s what she showed the world. Within the pages, however, she allowed herself to be raw, honest, vulnerable. Rio needed to show her how she felt about it.

Then there was the head menace herself, constantly touching Rio throughout the night. Trailing fingers down her arm, brushing their shoulders together, holding her hand. Who did this bitch think she was?

 

To which Rio had to remind herself that this was indeed her girlfriend and the biological parent of their daughter. 

The daughter who had seemingly disappeared from their small party. “Baby, have you seen Milly?” She asked, sipping her champagne. 

“No,” Agatha glanced around the room, eyes landing on her and Lily coming down the stairs. “Ah, there are two of our girls. Everything okay?”

“Yes, Mommy.” Milly beamed. “Just talking to Lily, I’m taking Raya outside.”

 

“Put your coat on!” Rio called after her, turning back to Lily, black heart slightly skipping a beat at how Agatha had referred to both of the kids. “Is everything okay? She’s seemed different, lately.”

Lily waved it off. “Just overwhelmed, and a little boy is bothering her at school. She’s a little anxious to go back. That’s all.” She locked eyes with Agatha for a few minutes longer before wandering off. 

 

“What was that about?” Rio turned to Agatha.

“She knows something, they’ll tell us in the morning, but she has it under control,” Agatha responded without missing a beat. “Now, I have a few more hours until midnight before I cuddle up next to you and fall asleep peacefully.”

Stepping into her, Death lowered her voice, “Oh, is that what we’re doing?”

Pushing a strand of hair behind Rio’s ear and dragging one finger down her neck and across her collarbone, she hummed. “Well, of course, we have a house full of guests, Feathers.”

Taking a step closer, she leaned next to the witch’s ear. “That didn’t stop you on Christmas.” 

 

Before she could reply, Lilia breezed in. “Get a room already. No one cares, well, your youngest does, but only that you’re together.”

“Sharon, gather your wife, she’s been in the Moonshine again,” Ian called out, joining them. 

“Oh, quit, I’m allowed to poke fun. These two owe me a month-long vacation at this point.” The older witch waved them off. 

 

Amelia ran back through the room with the boys in a much better mood than before. Rio let out a breath of relief she wasn’t aware she was holding and turned back to the conversation, more relaxed.

🦕🦖🦕

Midnight had come and gone, kisses and wishes for the New Year had been shared, and all living creatures had finally settled for the night. Amelia had fallen asleep using the dog as a pillow and was now safely in her bed.

 

Entering their bedroom, Agatha had immediately gone to the bathroom and begun her bedtime routine, Rio doing the same. 

Even when she was married to Ralph, Agatha always had her own room, her own bathroom, and so on. She had been the same with her ex, mostly referring to it as hers, not theirs. With Rio, she found it was easy to make the switch. It was automatic. 

What felt odd to her was that Rio still lived in another house and not with her. Now that most everything was in the open, she was ready to speed up their relationship. 

So what if there were a few things left to say?

 

They could worry about all of that later. Right now, she had other plans. Rio had already taken to bed, having a much shorter routine. Agatha, on the other hand, tied her short black satin robe around her and padded to the bed after fussing with her hair.

 

“What do you think you’re doing, babygirl?” She purred, standing at the foot of the four-poster bed.

Rio’s mouth held the hint of a smile. “I thought we were going to sleep, Agatha.” Big doe eyes looked up at her innocently.

“Did you think I didn’t notice your thighs clench every time I called the puppy a good girl? Or the way every touch caused goosebumps to erupt on your skin?” The corner of her mouth barely twitched at the sound of Rio’s breath hitching. “That’s what I thought.” She pulled the tie holding her robe close and let the fabric slip off her shoulders and fall to the floor, revealing the lavender, lace thong and matching bralette she was wearing. 

“Not to mention the way you bit your lip when I called myself ‘mommy’ earlier.”

Rio’s eyes raked over her, lips slightly parted, taking in the sight of her in lingerie. “You look amazing.”

“And you’re overdressed.” She pointed at the middle of the bed. “Naked, kneeling position, babygirl.”

 

Quickly removing her oversized t-shirt and shimmying out of her underwear, she crawled to the spot Agatha had indicated, kneeling, with her hands on her thighs.

“It’s been so long since I’ve had you like this. Since I said those two words that you crave.” Agatha had joined Rio on the bed and knelt in front of her, lightly running her hands and fingertips over her body.

The witch leaned forward, kissing Rio slowly, hands still roaming over her body. It’s been so long since my pretty girl called me Mommy. 

Rio’s fingers twitched, and Agatha smiled against Rio’s mouth, deepening the kiss. For remembering the rules, you may touch me. 

“Thank you, Mommy,” Rio sighed, but before she could get her hands on the witch in front of her, Agatha pulled away.

 

“On your back,” Agatha commanded, humming in delight when Death immediately obeyed. Her eyes grazed over her lover’s body, stopping on her glistening cunt. “Already wet for me, I see.” Dragging two fingers through her heat, she gathered the slick on her fingers and brought them to her lips, moaning at the taste.

“I missed tasting you whenever I wanted.”

Rio reached toward her desperately. “Aggie, I need to kiss you at least, please.” She nearly begged, squeezing her breasts, thumbs circling her pebbled nipples through the lace of the bralette.

Slotting her thigh against the entity, she leaned forward, crashing their mouths together, allowing Rio to taste herself on her lips and tongue. Agatha pressed and rocked her thigh against Rio’s core, eliciting soft moans that she eagerly swallowed.

 

“Touch me,” she rasped, driving her thigh harder into her.

Although she was still playing with her nipples, she knew what the woman above her meant. Rio held Agatha to her with one arm and ran the other hand down the woman’s body, slipping her hand past the waistband of the lace panties and through her folds, immediately finding her hardening clit. 

“Fuck yourself against my thigh. You can come when I do.” Agatha instructed, keeping pace with Rio. Sucking on Death’s tongue, she pulled back, kissing down her neck and working the spot where her shoulder met her throat. 

 

Rio’s fingers moved from Agatha’s clit to her entrance, circling slowly, her hips already losing their rhythm. 

“Oh, that’s my good girl,” she purred, two fingers pushing into her, causing her to gasp. “Yes, babygirl. Fuck Mommy.”

Rio humped against Agatha’s thigh faster and harder, chasing her own pleasure. Her hardened bud spread wetness over Agatha. She slid a third finger into Agatha, curling against the soft, spongy spot inside of her. Her palm ground against her clit.

Agatha cried out, feeling Rio’s movement stutter against her. “Rio, I’m going to—”

“Me too, Aggie.” Leaning forward, she bit into Agatha’s shoulder and let out a muffled scream.

 

Agatha thrust her hips forward, opening her mouth with a silent scream, before collapsing on top of her soulmate.

“That’s Mommy’s good girl.” She whispered into her ear.

Smiling against her shoulder, she gently withdrew her hand from Agatha, yelping in surprise when Agatha took her hand, licking the digits clean with a satisfied hum. 

 

“I want you on all fours when I get back,” She instructed, kissing Rio filthily before leaving the bed and going to the closet.

“Yes, Agatha.” Rio rolled onto her stomach to get herself situated as she heard the distinct sounds of her soulmate stepping into her harness. Heat began to pool again as she felt Agatha return to the bed and settle behind her.

“Did you enchant it again?” 

“No, the last time I did that, you ended up pregnant.” The witch stroked the tip of the toy against Rio’s sensitive core.

 

“Oh,” she gasped. The sensation was pleasurable, though she was slightly disappointed.

The witch tangled a hand in Death’s hair, leaning forward to kiss her shoulder. “One day, babygirl,” She whispered before slowly pushing into her and bottoming out, pulling back, and repeating the action.

“Look at that pussy, still greedy for me,” Agatha growled, driving in faster, muscle memory taking over. “Someone missed this.”

“Yes, Agatha.” Rio moaned, Agatha pumping into her harder. “All yours,” she added, unprompted.

 

The witch groaned at the admission, the base of the strap grinding against her clit. Her fingers dug into Rio’s hips hard enough to leave bruises, one hand sliding around to stimulate the neglected bundle of nerves.

“I’m so close,” Agatha confessed, pressing Rio down into the mattress.

Rio’s second orgasm hit her all at once, and she rose to her knees, pressing against Agatha’s chest, turning her head to kiss her, teeth and tongues colliding as Rio continued to ride the waves of her pleasure. Agatha held her closely as her own body began to convulse, and they both collapsed on the bed in the afterglow.

 

Minutes passed before Agatha could gather the energy to pull out of Rio, discarding the harness and fetching a soft washcloth to clean them both.

Rio looked up at her witch with half-lidded eyes after being offered water. “Did you mean what you said? About more kids one day?”

Agatha pulled her close and hummed. “It is foretold,” she joked, her tone becoming more serious. “Do you want that?”

“Yes, Agatha.” She nuzzled deeper into her arms and against her soft skin with a contented sigh, falling into a deep slumber.        

🦕🦖🦕

Amelia waited until she was sure everyone was asleep, or at least in their rooms for the night, before she appeared in the enchanted part of the basement. It was warded, but as smart as her mother was, she hadn’t thought to ward it against her children's astral projections.

In her parents’ defense, neither one of them knew Amelia could project like that. It was yet another reason why she didn’t want these powers. It was too much for someone her age. 

Summoning the Darkhold, she quickly got it to open. In her Astral form, it took her for a cosmic being, not four-year-old Amelia. 

 

It was easy to get what she needed this time, absorbing the spells to memory and sending the tome back to its dimension. 

She didn’t even leave a Magickal signature.

She really was too powerful and smart for her own good, she thought.

 

Before returning to her body, she stopped by Lily’s room and quietly slipped in. “I know we need to tell them, but I have it under control,” she whispered, before using the memory spell to remove any trace of the conversation they had hours before.

Sinking back into her body, she relaxed slightly, knowing that after the full moon, everything would be exactly the way it should be. 

 

January 2, 2026

Agatha was relieved that the holidays were over. Not because she hadn’t enjoyed them, but because she was ready to have time that wasn’t packed with events. She was even going to get a few days by herself while Amelia spent the weekend in the city and came back in time to go to Rio’s for the week.

Not that Agatha thought they should go to Rio’s. 

“Have everything you need, Plumita?” She asked, checking over her suitcase and backpack.

“Yes, mom.” She sighed like she was fourteen, not four, her hair tamed in two braids.

“You listen to your siblings and Zora? Understand?” 

“Yes, Mom. I’ll be fine. Take care of Raya?” She asked in return. 

“Of course.” She hugged her tightly and kissed the top of her head, turning to Lily and Nicky. Everyone else had already returned to their respective homes. “Call me if anything happens. Be careful, no parties or multiverse surfing.” She hugged them both, kissing their cheeks, unable to reach the tops of their heads.

“Yes, Mom.” They replied in unison.

 

“You have Sir Cuddles and your blanket?” She asked, double-checking.

Milly held them up as evidence.

“Alright, call me tonight.”

Purple, blue, and green mist swirled, and they were gone.

Agatha looked down as Astraea let out a whine. “I know, sweet girl, it’s just us and Scratch for a few days. We’ll find something to do.”

 

January 3, 2026

Amelia was having fun in New York and wanted to return for a longer visit. She was enjoying the family time with her siblings, nephews, and niece. Saturday, they spent time in Central Park and went to the zoo. Even just spending time indoors was special to her until she had to take a break from being overwhelmed.

Wanda had tried to ask her about it, and she just told her she was tired.

When really she had a headache and heard too many people whispering from different directions at once. 

 

That night, she was staying the night with Wanda and Zora. She had hoped to stay with Lily and Nicky; her Mama’s old apartment would have been better for what she needed, but the Scarlet Witch’s place would work just as well.

Unable to Astral Project this time, she teleported herself to the rooftop. It probably wasn’t her smartest move, but hopefully she’d be able to get back inside. Standing beneath the full moon, she began to recite the various spells to suppress her powers and what made her an entity. 

As she chanted the words, wind whipped around her as her vision blurred and the ground gave way beneath her. The last thing she saw was a flash of red hair before everything faded to black.

🦕🦖🦕

Agatha was explaining Drag Race to Astraea when the blood in her veins turned to ice. Her hands took on a dark purple glow, responding to a powerful spell from the Darkhold being used.

Amelia.

Somehow, she had accessed another spell, though Agatha wasn’t sure which one; she couldn’t sense it from this distance and immediately reached out to Wanda.

LA Basement.

They appeared simultaneously in the sacred space. It was the only place that held everything they could need and was runed appropriately. Agatha was already running through the possibilities of what Amelia could have possibly cast upon herself.

Wanda laid Amelia’s limp form on a surgical table that appeared in the middle of the room, Rio joining them and taking in the scene.

The Darkhold levitated next to Agatha, pages flying as she hurried to Rio’s side, next to Amelia. Thankfully, Rio was dressed casually, no flower in hand. She wasn’t there to collect; she was there because their daughter needed both of them. 

Taking Death’s hand in hers, Agatha turned to Wanda as she began to explain. 

 

“I caught her right before she collapsed on the roof of my building. Like you, I sensed a Darkhold spell being used.”

 

Rio took Amelia’s hand in hers, looking to Agatha. “Aggie, what could she have been doing?” 

 

Lily arrived with Ralph at the same time as Jen, Alice, and Lilia stepped into the basement, Jen’s hands already glowing.

The bond of the coven had alerted them all.

 

“Lily, come here.” Agatha’s voice faltered. She put two fingers to her daughter’s temple, starting to piece together the past few weeks. Pieces of conversations, behaviors, and therapy sessions. When she combined that with the Harkness mentality, a clear picture began to form. 

“Amelia erased a memory.” She mumbled, “She’s good, but she’s still learning.”

Finding where it had been suppressed, she projected it for everyone.

 

Hearing her four-year-old discuss wanting to block her Magick and separate her cosmic side ripped at her.

All Agatha had ever wanted was her powers. She had nearly destroyed her chance at happiness to get it, and here was her young child doing the opposite. 

 

“Mom, Rio, I’m so sorry. If I had known—” Lily started, the memory fading.

 

The Spirit Witch stood silent, but vigilant, eyes now locked on Amelia. She didn’t know when the tears had started, or when her fingertips had pressed against her lips, but they had.

Consciously, she knew that when Jen and Ralph had arrived, they began assessing the situation. Not only had Amelia tried to bind and suppress her powers, but she had tried to separate her cosmic form completely—an impossible feat. An IV had been started, Pietro kept the basement continuously stocked in case of emergencies, and Ralph was monitoring her vitals. 

 

Pietro had joined them and was discussing using their contacts to move her to Cedars-Sinai or another hospital.

 

Rio’s voice barely came through, consoling Lily and assuring her that it wasn’t her fault. Amelia was stubborn and headstrong; she’d find a way to use the spells anyway.

But how? How had Milly done it? There wasn’t a new signature on the damned book. Why hadn’t Milly talked to them? How had they missed this? Milly always had her moments of appearing older, hadn’t she? 

Had Eternity been right about Milly being dangerous? Too smart? Agatha quickly pushed that thought away. No, Amelia was perfect, like all of her children; she had just hit witchy puberty entirely too young, and they had to fix it.

 

A familiar hand at the small of her back finally pulled her out of her spiral. “Come back to me,” Rio whispered in her ear.

 

“Oh, sorry, I was just thinking of a plan.” Turning, she tossed her hair over her shoulder. This was not the first time one of her children had been ill, injured, or, in Pietro’s case, tried to die of her. “Jen, do something.” She commanded.

 

“I have, Agatha. While you were having your moment, the three of us were stabilizing your mini-me over here. This is exactly something you would do. Try to fix something yourself and not ask for help?”

“I don’t need a lecture right now, Jenny Kale.” She snapped.

 

“She’s right, Jennifer. I know you’re trying to lighten the mood, but the situation is still dire.” Lilia added, resting a hand on Amelia’s shin.
