Ralph cleared his throat. “We gave her a mild sedative. We can transfer back to Boston as long as Jen gathers whatever she used and we have the appropriate runes. I can get the equipment I need brought to your house if needed, and I’ll have Emma run a few brain scans. She technically zapped herself with lightning when she tried to separate instead of suppress. Her heart stopped for, I estimate, thirty seconds.”

 

“Because she tried to conduct the spell on herself and used Magick while trying to suppress Magick, it rubberbanded on itself, which caused the shock,” Jen added. “We need to watch her for a day or so. She still has her powers, but, Aggie, if she is this serious, you may need to think about,” the potions witch looked away, not finishing the thought.

Dragging a hand through her hair and rolling her shoulders back, she grunted. “Right. The thing I fought to get back my whole life, our daughter doesn’t want.” Exchanging a look with Rio, she sighed. “We’ll think about it.”       

Nicky, who had also apparated into the basement, pointed at the Darkhold, which had finally settled on a page. “What does it say?”

Agatha leaned over and sneered, floating the cursed object to Wanda, knowing Rio wouldn’t read from the book.

Reading over it, Wanda pursed her mouth and scrunched her nose. “I don’t know. It’s very specific, but she’d come into her powers again at thirteen and remain herself. It’s worth thinking about.”

Sighing for what had to be the tenth time that hour, she reached for Rio’s hand. “We’ll have to discuss it and talk to Milly about it.”

🦕🦖🦕

Agatha was seriously considering permanently keeping one guest room as a medical room. She was grateful they still had the LA house and the basement for emergencies, but the Boston house was now her home. She’d even written about it in the journal she had gifted to Rio.

Lying carefully next to Amelia, monitors beeping, she thought about the past several hours. She had needed Rio. The moment she knew their daughter was in danger, she immediately called out for her subconsciously through the bond; she knew she had.

Yet when her soulmate appeared by her side, the first thing she did was check for any sign that she was there on official business.

It was the one thing she had yet to completely accept about Rio Vidal being Lady Death. She took children to the next realm. Away from their parents, their families. How could a mother do that to another parent?

What about their children? Agatha took comfort in knowing that Amelia was immortal, but what about future children? Tragedies still happened in pregnancy and childbirth, not to mention childhood. Would their children give her permission to take them?

There were so many questions and doubts floating in and around her head. Knowing their children could still fall ill despite being half entity. If they suppressed Milly and any future child’s powers, then who knew what could happen?

“It’s a lot to think about.” She said out loud, knowing Milly was still in a comatose state, to allow her to heal.

 

“Agatha?” Rio whispered, slowly pushing the door open.

“I’m right here,” She answered, moving to a sitting position.

“It’s nearly five in the morning, you need to sleep. Do you want to trade?” Making her way to the bed, she sat on the edge, careful not to disturb their daughter. 

 

“No,” Agatha yawned. “I don’t want to leave, either. I know she’ll be asleep for a few days, but I don’t want her to be alone. I know she has her comfort items and three animals, but I wasn’t there before, and I am now.” She choked on a sob and allowed the woman in front of her to wrap her arms around her and pull her to her chest.

Rio held her there, letting the tears soak through her Harvard Paleontology shirt. “I felt her heart stop. I never want to feel that again.”

Agatha stiffened, afraid to sit up and look at her beloved. “Did her soul call to you?”

“No.”

 

Agatha’s voice caught in her throat as she choked out the question. “Would you? Would you have walked her across? If she had agreed, that is.”

Death hesitated, the air growing cold, before answering. “Yes, Agatha. It’s my job. I wouldn’t want to, and at this age? She wouldn’t say yes. But if she were not immortal, or if she agreed to go, then yes. I would do my job.” Pressing her lips to Agatha’s hair, a few tears falling into the thick dark locks, she added. “You know I would have to.”

Finally, leaning back to look at her, her brilliant blue eyes swollen from crying, her eyebrows furrowed, she asked the difficult yet simple question. “Why?”

 

Taking a deep breath, then blowing it out harshly, she cupped Agatha’s cheek, running her thumb over laughlines near her eye, a small smile creeping out for just a second. 

“While there are exceptions, everyone eventually runs out of time or will decide that they are ready, Agatha. Some people get more time than others, and it’s not fair. Especially when it’s children. I hate taking children the most. They’re innocent, and they have so much life ahead of them. War, domestic violence, car accidents, illness, there are so many causes every day, and every single one of them is awful, Agatha. It never gets easier. 

“The only comfort is knowing that I can talk to them while we’re walking, instruct them to kiss their loved ones again, and lead them to where they're healed or not in danger. I won’t say it’s a better place or that they’re happier, because most of the time, almost every child I collect would rather be with their families and friends.” Using her thumb, she wiped a tear from Agatha’s cheek before continuing. 

“When I was pregnant, babies were extremely difficult, hearing the parents? It never got easier. As a mom? I know I’m doing what I would want someone to do, taking care and loving my child until their final destination.”

 

“I still,” Agatha turned away, considering what she was about to ask. “Show me?”

Rio looked at Agatha as if she had morphed into Mephisto himself. “Show you? Take you with me on collections?”

“Yes, Rio. Right now. Just a few, I can’t take very many. I need to understand before,” She glanced away, unable to finish the sentence.

Standing, she took Agatha’s hand in hers. “You’re sure you’re okay leaving her?” 

Leaning over and kissing Amelia’s cheek, she nodded. “Just for a bit. I’ll let Ralph, Ian, and Ellie know they’re on parent and aunt duty for a bit.”



Rio wasn’t exactly sure how Agatha was going to react to the experience of collecting the souls of children, yet it had been the last request she had. Her witch hated the thought of any parent losing their child, and Rio knew it had to do with how ill the twins had been as children, and the constant threat of losing all four children. She would save the child dying from illness for the end. 

Their first stop was a teenage boy in a car accident. She knew all of this would affect Agatha, and she wasn’t looking forward to it. 

“Where are we?” Agatha asked as they stepped into a hospital teeming with far too much activity for the early morning hours of Sunday.

“The southeast, bordering on the midwest.” She answered, leading the way into an operating room where a sheet had just been pulled over the boy’s face, a doctor announcing a time of death. 

Before them stood the boy, sixteen, dressed in camo, with shaggy brown hair and green eyes, staring in disbelief at his body. Catching a glimpse of them, he turned, his eyes lighting up. “Are y’all angels? You’re smoking hot!”

 

Rio scoffed. Overconfident teenage boys and young men were never boring, at least. “No, Zach, but you are dead.”

Zach nodded. “I was hoping this was a dream, but the MILFs in my dreams are never as hot as you two.” He frowned. “What about Ben?”

“He lived. He’s in rough shape, but he made it.” Rio answered, tone even.

 

“Oh. I told Ben to slow down, you know? His truck is too old to take those backroads like that. But we were hunting and had a few beers.” His eyes glossed over as he explained what happened. “Can I see my parents and sisters?” 

Rio nodded, leading the way to the waiting room filled with teenagers, teachers, and the families of the two boys. A mother began to wail, four girls crying, and a father attempted to hold it together. 

Zach’s face crumbled. 

“Can I?” 

“Go hug them, kiss them on the cheek. They might feel it.” Rio instructed. “But then it’s time to go.”

He wandered through the group, stopping to hug a few others.

 

“Is it always like this?” Agatha asked, taking in the scene.

“For these types of tragedies? A lot of the time. He’s from a small, close-knit, farming community, and this is the closest major hospital. News traveled fast. Most of his teachers have known him since sixth grade, or longer if they live in town. Some of these teachers either went to school with his parents or taught them. There are more people outside.”

Zach returned, tears streaking his face. “Hey, uh, don’t tell the guys I’m crying,” he joked.

“Never.” Rio promised, “Ready?”

“Do you get a lot of yesses?” He asked skeptically.

“You’d be surprised.” She smirked.

 

“Then, no ma’am, but my mama taught me to be respectful.” 

“This from the kid who told us we were hot and called us MILFs,” Agatha said with a smirk.

“In my defense, ma’am, I was in shock at the whole being dead thing.” He attempted to apologize.

Rio shook her head. At least teenagers were usually amusing until the very end. “This way, Zach.”

Until they ran out of bravado.

 

He looked at both of them uneasily, following through a thick fog. “What happens now?” 

“I can’t tell you too much, but you go to the next realm, live a life there, and wait for your loved ones. Time passes differently. You’ll like it.” She assured.

“Do I have to go to like, death school? Since I’m still a kid?” He began to ramble, sensing they were getting closer.

“No school, silver lining.”

“Is it like they tell us in church? Where we get to look down and watch our loved ones?” His voice began to shake when they stopped at a wrought iron gate.

 

Death turned to him, still in her human form, and gave him a comforting smile. “Humans tell each other a lot of things to make death and the afterlife easier for themselves to accept. While the answer is no, you’ll always be able to feel them, and you’ll remember them. That never changes. Death is harder for the living than it is for the dead, but I assure you, everyone is going to pull together and make it through this. You left a positive mark on many lives, kid. None of them will ever forget you, but they’ll heal. They’ll always grieve, it’s human nature, but they’ll make it.”

He nodded, contemplating her words. “Alright. Then, I’m ready, and I’ll see them when it’s their time. Thanks, both of you.” 

The gate opened, and with a deep breath, the young boy stepped through and disappeared into the fog.

 

Rio pivoted to Agatha, “Are you ready for the next one?”

Agatha shook her head violently. “No, one was plenty. I absolutely understand, and I can hear the spirits on the other side of that gate, children included. I still don’t like it, but I understand. I can’t do a younger child, or any other one. I will leave that up to you. I want to get back to our child and never let her out of my sight.”

“It never gets easier, you know.” Rio took the witch’s hand, taking them back to the house, her clothes transforming into lounge clothes.

 

“I believe you.” Arriving right outside of Amelia’s temporary room, the door cracked open, she melted into Rio’s arms. “You’re very good at your job, and I’m sorry I disrespected it for all these years.”

“Wait, did Agatha Harkness just apologize to me, AGAIN? How many is that since August?” She pulled back, a stunned expression on her face.

“Knock it off, Feathers, or you won’t come for a month.” She growled before pulling her into a soft kiss. 

“Hey! Some of us have ears!” Ralph called out from inside the room.

🦕🦖🦕

January 7, 2026

It had been roughly four days since Amelia had tried to suppress her Magick, and she was finally deemed healed enough to taper off the sedatives. 

Rio sat next to the bed, reading the final entries of Agatha’s journal as the witch slept, an arm protectively slung around their daughter. She hadn’t been sleeping well since the incident, and Jen had finally drugged her, forcing her to relax and rest.

Astraea, now twenty-five pounds, lay stretched against Amelia’s other side, chewing on what Rio suspected was her shoe. She still refused to apologize to the beast, though she was beginning to think she owed her one.

The last several days had been a test of patience and their views on parenting. They began discussing how they would handle the inevitable conversation about Milly’s powers when she awoke, and ultimately, it would be their daughter’s decision—a hefty choice at any age, but especially for a four-year-old. Neither of them took suppressing her powers lightly, afraid it would alter who she was, but they could reverse it if needed, and if it prevented this from happening again, it was a chance they were willing to take.

Shifting in her seat, she turned to the last entry.

 

December 24th, 2026

My Rio,

As you’ve read throughout these and many other pages, I have come a long way.

We have come a long way.

We have loved, lost, and loved again.

We lost our way, myself especially.

I will not lie to you and say I didn’t love her during those few months. I did. But not the way I love you. Never the way I love you.

I told you in October that I was trying to forget you. The truth is, it’s impossible to forget you. You are literally etched, entwined, into the fabric of my being. I was created for you and only you.

Even if I weren’t, even if we were not bound by fate, you, my love, are impossible to forget.

I see you everywhere I look. You’re in the research I do daily, the papers I write, the journals I read. You’re in every raindrop that falls on my skin and streak of lightning that crosses the sky. 

I now, finally, have a chance at a life with you, and I would be a fool to throw it away yet again. 

It’s you. It’s always been you, and it will always be you. We’ve done things in a very non-traditional way, but Lady Death, just as I told you that night in a fit of passion and anger, I want it all.

You, Amelia, marriage, (more) kids. 

I love you. I always have, and I always will. 

I tried to belong to someone else when really, I am yours. Completely.

 

Rio hadn’t noticed the tears rolling down her cheeks until one hit the page. 

“I mean it, you know. Every word.”

 

Startled, the book slipped from her fingers and fell to the floor with a soft thump. “I didn’t realize you were awake.” She sniffed, wiping her eyes.

Agatha carefully moved to sit in the entity’s lap and lay against her. “I didn’t want to interrupt.”

Adjusting in order to see her witch, she ran a hand through her thick waves. “Agatha, I—”

 

The sound of movement and a small groan had both of them practically leaping from the chair and to the other side of the room. Scratchy took one look at Agatha and vanished to retrieve Jen.

“Mama? Mommy?” Amelia’s voice was hoarse from sedation.

“Baby, we’re right here,” Agatha took a small hand in hers and brushed her hair out of her face.

 

Standing next to Agatha, Rio put her hand atop hers. “You frightened us, cariño.”

“I’m sorry,” she rasped out, Jen and Ralph entering the room, trying to work around the women standing bedside.

“We’re just happy you’re here, and awake, Plumita.” She bent over, pressing a kiss to her forehead.

 

Milly didn’t feel any different when she awoke. Her head was fuzzy, and she hurt in different places, but she still felt her Magick flowing through her, and could still access what she called the Universal Knowledge Database. 

Her plan had failed, and she had scared her family in the process. She could see it in their eyes as they visited and fussed over her. Once awake, she healed rapidly, yet her mothers were unconvinced. She had finally woken up in the early evening, and by early afternoon on January eighth, she felt physically fine.

It was very hard to fool Agatha Harkness. 

The matriarch had made everyone return to their normal lives, while she and Rio continued to watch over her, both of them still on break. 

 

Milly was sitting on the couch, reading a book about the migration pattern of the African Swallow, when her parents entered the room and sat on either side of her.

“Amelia,” Rio started, gently taking the book out of her hands. “I think it’s time we discussed this.

“Do we have to?” She asked, looking up at both of them.

“Yes, Plumita. You tried to cast a very serious spell. We saw the memory of the conversation with your sister, but will you tell us why?” Agatha turned to lean against the armrest to see Milly better.

 

The young witch’s shoulders dropped as she curled into herself. “It’s hard to have all of this power and knowledge. The other kids can barely read, and I’ve read classic books. My speech is perfect if I’m not acting. Did you know I touched a dead bird one day at school, and it flew away? Try explaining that to kids who suck their thumbs.” She drew her knees to her chest.

She missed the look her moms shared before they looked back at her.

 

“Did this happen in Soria, mija?” Rio’s hand cupped over her shoulder, an attempt at comfort.

“Not as much, but the knowledge and powers weren’t this strong until after Auntie Eternity’s,” she spat the name, “realm. In Soria, I spent a lot of time creating illusions or traveling the… never mind.” 

“We know you traveled the multiverse, Amelia Jade,” Rio confirmed.

“Oh. Well, I did that a lot.” She hugged her knees tighter and turned her head toward Rio. “Cosmic beings are created; they don’t have childhoods, so I couldn’t talk to you about it.”

 

Agatha cleared her throat, garnering Amelia’s attention. “Come here, Plumita.”

Amelia hesitated before allowing her to wrap her arms around her and pull her close.

 

“Magick and power were the one thing I always craved. I didn’t get mine when I was thirteen; my mother bound me. You know that. Instead, I taught myself what I could. However, I was always more intelligent and mature than everyone around me.” The familiar smirk adorned her face. “I still am.” 

Milly’s smirk matched hers.

“I know what it’s like to be leagues ahead of everyone else and feel like you don’t belong, honey. Lilia helped me with that a lot. She let me talk her ear off or gave me extra assignments whenever I wasn’t challenged enough. We would discuss whatever topic I was on about at the time. We’re all here for you in that. I also understand just wanting to be a child, and we both support that.”

 

“We also support you if you want to hold off on having powers,” Rio added, hesitating slightly.

Blue eyes widened, looking between her mothers. “What? You would let me suppress my powers?”

“Witches don’t get their gifts until they’re thirteen, normally. I consulted the Darkhold. You would still be you, though your cosmic and witch powers would go dormant for nine, well, a little over eight, years. You would be intelligent, but not this all-knowing being. Like you were before you went to a cosmic realm.” Agatha explained, keeping her eyes locked on Amelia’s.

 

“Would I be able to do any Magick? What about dream-walking? Could I still hear Señor Scratchy and Victoria? Transfer? Portal?” Her mind was racing. She wanted this, of course, she did; however, there were a few perks to having abilities.

“You will keep all of your memories, no to dream-walking for a few years. You will be able to speak with the familiars, and while you can’t transfer, you can portal, use runes, and teach yourself analog Magick and runes.” Her Mama clarified. “No resurrections though.”

 

She thought it over, the positives and negatives generating in her mind as she looked at her hands. The purple and green Magick, the perfect mix of the two women who had created her, would be absent for the next eight years, but it was a sacrifice she was willing to make. 

“Yes. I want to go back to who I was before I went to that place, and I just want to enjoy being a kid.” She decided, with a determined nod. 

 

Agatha processed her daughter’s decision, letting the words soak into her skin. Clapping her hands together, startling both the miniature version of herself and Rio, she nudged Amelia out of her lap to stand. “Let’s get started, shall we?”

“Don’t we need a full moon?” Milly’s eyebrows crinkled in confusion.

“You might, but I don’t.” She smiled warmly, taking the junior entity’s hand. “Now, for this, we do need the special basement, and the rest of the coven.” Winking, she transferred them to the ancient, enchanted basement beneath their LA home. Thankfully, someone had been in to clean up the aftermath from the previous weekend.

 

“This one is way bigger than the basement in Boston,” she observed, taking in the various potions and ingredients lurking in the shadows.

“It’s where the coven does some of its harder work,” Rio explained, noting a shelf of souls and another of various prehistoric ingredients. She hadn’t exactly paid attention the last time she had been there. 

Souls, Agatha?

Ignore those, not important. Agatha responded, finding the pages in the Darkhold as their coven members appeared.

 

“Oh? We’re doing this?” Lily asked, hugging her baby sister. 

“I think it’s for the best, enjoy your youth, kiddo,” Lilia advised, picking up a crystal and taking her place.

“Easier to protect if you’re not running across multiverses.” Alice grinned. “Of course, I knew you could.”

“Just, stay on the ground, don’t break anything. You’ll still heal quickly, but I need a break.” Jen looked across to Agatha, “Couldn’t let your own kid outsmart you?”

“Go to hell, Jen.” She shot back, not lifting her eyes from the book in front of her.

“I’ll save you a seat,” She smirked, assuming her position next to her wife.

Sharon bent down beside Milly, “I’ll be charging up Sir Cuddles and your blanket with your Magick in case you need it. Our secret.”

Amelia giggled and nodded.

Wanda knelt beside her sister, “Cosmic being to cosmic being, I’m always here if you need to talk, you know.”

Milly nodded, hugging her. “Thank you for saving me,” she whispered.

 

Rio took in the scene before her, preparing to bind her own daughter essentially. It was something she never thought she’d do, but it made sense. A child of her age didn’t need to be as powerful as she was or have as much knowledge as she had. Pre-cosmic realm, it hadn’t been as obvious, but now? Amelia spoke like an adult at times. 

Death often wished she had experienced a full life span, and while she did still gain knowledge from new discoveries, it was rare. 

The knowledge she gained was from interactions with others, relationships, and everyday life experiences. It’s what she wished for Amelia and their future children. 

Joining the circle of women, Amelia in the middle, she joined hands with Agatha on one side and Lilia on the other. As they began chanting the spell that would suppress their daughter’s powers until her thirteenth birthday, she felt lighter. As much as she wished this wasn’t the only choice, she knew Amelia had made the right decision for her.

 

A myriad of colors flashed around Amelia before the spell came to an end, hands dropping to individual sides.

Amelia blinked twice, regaining focus before aiming a gap-toothed grin at her moms. 

“How do you feel, mija?” Rio asked cautiously, still gripping Agatha’s hand nervously.

“Tired, Mama.” She yawned and stretched her arms above her head.

Jen stepped forward, hands aglow, double-checking their work. “She’s still a menace, but slightly powered down,” she confirmed.

🦕🦖🦕

Nearly collapsing into bed after the quickest shower she’d taken since her last dig, Agatha was convinced she could sleep for a week. Just traveling across the country and back twice in less than a week, even through teleportation, had drained her. Not to mention the emotional toll of the past few days.

The previously reluctant soulmates hadn’t even finished their conversation from before Amelia had woken up, and she wasn’t quite sure where they stood. What she knew was that technically, it was Rio’s week to have Amelia, yet they were both at her house, not that she minded. She preferred it that way.

Feeling the mattress shift as Rio joined her in the bed, she knew the time had come to pick up where they had left off days before. 

 

“Where does this leave us?” She turned her head to meet curious gold-flecked eyes.

“What?”

The witch propped herself up against the headboard, taking Rio’s hand. “Did you take a shower in the other room?” 

“No, I took one at my house and then came back, Agatha, focus.” 

 

“Well, that’s part of this. Why are you going to your house at all? You read the journal, you know how I feel. We’ve been on dates, we’ve been communicating, we’ve tackled some pretty big things together. Move in with me.” She was being raw, vulnerable, and her words were very much out of order, but if anyone understood, it would be Rio.

“Agatha, I, we just got back together.” The entity worried at her bottom lip.

 

“My love, we’ve been going back and forth for nearly seventeen years. This relationship of ours is old enough to drive.” She huffed a laugh. “Look at all that’s happened during that time. We haven’t done any of this ‘correctly’ when you think about it. I want us to be together, to be a family.” Framing Rio’s face in her hands, she ran her thumbs across her cheeks. “I want you, Rio. For the rest of our immortalities.”

Wrapping her hands around her wrist, she sucked in her breath. “Do you mean it? Everything you’ve said?” 

“Which parts?” Blue eyes gleamed with a hint of mischief.

 

Rio scoffed, “Marriage, more kids, it always being me? The things Agatha Harkness rarely admits.”

Agatha slotted their lips together, still holding Rio’s head in her hands. It started softly, a promise, their mouths moving gently against each other. Their desire and need for each other grew. Agatha, not wanting to part, flexed a finger, undressing them. 

 

Death hitched a leg over Agatha’s lap, straddling her, hands tangling in a mass of thick, dark curls. 

The witch’s hands gripped her waist, pulling her flush to her, not wanting any space between them. 

 

“Yes, Agatha.” Rip gasped between kisses. “We’ll move in with you.”

Agatha pulled back, looking up at the woman she’d been going back and forth with for nearly two decades. “Seriously?”

 

She nodded with a grin, before it faltered, a hint of nervousness creeping into her voice. “I haven’t told you, and I have filled out Amelia’s paperwork, so you haven’t seen her birth certificate, but…” she looked down as her voice faltered.

“What is it, my love?” Agatha caressed her cheek, face twisted in worry and concern.

“I didn’t want to at first, but you are her mother.” She met Agatha’s cerulean blues. “The birth certificate, you’re officially listed as her second birth parent.”

Agatha’s heart nearly burst as she pulled Rio back into her, kissing her tenderly at first. “Thank you, my love, I didn’t expect to be and never thought to check.”

“While I knew the decision I was making in raising her, I couldn’t leave you out. She’s as much a part of you as she is of me, and I saw it every day.” A tear rolled down her cheek, “Agatha, if I could go back,”

“Don’t.” She said sternly. “We can’t take back the things we did. We’re here now, and you’re moving in.” The devious witch grinned slightly. “We should get married.”

 

Rio barked a laugh. “Now, who do you sound like? Sorry, Ags, you’re going to have to do better. Maybe get down on one knee?” 

Planting a kiss on her collarbone, the witch smiled. “A proposal kink? That’s a new one, Feathers.”

Shaking her head, she placed a finger under her new housemate’s chin, lifting it to where they were looking at each other. “You’re ridiculous.”

Agatha smirked. “And you love me.”

“I do.” Leaning down, Rio kissed her, sucking her bottom lip into her mouth and biting it. “So very much.” She added.

 

Agatha’s hands began to travel from Rio’s hips to the expanse of her back, lightly grazing her blunt nails over the defined muscles before bringing one hand between them to gently knead Rio’s breast, rolling the hardened nipple between her fingers. 

Moaning, she moved off Agatha’s lap, repositioning them to press their cores together, crashing her mouth back into Agatha’s.

“Straight to the point, Feathers? I like it.” The witch joked, rolling her hips into Rio.

 

Licking and sucking on her pulse point, both of them growing wetter, they moved against each other. Rio smiled against her neck. “I just know what I want.

“What’s that, my love?” Agatha husked, nails digging into Rio’s flesh.

“You, infinite lifetimes together, watching you do two am feedings, seeing my ring on your finger.”

Agatha’s rhythm faltered, “No more babies until we’re married.” She panted.

“Okay, Agatha.” Rio bit into her shoulder, her body shuddering against Agatha.

 

Agatha held Death tightly against her, grounding their clits together and seeking out Rio’s mouth to kiss her as they came, their fluids mixing.

In that moment, they allowed their emotions to flow freely through the bond, increasing the intimacy and intensity.

When they finally came down from their high, Agatha held Rio against her, tracing soothing circles along her spine.

“I love you, Feathers.” She whispered into her hair.

The ageless entity smiled. “I love you, too, Agatha Harkness.”

 

Agatha was the first to move, insisting they change the sheets and shower again. Their love had been anything but conventional, but they had made it somehow. Research, dinosaurs, a child, and breaking the universe all paled in comparison to the love they shared.

As they slid between clean sheets in fresh pajamas, Agatha pulled her future wife into her. 

“There’s something I’ve always wondered, my love.”

“What’s that?” She asked sleepily.

“How did you get humans to believe you about dinosaurs?” 

She laughed with a playful smile. “Most humans were pretty easy, but there was this one woman, very hot, mind you, that disagreed with me about everything.”       

“Maybe she’s just smarter than you, Feathers.” Agatha smiled, drifting off.





Chapter End Notes

Now for the MUCH HAPPIER epilogue.

Chapter 9: You're The One I Want To Go Through Time With

Chapter Summary

Everything comes together, finally.

Chapter Notes

It all comes to an end.

Warnings: So much fluff. Smut. Just all the things. You know what to expect by now.

As always, thank you so much, Hannah-0730/CrystalsandLavender, for the support, read-throughs, and being my emotional support human!

See the end of the chapter for more notes

January 9, 2026

 

Amelia Jade Harkness-Vidal knew something had changed. She could feel it in her bones even though her powers were suppressed; she still sensed it. Having slept in her own bed, rapidly growing hellhound beside her, she made her way downstairs, noting that it was midmorning. 

Following the sound of voices, she found her moms downstairs at the kitchen table, with her grandmothers, saying something about her Mama’s house.

The discussion ended abruptly as they noted her presence.

“Good morning, Plumita, how are you feeling?” Her Mom smiled at her and stood to hug her.

“Better,” her voice hoarse from sleep. “What about Mama’s house?”

The four women exchanged glances, with Agatha moving to the refrigerator to pour juice for Amelia.

Amelia took a seat at the table, taking the juice and croissant she was handed, while Astraea went to her own breakfast.

Rio finally spoke. “Mija, how do you feel about us living here, with Agatha?”

Milly couldn’t believe what she had just heard. “As a family? Are you getting married?” 

Agatha returned to her seat and took Rio’s hand. “We’ve discussed it, but not yet, honey. We would be living together as a family. We realize that we do belong together.”

“Finally,” Lilia muttered, Sharon elbowing her in the ribs. 

“When? Can we do it today?” Amelia asked excitedly, bouncing in her seat. 

“Please hold still while you eat, Plumita. You don’t want to choke on your food.” Agatha warned with an amused smile.

“We’re moving in this weekend,” Rio answered.

Milly squealed, Astraea barking in response.

Sharon laughed, “You'd better apologize to Raya before she chews anything in this house.”

Agatha pinned Rio with a look. “You still haven’t apologized?”

Rio averted her eyes. “No, and we haven’t been at my house for her to ruin my belongings.”

Their daughter giggled and ran upstairs. 

“Oh, I have a feeling she’s managing.” Agatha deadpanned, taking a drink of her coffee.

As predicted, Amelia returned carrying a box containing a pair of shoes, scraps of clothing, and two books. “This is just from the past few days we’ve been here. Or what I’ve found. I didn’t want her to get in trouble.”

Agatha bit back a laugh. “There’s liver in her refrigerator. Go apologize.”

Rio mumbled something under her breath, Amelia grinning. At least they would be together.

🦕🦖🦕

January 10, 2026

“Mom, why aren’t we using our powers?” Nicky asked, carrying a vase that he assumed was thousands of years old. 

“Because we are showing your sister that we don’t have to use Magick for everything,” Agatha reminded for what had to be the hundredth time in the past thirty minutes.

“Except for ancient artifacts and antiques, absolutely transfer those,” Rio instructed, going against her girlfriend’s wishes.

“Ri!” Agatha scolded. 

“I am not putting that on a U-Haul, Aggie!” She rebuffed.

Zora smirked, turning to Ellie. “Did you two bicker like this?”

The older blonde scoffed. “Absolutely not, our fights had casualties.”

“They destroyed multiple Evanora Harkness-owned homes, if I remember correctly.” Ralph reminisced.

“We are the best of friends and the worst of partners.” Ellie reminded.

“This is not a fight, it’s a disagreement.” Rio loaded a box of books onto the truck. 

Wanda snapped her fingers, loading the rest of what was being taken to Agatha’s. “I, however, did not agree to Agatha’s stupid rule. It’s four in the afternoon, it has dropped below freezing again, and Milly is at Lilia’s. Speaking of disagreements, did you apologize to Astraea?”

“I did, and she hasn’t touched anything since. No, I don’t want to hear it.”

“Wanda!” Agatha cried from inside the house.

The redhead clapped her hands together, a habit she had picked up from the older witch over the years. “That’s our cue, everyone in a car, let’s go.”

Agatha stepped out of the house, watching as the family dispersed back to hers. She encircled Rio’s waist and grinned. “I’ll give them half an hour, snap to put everything where we want it, and then we have the rest of the evening to ourselves.”

Rio gasped in faux shock. “Why have them come at all?”

“I like seeing their faces occasionally.” 

“Agatha, you had everyone come in to help us move the ‘mortal way’. We’re going to finish helping. You even insisted on driving separately.” 

“Okay, fine, I’ll go ahead, you lock up and make sure you have everything you need for now.” She conceded, kissing her before leaving.



Rio had taken her time to get over to Agatha’s, no, their house. She had left some furniture that she wouldn’t need, but everything else had been moved or donated. It had been odd that Agatha had suggested doing everything manually, but she went with it. 

Walking into their place, it was unexpectedly quiet. As Rio had expected, everything was unpacked and put away. 

“Cheater,” She mumbled with a fond smile, before spotting Victoria waiting for her in the living room. “What are you doing?” She asked suspiciously.

The bearded dragon turned and led her straight into the backyard.

Snow still blanketed the ground from recent winter storms, candles and tealights floated in the air, just out of reach, giving the illusion of stars. Agatha stood in the middle wearing a tailored black suit, a white shirt unbuttoned just enough, and purple heels. 

It was the same outfit she had worn when they first met in 2009. 

Rio walked directly to her, mesmerized, amber eyes already glistening. “Agatha Harkness, what are you doing?”

“Thought I’d do some light snow gardening,” She teased, breathing deeply, reaching out and taking Rio’s hands in hers. 

“Rio Vidal, Lady Death. Everything I want to say, you’ve read. We’ve experienced our relationship out of order, we already bicker like we’ve been married for centuries, we have a beautiful daughter, and we’ve been through hell and back. I love you more than words can possibly convey.” Dropping down to one knee, Milly came forward from where she had been waiting off to the side with the rest of their family, and handed her Mom a small black box.

Agatha thanked her, kissing their daughter on the cheek. She opened the box to reveal an emerald, surrounded by small diamonds on a black titanium band inlaid with more diamonds, Agatha’s Magick coursing through it.

“Will you do me the honor of being mine legally and not just cosmically, for the rest of immortality?”

Death stood there, stunned, mouth gaping open. Yes, they had talked about it, but she didn’t expect it to be this soon.

“Feathers? An answer?” Agatha prompted.

It was then that Rio realized that while she had stuck her left hand out, she hadn’t actually said the words. “Yes! Agatha, why do you think my hand is out?”

Sliding the ring onto her finger, Agatha stood, “You have to use your words, babygirl.” She kissed her chastely before their loved ones descended. Milly reached them first.

“Mama! Did you forget your line? You had one job!” She scolded.

Laughing, Rio lifted her daughter in her arms. “I was just nervous and surprised!”

“So, does this make you Mama to all of us now?” Wanda asked with a smile, pulling Rio in for a hug.

“You could have a double wedding with Emma and Pietro!” Lily suggested with a smirk as they all made their way back inside.

Both Emma and Agatha quickly struck down the suggestion.

“Nicky, when are you going to bring your girlfriend around?” Tommy asked curiously.

“When everyone stops talking about marriage and babies,” he grumbled, looking over at Gabby sleeping in Zora’s arms. 

“No more babies for a while.” Agatha laughed, kissing Rio on the cheek. 

🦕🦖🦕

February 13, 2026

 

Rio stared down at the pregnancy test in her hand in horror, trying to decipher exactly how she could have potentially conceived. Agatha had refused to enchant any toys; they hadn’t used fertility spells, and unfortunately for both of them, it wasn’t like her cycle happened like clockwork. Fucking cosmic biology.

Amelia had proved that fertility spells weren’t needed between Death and her witch, but surely Death hadn’t accidentally willed herself pregnant as Wanda had. 

Shaking, she vanished the pregnancy test and called Jen. She had to take a blood test to be sure—no need to tell Agatha Harkness anything if she wasn’t pregnant.

“Where’s Agatha?” Jen asked, processing the blood samples quicker than any mortal lab could.

“Board meeting for the parks,” Rio answered nervously, watching the witch work. She had transferred to Harkness labs, where Jen discreetly ran the test in minutes rather than hours.

“She doesn’t know?”

The entity shook her head. “I didn’t want to tell her until I knew for sure.”

Jen looked down at the screen as the report generated, then pressed print and handed the slip to Rio. “Now you know what to tell her. I promise not to say anything.”

Her hand flew to her mouth. “How?” Her eyes widened, looking at the medical witch in shock.

“Well, when two fated witches love each other very much,” Jen began.

“Jen.”

“Think about it, Rio.”

February 14, 2026  

Valentine’s date night, and Rio was nervous. By Jennifer’s calculations, she was roughly five weeks along, still early. With Amelia, she’d been close to her second trimester when she found out, mistaking morning sickness for the consequences of cutting off the bond.

Returning home from dinner, she felt the familiar nausea settle in the pit of her stomach, though she wasn't sure if it was from nerves or the life inside of her. Agatha had been adamant about waiting to have another child; would she be excited? 

“Are you okay, Feathers? You seem anxious.” Agatha looked at her with concern as they crossed the threshold of their home.

Shrugging it off, she removed her shoes. “Of course, just worried about Milly. The flu is going around, and she seemed a bit lethargic this morning.”

Agatha hummed, unconvinced. “Lilia will call if something goes wrong.”

“I know,” she hung up her coat, “I have a gift for you, but it’s upstairs.”

“Is it underneath this dress that I’ve wanted to take off of you all night?” The witch ran her hands over the black mesh sleeves, her eyes tracing the deep V-neck.

“You’ll have to find out, go upstairs, strip down to the lingerie I know is underneath this very sexy suit, and meet me in bed.” Rio pressed a promising kiss to her lips. “I’ll be there in a moment.”

Watching the way Agatha purposefully swayed her hips as she ascended the stairs, Rio apparated into their closet. She had been shocked when her witch had actually made room for her clothes. Granted, the other scientist had just moved them to another closet, but it was the thought that counted. 

Not bothering with clothing of any sort, she stripped bare and wrapped a forest green robe around herself, before plucking the box she had wrapped out of its hiding spot.

As instructed, Agatha lay in bed wearing a dark purple lace bodysuit. The lace was a floral design, the top framing her breasts, with fabric covering her already hardened nipples. The sides were cut out, exposing much of her waist and torso, barely covering the curve of her ass. She has her legs spread, showing off the open gusset.

Rio’s mouth went dry at the sight, the familiar heat shooting straight to her cunt. Maybe her gift could wait.

“About time, Feathers, thought I was gonna have to start this myself.” Dark blue eyes honed in on the box in Rio’s hand. “What’s that?”

“I know we said no gifts, but I got you something last-minute anyway,” Rio half-lied.

“Hate to break it to you, my love, but the only gift I have for you is me.”  Extending her arm, she crooked her finger in a ‘come here’ motion.

Setting the box on the nightstand, she crawled across the bed to Agatha before the witch pulled her down into a searing kiss. 

“Ags,” She breathed, her robe having been removed. “Your present.”

“It can wait,” she huffed, spanking Rio’s buttcheek.

Death moaned, moving to kneel between Agatha’s legs. She could always make her open the box afterward. Kissing down Agatha’s body, she traced the edges of the bodysuit with her fingers and tongue.



Agatha tangled her hands in Rio’s silken hair as her tongue circled one of her nipples through the lace of the bodysuit. Nimble fingers moved lower to caress Agatha’s cunt where the fabric was parted.

The shift from warm lips to cool bone caused Agatha to gasp. Peering down, Rio’s half-skeletal face gazed back at her, flicking her tongue over her nipple.

Agatha growled in response. “You’re fucking magnificent in your true form, my love.” Using the grip she had on Death’s hair, she dragged the entity back to meet her mouth, kissing her teeth and licking along her jaw. 

Take this off before I rip it off. Rio tugged at the lace garment as Agatha made it disappear rather than strip out of it.

I was hoping for a show, Agatha.

The witch huffed, “I’m too impatient when you look like this.”

You’re never patient. She bit down into Agatha’s lip, drawing blood before licking the flesh and healing it. Continuing her descent down Agatha’s body, she licked and bit her way down before settling between her thighs. 

Rio bit at Agatha’s inner thighs, her bony fingers digging into them, leaving the witch gasping and moaning. 

“Rio, baby. I’m so wet.” She pleaded. “Look what you do to me.” 

I know, sweetheart. You’re being so good.

Agatha groaned at the praise and tilted her hips for her fiancée.

Teeth grazed her clit before it was soothed by Rio’s tongue and then replaced by bony digits, Death’s tongue dipping into her entrance.

The witch cried out when Rio sped up, fucking into her with the wet muscle and working her clit with the bones of her fingers. Agatha’s hips bucked wildly against her, teeth grazing the sensitive skin.  

Agatha’s nerve endings were on fire as desire and pleasure wound tightly in her core, ready to burst forth. She squeezed her right breast, pinching the nipple between her fingers, moaning while Rio worked her with her tongue. 

Tendrils of lilac Magick darted forward, traveling over Rio’s body, mimicking Agatha’s touch and kisses wherever they grazed her skin.

Agatha, yes. Right there, don’t stop.

Tendrils found their way between Rio’s legs, one thrusting into her and another on her sensitive core. 

They began to move in sync, the fire building in both of them until they began stuttering in their movements, breaths ragged, names falling from each other's lips, the sounds of their releases permeating the room.

They finally stilled, Rio settling against Agatha’s chest, listening to her heartbeat. Agatha ran her hand over a few of the other woman’s bare ribs. 

“You’re beautiful, Lady Death.”

I love you.

Agatha blushed, something she rarely did, but with Rio in her true form, she was reminded that this creature, this cosmic being, was the one she was fated to be with. Pushing her back slightly, she gazed in awe at Rio’s heart and the undeniable mark on it. The three of swords etched there. “I’m looking right at it, and I still can’t believe it sometimes.”

I’m all yours, Agatha.

Staring into her eyes, she leaned forward and kissed her softly, savoring the taste of herself on Rio’s mouth. “Thank you, my love, for not giving up on me.”

“I tried.” She replied, returning to her human form.

“Shower with me, and then I’m opening my present.” Agatha grinned, stealing another quick kiss.

🦕🦖🦕

Freshly showered, Rio sat nervously in their bed, waiting for Agatha to join her and open the present. The wait was torture. “Aggie, hurry up!” She called.

“I’m coming! Not all of us have flawless skin, Ri!” She laughed, climbing back into the bed, reaching for the box.

“I just hope you like it,” She whispered, watching her unwrap it, poking her tongue in her cheek, eyes trained on Agatha’s face.

 

Agatha wasn’t sure what had suddenly gotten into her soulmate, or why she was so nervous. Opening the box, she picked up the shirt and carefully unfolded it. It was black, white letters reading ‘Mother of Six’, with six dinosaurs printed on it. Her eyes widened, looking back in the box to see a positive pregnancy test.

Turning to Rio, lips parted in shock, she took a moment to find her words. “Feathers? You’re pregnant? How?” She was reeling, tears were stinging the backs of her eyes, she was having trouble breathing, and the world was spinning.

Rio seemed terrified and unsure of how to react. “Well, we know that we can just get pregnant because we’re fated. Apparently, scissoring will do it.”

Agatha dropped the shirt back into the box and threw her arms around Rio. She kissed her urgently, reverently, tearfully. Pressing their foreheads together, she held her entity in her arms, tears of joy running down her cheeks.

“You’re not upset?” Rio asked cautiously.

“Of course not, my love. Why would I be?” She let go for a moment to move the box to the side table and settle them both in for the night.

“You wanted to wait until we were married, and you have this need to be in control if you haven’t noticed.”

Agatha scoffed. “We can still get married before the baby comes, and we still have, what, eight months? October?”

“Yes, Jen said I’m roughly five weeks.” She confirmed.

Pulling her closer, she smiled into her hair. “I am going to take such good care of you. I love you, both, so much.”

“Happy Valentine’s Day, Aggie.”

 

Hours had passed since Rio had surprised Agatha with the news. They had made love again, and Rio was asleep on her shoulder, breathing softly against her skin.

The anxiety and what-ifs, however, were already starting to plague Agatha, and there was only one person who could talk her down.

Carefully, she moved Rio’s head to the pillow and rose from the bed, wrapping herself in a floral silk robe before taking her phone and padding downstairs.

Ralph answered on the first ring.

“Shouldn’t you be getting railed by Death?” 

“I already did, thank you. How’s the Valentine’s shift going?” She knew Ian was on Isla Sorna this weekend, and Ralph had decided to work while he was gone. 

“It’s going. Had some teens careflighted in from a nasty car accident. They’ll be okay. I doubt that’s why you called.” 

Damn this man for knowing her so well. “I can’t call my ex-husband at three am to check in?”

“Agatha.” He scolded, lovingly. “What’s wrong? I can hear the apprehension and doubt in your voice.”

“Rio’s pregnant.” It was barely a whisper, and while she was excited, she was still in shock.

“Well done! I’ll bring cigars when we come for Milly’s birthday. I know we joke, darling, but you’re an amazing mother. You have nothing to worry about.” He smiled into the phone, joy radiating from Connecticut.

“That’s not what I’m worried about, Ralph. What if I’m not a supportive enough partner? I told her I would’ve taken such good care of her had I been around the first time, and I’ll do a great job this time…” 

Ralph cut her off, refusing to let her spiral into self-doubt. “Agatha, this is all remnants of a lifetime of listening to the vitriol Evanora used to spew. When you care about someone, you protect them, care about them, and love them with every fiber of your being. Look at this family you’ve created. Wanda’s pregnancies, especially with the twins, you were there for everything.”

“Of course I was, Ralph. She’s our daughter.” Agatha sounded somewhat offended, as if she wouldn’t have been there every step of the way.

“And Rio is carrying your child. You’re going to be amazing. I know you’re still worried you’ll disappoint or hurt her again, but you’re not going to Aggie. You’ll be a wonderful partner.” He spoke with love and conviction, having seen Agatha through everything since they were teenagers.

“What would I do without you?”

“Thankfully, the world will never know.” 

🦕🦖🦕

March 13, 2026

Agatha was thankful they had both decided to have their Fridays off for the Spring semester. After dropping Milly off at school, they portaled to New York to Jen’s clinic for Rio’s nine-week appointment. 

Rio was calm, beautiful, glowing. Agatha was a wreck. They hadn’t announced the pregnancy to the kids, though they were trying to make career decisions for the next academic year. Ralph, Jen, Alice, and Lilia were the only ones who knew. Which meant Ian, Ellie, and Sharon also knew. Agatha was frankly shocked that no one else had found out. 

They had yet to tell Amelia, the sibling who would most be affected, and weren’t sure how. Rio’s morning sickness had gotten worse, and she was sleeping more, especially for an entity. Both Victoria and Señor Scratchy were spending more time by her side, providing her with healing energy. 

It wasn’t that they were hiding it from her. They were just waiting until the second trimester, ideally. Yes, Rio was the almighty Death, but it didn’t make her immune to loss. She was still in human form, even if she could still transition between the two. 

“You’re stressed, my love.” Rio soothed, using the moniker Agatha so often used for her. “Everything is alright. Help me undress.”

A small smile adorned her face, and she stole a kiss. “Always, Feathers. The oversized button-up and leggings were a good choice.” She made short work of the leggings, folding them and setting them on a nearby chair before unbuttoning the shirt and letting it hang open. 

Rio took her place at the end of the exam table, Agatha slotting herself between her legs and running her hands up her thighs, leaning forward and kissing her gently.

“I wonder if we have time—” 

“No sex in the exam room, Harkness,” Jen scolded, breezing into the room. “Good Morning, Rio. How are you feeling this morning?”

“Nauseous, hungry, and exhausted.” 

“That’s normal.” Jen gave her a sympathetic smile, then washed her hands and donned gloves. “Agatha, move to the side, please. "

Agatha frowned before doing as she was told and then took her fiancée’s hand.

Focusing on Rio, Jen began asking her questions. “Any differences from last time?” 

“I didn’t know I was pregnant this early with Amelia, but no. Seems to be the same. No true cravings yet, sick at all times of the day, utterly exhausted, no problems with my magick.”

Agatha held Rio’s hand through it all, cataloging every word, every symptom. Reminding herself to order more of the ginger drops that Rio liked for nausea, make soups to keep in the freezer, and watch for any changes in symptoms.

Rio was lying back now, jelly spread across her still-flat stomach.

Jen had a mischievous glint in her eyes when she peered at Agatha. “Ready, Dad?”

“Yes, and I’m letting that one slide.”

Carefully gliding the Doppler over Rio’s bare stomach, her uterus came into view, and the unmistakable ‘whoosh whoosh’ of the baby’s heartbeat filled the room. 

Jen pointed to a small bean-shaped spot on the screen. “There is your newest little witch.” 

Rio’s grip on Agatha’s hand tightened as her eyes met hers, tears beginning to pool. “We’re having another baby,” She whispered, “And you’re here this time.”

“I’m not going anywhere, my love.” Agatha pressed their foreheads together. “I love you, Lady Death. Wild Rexes couldn’t pull me away.”

Jen cleared her throat, breaking the moment. “I took the liberty of recording the heartbeat. Here are baby’s first pictures.” She handed over the strip of sonogram photos. “You get dressed, I am going to grab a few things for your all-time sickness and fatigue. I really am happy for both of you.”

The door clicked softly behind her. Agatha gently wiped the cold, gooey substance from Rio and assisted in redressing her. “We’re having a baby.” She repeated in awe, kissing her reverently.

Death melted into the kiss. “We are. Together.”   

🦕🦖🦕

March 21, 2026

Amelia collapsed in the middle of the living room floor, exhausted from the events of the day. All her friends from school had come to the birthday party at her house, and she had spent three hours running, playing games, and jumping in the bounce house.

Now, she lay on the rug next to Astraea after dinner with her entire family, completely drained, watching and listening to the people around her. The TV was turned on and tuned to a hockey game as she half-listened to the twins discuss their Spring Break plans for the following week, and her eyes began to drift closed.

Hearing snippets of conversations around her, she thought about how happy she was now that she didn’t have to worry about random powers or feeling decades ahead of everyone her age. It was just her and her moms most of the time, and she liked it that way. It gave her time to adjust to their dynamics, and she was helping her aunts and siblings plan the summer wedding.

Everything was perfect.

 

“Have you told Princesa yet?” Ralph asked, smoking a celebratory cigar, where they sat outside in the cool evening air.

His ex-wife shook her head. “No. We’re waiting until the second trimester to announce it. None of the kids know.”

His mouth curved into a smile. “I call bullshit on that one. The older girls absolutely know, they just won’t say anything until you do.”

“Why do you think that?” She quirked an eyebrow at him.

“Agatha Harkness doesn’t dote or coddle. Yet anytime Rio needs anything, you’re five steps ahead of her. Pulling out her chair, opening doors. I have always treated you like the queen you are, and you’re making me look terrible.”

Her brows furrowed. “Do you think I’m going overboard?”

“She’ll let you know if you are, but honestly? Darling, it’s a good look on you. Not only are you the happiest you’ve ever been, but the way you look at her like she’s the damn moon is a fucking relief.”

“I want to marry her before the baby gets here, Ralph.” Agatha took a puff of her cigar, letting the smoke linger in the air.

Chuckling, he took a drink of his whiskey. “I knew your wedding would be before Pietro’s.”

“Emma’s going to drag that boy down to the courthouse at this rate, not that I’ll mind.” She took another drag of the cigar, deep in thought. “If I decide to walk down the aisle, instead of waiting at the altar, will you do the honors?”

Smiling warmly, he nodded. “Gladly. I’ll even officiate if you prefer. As long as we get the two of you married.” He took another drag and put his free hand on hers. “I’m proud of you for getting here. For allowing yourself to have nice things,” glancing toward her, he smiled fondly, a tear threatening to fall.

“You’re such a sap.” Her own small smile was watery. “I had a really great support system, role models, whatever you want to call it. 

Ralph put his cigar out, turning his full attention to the woman by his side. “And an alarming amount of booze if I recall correctly.”

“No one’s perfect.” 

“Despite their flaws, I still think our kids are.” He winked. “So, when are you wanting to get hitched? June second is still ours, and you two can’t have it.”

Laughter rang out. It had been a random Wednesday in 1999 when they had been married by a justice of the peace, despite her mother’s wishes. Evanora had wanted a large church wedding for social status purposes. 

Agatha and Ralph eloped and celebrated with custom cocktails that they named June Second. Citing that it was the only reason they remembered their anniversary. 

Evanora still forced her circus of a ceremony, though they didn’t recognize that date, just the party they had. 

“I have a date in mind, if she’s willing.” She answered once their laughter subsided.

“I have a feeling she’ll say yes.”

🦕🦖🦕

“What were you and Ralph discussing?” Rio asked casually as she joined her fiancée beneath the covers, both of them fresh from the shower.

“Your pregnancy, marriage, normal divorced couple fare.” Drawing Rio close to her, she ran her fingers through her hair. “I want to get married before the baby gets here.”

The entity leaned her head back, taking in Agatha. “When were you thinking?”

“Beltane. Isla Dunblar. Just the family.” Her tongue ran across the front of her teeth, anticipating the answer. 

Cupping the back of Agatha’s neck, she answered her with a kiss. “Fertility, growth, renewal?” She listed what the day represented. “It’s perfect, Agatha.” 

“I thought so.” She smirked. “Now to tell the kids.”

“Tomorrow? The first day of spring, we can also tell them I’m pregnant.”

Agatha considered it before nodding. “Might as well, Ralph says I’m obvious about it.”

A grin spread across the cosmic being's face. “You are, and I love it.”

“Am I overdoing it?” She bit down on her lip.

“No. You promised to take care of me, and you are, Aggie.”

 

Amelia was suspicious as everyone was called into the living room. It was Spring Break, and she was going to spend a few days in New York. She didn’t understand why they needed to have a giant Harkness family meeting right then. She took a seat on the floor next to the twins, all sixty pounds of Astraea in her lap. Looking around, she noticed everyone was there—her siblings and their partners, the dads, the three aunts, and her grandmothers. 

Even Victoria and Señor Scratchy were in attendance as her moms stood in front of them, looking official, yet relaxed at the same time. 

Amelia felt a sense of dread in the pit of her stomach, as if it were warning her that more change was coming. They had settled into a good routine; she had just turned five, they were getting married, and everything was settling into place.

Lily, her sister who had just had a birthday of her own, sat next to her. “You’re nervous, Green Goblin.”

“I don’t like this. What’s this about?” She whispered while everyone still settled around them.

“I have an idea, and if I’m correct, it’s nothing to worry about. I’m here if you need me.” Her hand settled on her little sister’s back for comfort.

Amelia’s attention was drawn towards her mother as Agatha called for everyone’s attention.

“Rio and I have a few things to tell everyone and thought it would be easier to do it while we’re all together,” She began. “We’re getting married—”

“Yes, we know,” Jen interrupted.

“We were all there,” Nicky added.

“Let me finish before I hex all of you.” Agatha sneered, though there was no bite to it.

“On Beltane.” Rio finished for her.

“That’s in a little over a month!” Emma explained, hand covering her mouth at her outburst.

“Keen observation. The ceremony will be on Isla Dunblar.” The matriarch added, slipping her arm around Rio’s waist.

Milly grinned up at her moms. This was good news to her, but why did her stomach still feel all twisty?

“There’s something else,” Rio’s voice was soft, her eyes on Agatha’s, and then Amelia’s.

The little girl froze, reading her Mama’s expression and remembering what she had asked the mall Santa. Yes, that was something she wanted then, in an effort to bring them together. Now she wanted them to herself for a while. She could wait another eleven years.

“I’m pregnant. Due in October.” She finished.

“Shotgun wedding!” Ian exclaimed, “Pay up, Calderu.”

“I did not divine this,” Lilia grumbled, still smiling. 

 

There were congratulations and individual conversations all around, her family rising to hug her Moms. Milly absentmindedly petted the hound in her lap, unsure of what a shotgun wedding was. She was just healing from the events of the fall, getting used to her more age-appropriate knowledge level. When the baby was born, she would have had Agatha for a little over a year before having to share her full-time.

The benefits of being so much younger than her siblings were that they only visited for short stints. She was practically an only child and was getting to make up for four years of lost time. 

“You okay, Green Goblin?” Lily asked, eyes gazing at her intently.

The current youngest Harkness startled. “I think so. Mama’s having a baby.” She repeated.

“Yeah, she is,” Lily confirmed, standing up and holding out her hand. “I think it’s time for a sibling meeting, kiddo.” 

Harkness kids to the basement. No kids, wives, or fiancées. She sent her siblings, guiding her sister downstairs.

 

Agatha knew there would be joking and laughter; she had expected it. Jen’s jokes about contraceptive potions, Ellie and Ian wagering bets on how many children Agatha would father until the end of time, and Sharon already breaking out her knitting supplies came as no surprise.

Wanda threw her arms around both of them, already blubbering. “I knew it!” 

Rio looked at Agatha. “Did you tell her?”

“No, she didn’t,” Wanda immediately defended. “I could tell by the way she was four steps ahead of you all day yesterday, and then today. Ag’s has always looked at you like you’re her reason for existing, more so when you came back, and it’s even stronger now.”

Zora stepped up to Agatha’s side, “Is there something I need to know? I’m still shocked we created Gabby.” 

“Scissoring.” 

Zora blew out a breath. “Fucking hell.”

Agatha grimaced. “Not that I want to be discussing this, since that’s my daughter, but she’s the Scarlet Witch, Zora. It doesn’t matter the position or means. If she wants a baby, she’ll have a baby.”

“Here’s to an eternity of creating children, then.” 

“We’re gods amongst our kind.” The witch laughed, looking around the room for Lily and Amelia, spotting the two brunettes leaving the room, Raya following. From the corner of her eye, she caught her sons excusing themselves, and finally, Wanda heading toward the basement as well.

Emma raised an eyebrow toward Agatha. “What’s that about?”

“Harkness Huddle. They’ve done it since they were kids, when they need to talk, just the siblings.” Ellie answered.

“Amelia’s first,” Ian noted. 

Death’s eyes flicked to her witch, concerned.

“She’s okay.” She reassured. “However, you and Ellie need to have a conversation. Don’t give me that look, Sattler. I’m fine with it. Rio is the one you have to ask. I suggest my office.” Leaning over, she placed a kiss on Rio’s temple. “Don’t stress, my love. I’m here if you need me.”

Rio shook her head. “It’s not like there are many secrets around here anyway. I’ve made peace with it, bring Darcy. She was there when Agatha needed her, and I’m not going to punish you for Agatha’s decisions. I know you two have a sordid history, and I actually enjoy your presence.”

Ellie looked between them. “You’re really okay with it?”

“Of course. You’re serious about her, which means she’s going to show up at family gatherings anyway, eventually. We’re not going to push you out for that. Family is forever. Harkness motto, right?” Rio’s smile was sincere as she hugged Ellie. “I’m happy you found someone, Ellie.”

Ellie pulled back and focused her attention on Agatha. “If you hurt this woman again, they will never find your body, Harkness.” 

“Please, Wanda will be five steps ahead of you. But yes, I understand.” Her signature smirk slid across her face, soaking in the normalcy of it all. 

“Should we check on the kids?” Ralph stepped beside Ellie, eyes shifting to the doorway.

“I trust them, they’re okay,” Agatha answered, an arm around Rio’s waist, her other hand protectively on her abdomen. 

Ralph raised an eyebrow. “They charmed the basement, and you can’t spy without them knowing, didn’t they?” 

“To-may-to, to-mah-to.”

 

The five Harkness siblings, ranging in age from five to thirty-seven now, sat around in the main part of the basement. Wanda looked toward the door and cast a simple enchantment to prevent anyone from overhearing them.

“Áno, sestra?” Pietro asked as he sat in the corner of a sofa, Nicky at the other end.

“Someone is feeling a little insecure about the new baby, and I think she would benefit from hearing how the two of you felt,” Lily answered, sitting with Amelia next to her.

Wanda took the spot on the other side of Amelia, “We were older than you, of course, but Lilith is right. That might help, and then hearing how they felt about the boys. Which is slightly different.”

“Not much when you think about it, Wands.” Nicky inserted.

The redhead shrugged and opened her arms for her youngest sibling. “I thought you wanted Mom and Rio to have a sibling?”

Milly climbed into her older sister’s lap and sighed. “I did, at Christmas. But then they got together, and we’re happy, and I don’t have to share them except when everyone visits, and it’s perfect.” She pouted, crossing her arms over her chest.

Pietro chuckled. “You sound just like Wanda did when Mom was pregnant with the twins.”

“I was excited! It was you who was upset!” Wanda shot back.

“No,” Pietro shook his head, leaning forward. “We were fifteen when the parents told us. All four of them, in case we needed extra emotional support. Let’s not pretend like Ellie isn’t a mom figure. Especially back then. Anyway, we knew it was going to happen, so we weren’t surprised really. They already knew she was having twins, and I remember immediately asking Papa if we could build the cribs. He’s much better with that kind of thing than Dad.” He grinned at the memory.

“Ellie even pitched in. The three of them made your cribs. Same ones we used for the boys, and Gabby.” Wanda smiled.

“Wands, however, had always been Mom’s shadow. She was already taking every internship, going to every camp, and doing anything she could to follow in her footsteps in the paleontology field. It’s how they bonded, other than Magick.”

“The thought of losing that terrified me. Not going on expeditions, writing papers, contributing to groundbreaking research. I thought my life was over.”

“It wasn’t. Mom had us and adjusted.” Nicky inserted.

Wanda hummed. “She did. Not only that,” she looked down at her sister, who was watching her every expression. “The second I met them, I was overcome with this protective, profound love for them, just like when we met you. You’ll have that bond with this baby, even if you don’t like them all the time, and you won’t. But you’ll always love them. Twin bonds and sibling bonds are different, but it’s still a strong, unbreakable one that lasts your entire life.”

Amelia nodded, taking in everything they were telling her. “Ralph and Agatha didn’t love you less?” She asked meekly.

Pietro’s eyebrows stitched together. “Of course not. If anything, their love grew just like our family did. We adjusted to a new normal, but that’s all.”

“As the twins grew older, we got more involved. Story time, helping out if one parent was gone. That is, until we started going with them or on our own trips. There’s a much bigger age gap between us than there will be between you and BabySaurus.” Wanda explained. 

The girl in her lap giggled at the nickname. “I like Babysaurus.”

“We called the twins bacteria and baby-rex,” Pietro told her. 

Nicky rolled his eyes, “We were kids when Wanda had the boys, and due to her and Viz’s jobs, they lived in the LA mansion with us. So it was like having two baby brothers. It wasn’t so much sharing parents as it was sharing attention in general.” 

“We lived in a house with three scientists and two doctors. Nonna and Mimi took care of us a lot, and of course, we spent time with Papa and Ellie, depending. Then the illness on top of it. There was still never a shortage of love or affection.” Lily reached over and took Amelia’s hand.

“And there won’t be now, Amelia,” Nicky assured her. “You have the four of us, and you can call us any time through your tablet. However, Mom and Rio will never let you feel unloved, no matter how many kids they have.”

“And with the way they are, they’re going to run out of names in a few thousand years.” Lily’s mouth curved into a smirk.

“What do you mean?” Amelia asked curiously.

“Nothing.” All four siblings responded.

“Feel better?” Wanda asked, giving her a tight squeeze.

Milly nodded. “I liked this, too. Getting to talk to all of you. We need to have more sibling time.”

“I agree, we should make it a thing, Wanda, you’re responsible. Set it up.” Lily demanded.

“Every Sunday evening, group FaceTime. No matter where we are in the world,” The Scarlet Witch decided, her siblings agreeing.

“I’m bringing Morgan to the wedding,” Nicky announced suddenly.

“Who’s Morgan?” Amelia asked.

“His girlfriend, whom he rarely talks about and sneaks in and out of our apartment like I don’t know she exists.” Lily teased.

“You’ve told me all about this elusive woman, and you decide to introduce her to everyone at Mom’s wedding? Surrounded by dinosaurs? The same dinosaurs that ate Wanda’s ex-husband?” Pietro’s eyes were lit with amusement.

“We were never divorced, so I think the term is just late husband,” his twin mused. “It is odd timing, Nicholas.” 

Nicky shrugged, “We’ll all be together, it’s somewhat neutral ground, and it’s not a major holiday. Well, it kind of is for us, but I didn’t know it was going to be Beltane. Anyway, I’ve been getting grief from the elders for not bringing her around yet. Hopefully, everyone is too distracted to interrogate her.”

Lily’s eyes sparkled with mischief. “What my dear twin isn’t saying is that she’s a Stark and he doesn’t want Mom’s personal distaste for one Tony Stark to cloud her judgment.”

Milly raised her eyebrows in question, thoroughly confused. “Who?”

Nicky chuckled, reaching over and ruffling her hair. “A billionaire who mom wouldn’t mind feeding to Astraea, Milly.” 

Astraea’s ears perked up upon hearing her name and food mentioned so close together.

“Oh, well, as long as he isn’t there, mommy should be fine.” Turning her attention to Lily, she tilted her head. “Are you bringing a date?”

“Oh shut up,” Lily shot at the other three when they began to laugh. “No, Green Goblin. I don’t exactly date, and there’s no one I would take to a wedding.”

“It’s okay, I don’t date either. Billy has a boyfriend, and Tommy likes a girl.” She quickly covered her mouth, looking up at Wanda. “Oops.”

“I need someone to spy on my sons. As my sister, your loyalty is to me first, your nephews second.”

Nicky shook his head. “I don’t think that’s how it goes.”

“Let me have this, Nicholas.” Wanda hissed.

Milly giggled as she remembered something their Papa had said. “What’s a shotgun wedding?”

The basement erupted into laughter as her siblings explained what Ian had meant until they were summoned for lunch.  

🦕🦖🦕

May 1, 2026 - Beltane

Isla Sorna

 

The day of the wedding finally arrived, and Rio stood in a room at the main compound on the island, Alice fussing over her dress. At sixteen weeks pregnant, she still wasn’t showing unless she ate a particularly large meal. Death had decided to hand over her supernatural duties to Hela for the time being, at least until after their son was born.

“You’re stunning, Ri.” Alice gasped, stepping back. “The natural elements are perfect.”

Rio stared at herself in the mirror; her raven hair was down, curling softly around her shoulders, crown perched on top of her head. Green and gold makeup was done perfectly, with a bold red lip. 

Her dark green and black dress perfectly encompassed who she was. Black vines and flowers snaked up, covering her chest in a deep V down to her navel, much like her battle outfit. The forest green silk skirt flowed around her, shimmering as if made of water. Black lace, interwoven with more black flowers, was layered over the skirt, adding to the effect. She decided to go barefoot, choosing to connect with the Earth during the ceremony.

The ring on her finger sparkled in the late evening sun streaming in through the window as she threaded emerald earrings through her ears, and Alice clasped the necklace from Christmas around her neck.

“I think I’m ready to marry my witch,” a hint of a smile played at her lips as she turned to her best friend. “I can’t believe I finally get to say that.”

“It took you two long enough. Idiots.” Lilia mumbled with a smirk from the doorway. “Let’s get you down that aisle before either of you overthink anything.”

 

Agatha stood at the altar, on the plateau of the island where so much had happened. Her long, midnight brown hair cascaded down her back in waves. The plum suit she wore with only a black lace bra underneath was perfectly tailored to her, showing off every curve. Rio had given her an engagement ring as well. A purple sapphire, in a rose gold band shaped to look like vines, inlaid with diamond and emerald leaves. The bracelet, her locket, and many piercings added to her jewelry. Light makeup and black stilettos finished out the look.  

“You look amazing,” Ellie whispered in her ear, readying herself to join Alice at the end of the aisle. Her knee-length lilac dress with a sweetheart neckline and fluttery mesh sleeves perfectly complemented Alice’s black suit with green accents.

“You clean up nicely yourself, Sattler. Where’s your girlfriend?”

“Talking to Ian about work, avoiding the kids. I told her it wasn’t awkward, but she doesn’t believe me.” The blonde paleontologist shrugged.

“I’ll speak to her later.” Agatha turned to Ralph as he took his place behind the altar. Ellie walked back to the makeshift entrance.

“Thank you for doing this.”

“I wouldn’t miss it for anything,” He took her hand. “Like anyone else is worthy of marrying you off. Please. You’ll forever be my girl, Aggie.”

“And you’re the only man for me, Ralph.” She replied sincerely, before nearly being knocked over by something small and fluffy. “Okay, okay!” She let out a breathy laugh. “You’re in an entirely different category, Scratch, but you and Ian, too!”

Ralph glanced down the aisle, receiving a thumbs-up signal from Alice. 

“Darling, are you ready to be married for all of time?”

Pursing her lips, she looked back at him. “When you put it that way,”

“Agatha.”  

“Yes, Ralph! Gods. Start the damn thing already.”

With a small smile, he nodded, directing everyone's attention to the back of the open space. Chairs lined the aisle for the family and a few select friends, entities, and gods.

Amelia walked down the aisle first, her dress much like her Mama’s in color with azalea and rio blossoms, the shade of her Mom’s suit adorning the bodice and skirt. Her wild curls had been tamed, and half of it was pulled up. She expertly scattered the same petals in front of her as she walked, Astraea by her side with a purple and green bow clipped behind one floppy ear.

Alice and Ellie followed as the Matron of Honor and Best Woman, respectively, and took their places. 

Agatha reached out and squeezed Ellie’s hand, giving her a look that held more than words could ever say.

“I know. Me too, you big softie. Let’s get you hitched to Death.” Her Best Woman whispered through tears of happiness.

Agatha turned her head to the end of the aisle, where Rio appeared, her arm laced with Lilia’s, as they began to glide toward them.

Agatha’s lips parted as she let out a small, breathy gasp. Her soulmate was breathtakingly beautiful. Her fingers automatically twitched to reach out and stroke the petals of the black flowers covering Rio’s chest, to chase the vines encasing her body. The witch’s heart skipped a beat when she remembered that in just a few moments, they would be married.

Lilia released Rio, hugging her tightly and kissing her on the cheek, then doing the same to Agatha, before taking her seat. 

You’re stunning, Lady Death. Agatha used their connection as Ralph began the ceremony.

So are you, Harkness. I can’t believe this is finally happening.

Better now than never.

Ralph cleared his throat. “If the brides would please pay attention.” His eyebrow raised at Agatha.

She huffed and turned her attention back to him, several people in the audience snickering.

Once they got to the vows, the part Agatha had been waiting for, Ralph turned to her. “As a surprise to absolutely no one, these two wrote their own vows. Go ahead, Darling.”

Reaching out, she clasped hands with Rio, memorizing the position of every golden fleck in her eyes at that specific moment in time.

“My love,” she breathed out, “I know our relationship hasn’t been exactly a fairytale, despite the whole soulmate aspect.”

Rio smirked, an eyebrow quirked in sarcasm. 

“However, we have learned a lot about ourselves, each other, and the universe. I vow to stand with you for eternity, to face every challenge head-on, to put you and our family first. To work together in all aspects. Lady Death, I was created and molded for you by the Divine Mother herself, and somehow, my love still grows for you every day.” She had drifted into Rio’s space, arms encircling her waist, foreheads resting against each other. “I am completely yours, and always will be.”

Ralph sniffed, clearing his throat. “Any way you two are going to take a few steps apart?”

Agatha shot him a look that would send most men crying, but left Ralph unfazed.

“I take that as a no. Rio, your vows.”

Remaining in Agatha’s arms, Rio leaned back to regain eye contact. “Agatha Harkness, my witch. You know, it’s funny. Once I learned that you were a witch, I started referring to you as ‘my witch’ in my head. It didn’t matter what our status was; it was just a fact. You were always mine, no matter what was happening, and I was always yours. We let a lot of things get in the way of that, and I vow not to keep serious secrets from you. To communicate, to have a good work-life balance with both jobs.” They shared a small laugh.
